<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q: List the major components of a Lead-Acid accumulator and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Anode ‚ÄĒ Spongy Lead (Pb):** Acts as the negative electrode, gets oxidised during discharge. (1 mark)
2. **Cathode ‚ÄĒ Lead Dioxide (PbO‚āā):** Acts as the positive electrode, gets reduced during discharge. (1 mark)
3. **Electrolyte ‚ÄĒ Dilute H‚āāSO‚āĄ (Specific gravity 1.28):** Provides ions for conduction and participates in the cell reaction. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Write the overall cell reaction of a Lead-Acid battery during discharge.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad ; \quad E^{\circ} = 2.05 \;\text{V}$$

- Mark 1: Correct reactants written.
- Mark 1: Correct products written.
- Mark 1: Balanced equation with EMF value.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model ‚ÄĒ Choice 1]`

#### (a) With a neat construction diagram, explain the construction and working of a Lead-Acid storage battery. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**

- The cell consists of a hard rubber or polypropylene container holding the electrolyte and electrodes.
- The **anode** is a grid of lead-antimony alloy filled with **spongy lead (Pb)**.
- The **cathode** is similarly a Pb-Sb grid filled with **lead dioxide (PbO‚āā)**.
- **Microporous separators** made of PVC or rubber prevent physical contact between the plates.
- The **electrolyte** is aqueous H‚āāSO‚āĄ with a specific gravity of about 1.28 when fully charged.
- **Vent caps** are provided at the top for the escape of H‚āā and O‚āā gases formed during overcharging.
- Each cell delivers **2.05 V**; six cells connected in series produce a **12 V** automobile battery.

**Working (3.5 marks):**

During discharge, the cell acts as a galvanic (voltaic) cell.

**Anode (Oxidation):**

$$\text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \longrightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2e^{-}$$

**Cathode (Reduction):**

$$\text{PbO}_2 + 4\text{H}^{+} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Overall:**

$$\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

During charging, the reactions reverse: water and PbSO‚āĄ regenerate Pb, PbO‚āā, and H‚āāSO‚āĄ. The specific gravity rises back to 1.28, indicating full charge.

---

#### (b) Derive the Nernst equation for the Lead-Acid cell and calculate the EMF when [H‚āāSO‚āĄ] = 0.1 mol/L at 298 K. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

For the overall reaction $\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$:

Number of electrons transferred, $n = 2$.

Reaction quotient (pure solids and liquids omitted):

$$Q = \dfrac{1}{[\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4]^2}$$

Applying the Nernst Equation at 298 K:

$$E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$$

$$E_{\text{cell}} = 2.05 - \dfrac{0.0591}{2} \log \left( \dfrac{1}{[\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4]^2} \right)$$

$$E_{\text{cell}} = 2.05 + 0.0591 \log [\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4]$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given [H‚āāSO‚āĄ] = 0.1 mol/L:

$$E_{\text{cell}} = 2.05 + 0.0591 \log(0.1)$$

$$E_{\text{cell}} = 2.05 + 0.0591 \times (-1)$$

$$E_{\text{cell}} = 2.05 - 0.0591$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{cell}} = 1.991 \;\text{V}}$$

This represents the EMF under heavily diluted electrolyte conditions (near full discharge).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model ‚ÄĒ Choice 2]`

#### (a) Explain with reactions the charging and discharging process of a Lead-Acid cell. Mention any two applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Discharging (3 marks):**

During discharge, electrical energy is delivered.

**Anode:** $\text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2e^{-}$ (oxidation)

**Cathode:** $\text{PbO}_2 + 4\text{H}^{+} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (reduction)

**Overall:** $\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$

**Charging (3 marks):**

When connected to an external DC source, the above reaction reverses:

**At Cathode (reduction ‚ÄĒ connected to negative terminal of DC):** $\text{PbSO}_4 + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^{2-}$

**At Anode (oxidation ‚ÄĒ connected to positive terminal of DC):** $\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{PbO}_2 + 4\text{H}^{+} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2e^{-}$

**Overall:** $2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$

**Applications (1 mark):**

1. **Automobile SLI batteries** ‚ÄĒ starting, lighting, ignition.
2. **Standby UPS systems** in data centres (Information Science application).

---

#### (b) Calculate the mass of lead consumed at the anode when a Lead-Acid battery delivers 2 A current for 10 hours. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**

- Current (I) = 2 A
- Time (t) = 10 hours = 10 √ó 3600 = 36,000 s
- Anode reaction: $\text{Pb} \rightarrow \text{Pb}^{2+} + 2e^{-}$, so $n = 2$
- Molar mass of Pb (M) = 207.2 g/mol
- Faraday constant (F) = 96,485 C/mol

**Formula (Faraday's First Law):**

$$w = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step-by-step substitution:**

$$w = \dfrac{207.2 \times 2 \times 36{,}000}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

**Numerator:**

$$207.2 \times 2 \times 36{,}000 = 14{,}918{,}400$$

**Denominator:**

$$2 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970$$

**Final calculation:**

$$w = \dfrac{14{,}918{,}400}{192{,}970}$$

$$\boxed{w \approx 77.30 \;\text{g of Lead consumed}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting to balance charge:** Always balance electrons between half-equations before writing the net cell reaction. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for unbalanced electrons.
> 2. **Misidentifying anode/cathode during charging:** During charging, the electrodes are reversed relative to their discharge roles. Many students write the same equations ‚ÄĒ losing **2 marks**.
> 3. **Wrong sign in Nernst Equation:** The Nernst equation uses $\log Q$ where Q is the **reaction quotient based on products over reactants**. A common error is using $\log(1/Q)$. Mark deduction: **1 mark**.
> 4. **Unit errors in Faraday's Law:** Always convert time to **seconds** before calculation. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Specific gravity confusion:** Fully charged SG = 1.28, fully discharged SG ‚Čą 1.10. Mixing these is a frequent trap in Part A questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 1):**

- ‚ėź The Lead-Acid cell is a **secondary (rechargeable) battery** with $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = 2.05 \;\text{V}$.
- ‚ėź **Anode** = Spongy Pb; **Cathode** = PbO‚āā; **Electrolyte** = H‚āāSO‚āĄ (aq, SG ‚Čą 1.28).
- ‚ėź **Discharge:** $\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (galvanic mode).
- ‚ėź **Charge:** Reverse of discharge, driven by external DC source (electrolytic mode).
- ‚ėź **Same product (PbSO‚āĄ) on both electrodes** ‚ÄĒ the key to reversibility.
- ‚ėź **Nernst Equation:** $E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$ at 298 K.
- ‚ėź For Lead-Acid specifically: $E_{\text{cell}} = 2.05 + 0.0591 \log[\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4]$.
- ‚ėź **Faraday's First Law:** $w = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ ‚ÄĒ mass deposited/dissolved.
- ‚ėź **State of Charge (SOC) indicator:** Specific gravity of electrolyte ‚ÄĒ drops as battery discharges.
- ‚ėź **Applications:** SLI (automobiles), UPS (data centres), telecommunications backup, solar storage.
- ‚ėź Always **convert time to seconds** in numerical problems.
- ‚ėź Always **balance electrons** before combining half-cell reactions.
- ‚ėź **Modern variants:** VRLA (Valve Regulated Lead Acid), AGM (Absorbent Glass Mat), Gel-cell ‚ÄĒ relevant to backup power for IT infrastructure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrochemical Series and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE AND ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electrochemical Series and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrochemical Series (ECS)**, also called the **Activity Series** or **Electromotive Force (EMF) Series**, is a systematic arrangement of chemical elements ‚ÄĒ primarily metals and non-metals ‚ÄĒ in the **order of increasing standard reduction potential (E¬į)** measured against the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** which is arbitrarily assigned a potential of **0.00 V** at **298 K (25 ¬įC)** and **1 atm (1 bar)** pressure with **1 M (or 1 mol/L)** ion concentration.

Mathematically, the position of any element in the series is governed by its **Standard Electrode Potential**:

$$E^{\circ}_{M^{n+}/M} = \text{Potential of the half-cell when } [M^{n+}] = 1\,M,\; T = 298\,K,\; P = 1\,atm$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Energy Ladder of Metals" ūü™ú**
> Imagine metals standing on a ladder. The ones at the **bottom** are the most reactive ‚ÄĒ they *love* to give away electrons (like **Na, K, Mg, Zn**). The ones at the **top** are the "lazy" metals ‚ÄĒ they hold on to their electrons tightly and prefer to *receive* electrons (like **Cu, Ag, Au, Pt**).
> 
> - A metal higher up on the ladder (more negative E¬į) can **kick out** (displace) a metal lower down from its salt solution.
> - A metal lower down (more positive E¬į) **cannot** displace a metal higher up.
> - Think of it like seniority in a company: a senior employee (reactive metal) can take over the project (salt) of a junior (noble metal), but never the other way around.

### 1.3 Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) ‚ÄĒ The Reference Point

The **SHE** is the universal benchmark for all electrode potentials:

$$\text{H}^+(aq, 1M) + e^- \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2}\,\text{H}_2(g, 1\,atm) \quad\quad E^{\circ} = 0.00\,\text{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis):**
> - **Standard Temperature:** **T = 298.15 K** (‚Čą 25 ¬įC)
> - **Standard Pressure:** **P = 1 bar** (or 1 atm ‚Čą **1.01325 √ó 10‚ĀĶ Pa**)
> - **Standard Concentration:** **[Ion] = 1 M (or 1 mol¬∑dm‚ĀĽ¬≥)**
> - **Faraday's Constant:** **F = 96485 C¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ** (‚Čą **96500 C¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ** for calculations)
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** **R = 8.314 J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction of the Electrochemical Series ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Logic

The ECS is built by following a strict, methodical procedure:

1. **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Choose the Reference:** Fix the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) as the universal reference, assigning it a potential of **0.00 V**.
2. **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Measure Half-Cell Potentials:** Each metal is immersed in a 1 M solution of its own ions, paired with SHE, and the EMF is measured using a high-impedance voltmeter (to avoid current draw).
3. **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Sign Convention:**
   - If the metal **loses electrons** to H‚Āļ (i.e., metal is oxidized, H‚Āļ is reduced), the metal has a **negative E¬į** (e.g., Zn, E¬į = ‚ąí0.76 V).
   - If the metal **gains electrons** from H‚āā (i.e., metal ion is reduced, H‚āā is oxidized), the metal has a **positive E¬į** (e.g., Cu, E¬į = +0.34 V).
4. **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Arrangement:** Species are listed in order of **increasing E¬į** (from most negative at the top to most positive at the bottom).
5. **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Unified List:** Both metals and non-metals (e.g., F‚āā, Cl‚āā, O‚āā) are listed since all involve electron transfer.

### 2.2 Key Observations from the ECS

- **Top of the series (very negative E¬į):** Highly reactive metals ‚ÄĒ strong reducing agents (Li, K, Na, Mg, Al, Zn, Fe).
- **Bottom of the series (very positive E¬į):** Noble metals and non-metals ‚ÄĒ strong oxidizing agents (Au, Pt, F‚āā, Cl‚āā).
- **The "Hydrogen Line" (E¬į = 0.00 V):** Divides metals into those that displace H‚āā from acids (above) and those that do not (below).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units / Constants |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$ | Standard cell EMF | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ | Gibbs free energy change | Joules (J) or kJ¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ |
| 3 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -2.303\,RT\log_{10}K_c$ | Equilibrium constant relation | ‚ÄĒ |
| 4 | $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10}K_c$ (at 298 K) | EMF‚ÄďEquilibrium relation | Volts (V) |
| 5 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10}Q$ | **Nernst Equation** (at 298 K) | Volts (V) |
| 6 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | Nernst Equation (general form) | Volts (V) |
| 7 | $\log_{10}K_c = \dfrac{nE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{0.0591}$ | Equilibrium constant from E¬į | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $Q = \dfrac{[\text{Products}]^x}{[\text{Reactants}]^y}$ | Reaction quotient | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Derivation Hint for KTU Board Exams:** Combining equations (2) and (3) gives equation (4). This is a **very frequently tested derivation** (2-mark/7-mark question territory).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Corrosion Engineering:** Predicting which metal will corrode when two dissimilar metals are in contact (galvanic corrosion). The more negative metal (anode) corrodes.
- **Battery Design:** Choosing anode/cathode pairs with large E¬į differences for higher cell voltage (e.g., Li-ion, Zn-air, lead-acid batteries).
- **Electroplating & Electrorefining:** Selecting electrolytes and electrodes for industrial metal purification.
- **Cathodic Protection:** Using sacrificial anodes (e.g., Mg or Zn) to protect pipelines, ship hulls, and underground tanks.
- **Electrolytic Extraction:** Predicting feasibility of extracting metals from ores (e.g., Na from molten NaCl).
- **PCB Manufacturing & Microelectronics:** Electrochemical etching, deposition of copper traces in information technology hardware.
- **Fuel Cells & Hydrogen Economy:** Designing electrodes for H‚āā/O‚āā fuel cells used in electrical engineering applications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Standard Cell Potential and Equilibrium Constant

**Starting Point ‚ÄĒ Gibbs Free Energy and Reversible Work:**

For a reversible electrochemical cell, the maximum electrical work obtainable equals the negative of the change in Gibbs free energy:

$$W_{\text{elec, max}} = -\Delta G^{\circ}$$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Express Electrical Work in Terms of Charge and Potential:**

The electrical work done when *n* moles of electrons are transferred through a potential difference of $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ is given by:

$$W_{\text{elec}} = Q \times E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = n \times F \times E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$$

*Explanation:* Total charge Q = (number of moles of electrons) √ó (charge per mole) = n √ó F, where F is Faraday's constant.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Equate the Two Expressions for Maximum Work:**

$$-\Delta G^{\circ} = nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Substitute into the Standard Thermodynamic Equilibrium Relation:**

The standard Gibbs free energy change is related to the equilibrium constant by:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -2.303\,RT\log_{10}K_c$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Equate the Two Expressions for $\Delta G^{\circ}$:**

$$-nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = -2.303\,RT\log_{10}K_c$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Solve for $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$:**

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10}K_c$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Substitute Standard Values (T = 298 K, R = 8.314 J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ, F = 96500 C¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ):**

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{n \times 96500}\log_{10}K_c$$

After numerical evaluation:

$$\dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96500} = 0.0591\,\text{V}$$

**Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Final Reduced Form (Most Used in KTU Exams):**

$$\boxed{E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10}K_c \quad \text{at } 298\,K}$$

**Step 8 ‚ÄĒ Rearranged to Find $K_c$:**

$$\log_{10}K_c = \dfrac{nE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{0.0591}$$

$$\boxed{K_c = 10^{\,nE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}/0.0591}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Nernst Equation

**Starting Point ‚ÄĒ General Gibbs Free Energy at Non-Standard Conditions:**

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + 2.303\,RT\log_{10}Q$$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Substitute $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$:**

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + 2.303\,RT\log_{10}Q$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Divide Through by $-nF$:**

$$E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10}Q$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ At T = 298 K, substitute the constant 0.0591:**

$$\boxed{E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10}Q \quad \text{at } 298\,K}$$

This is the famous **Nernst Equation** ‚ÄĒ the cornerstone of electrochemical calculations.

---

### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Problem:** Calculate the standard EMF of the cell:
> Zn(s) ‚ą£ Zn¬≤‚Āļ(1M) ‚ą• Cu¬≤‚Āļ(1M) ‚ą£ Cu(s)
> Given: $E^{\circ}_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} = -0.76\,V$ and $E^{\circ}_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34\,V$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify Anode and Cathode:**
- More negative E¬į ‚Üí Anode (oxidation) ‚Üí Zn
- More positive E¬į ‚Üí Cathode (reduction) ‚Üí Cu

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply the Cell EMF Formula:**

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Substitute Values:**

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = (+0.34) - (-0.76)$$

$$\boxed{E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = +1.10\,V}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Calculate $\Delta G^{\circ}$ (n = 2 electrons transferred):**

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = -(2)(96500)(+1.10)$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -212300\,\text{J¬∑mol}^{-1} = -212.3\,\text{kJ¬∑mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Calculate Equilibrium Constant $K_c$:**

$$\log_{10}K_c = \dfrac{nE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{0.0591} = \dfrac{2 \times 1.10}{0.0591} = 37.225$$

$$\boxed{K_c = 10^{37.225} \approx 1.67 \times 10^{37}}$$

This enormous value confirms the reaction is overwhelmingly product-favored at equilibrium.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix ‚ÄĒ Applications of the ECS

| Property of ECS Value | Feasibility of Reaction | Sign of $\Delta G^{\circ}$ | Spontaneity | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} > 0$ (Positive) | Reaction is feasible | $\Delta G^{\circ} < 0$ | **Spontaneous (Galvanic)** | Zn + Cu¬≤‚Āļ ‚Üí Zn¬≤‚Āļ + Cu |
| $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = 0$ | Reaction is at equilibrium | $\Delta G^{\circ} = 0$ | **Equilibrium** | Reversible cell at E = 0 |
| $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} < 0$ (Negative) | Reaction is NOT feasible in given direction | $\Delta G^{\circ} > 0$ | **Non-spontaneous (Electrolytic)** | Cu + Zn¬≤‚Āļ ‚Üí Cu¬≤‚Āļ + Zn |
| $\vert E^{\circ} \vert$ very large | Reaction goes almost to completion | $\vert \Delta G^{\circ} \vert$ very large | **Highly spontaneous** | $2F_2 + 2H_2O \to 4HF + O_2$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Electrochemical Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    externalCircuit --> cathodeBlock["CATHODE\nPositive Electrode\nSite of REDUCTION\nGains Electrons"]
    anodeBlock -->|Ion Migration| saltBridge["SALT BRIDGE\nPorous Partition\nMaintains Electrical Neutrality\nKCl or KNO3 Gel"]
    saltBridge --> cathodeBlock
    cathodeBlock -->|Cathode Reaction| cathodeRedn["M_n_plus + n e_minus --> M\nDeposition of Metal\nor H2 Evolution"]
    anodeBlock -->|Anode Reaction| anodeOxn["M --> M_n_plus + n e_minus\nMetal Dissolution\nor Gas Evolution"]

flowchart TD

    identifyOxRed --> lookupE["Look up E_oxidation and\nE_reduction from ECS Table"]
    lookupE --> calcEMF["Calculate E_cell using\nE_cell = E_cathode - E_anode"]
    calcEMF --> decisionNode{"Is E_cell\nPositive ?"}
    decisionNode -- YES --> spontaneous["SPONTANEOUS REACTION\nDelta G is Negative\nReaction Proceeds Forward\nGalvanic Cell Formed"]
    decisionNode -- NO --> nonSpontaneous["NON SPONTANEOUS\nDelta G is Positive\nNeeds External Power\nElectrolytic Cell Required"]
    spontaneous --> writeNet["Write Net Ionic Equation\nand Balance Electrons"]
    nonSpontaneous --> writeNet
    writeNet --> endNode["END\nConclusion with\nEngineering Application"]

    style nonSpontaneous fill:#f7d4d4,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style endNode fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Define Electrochemical Series. Mention any two of its applications.**

**Model Answer:**

The Electrochemical Series is a sequence in which elements (metals and non-metals) are arranged in the **order of increasing standard reduction potential**, measured against the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** at **298 K**, **1 atm** pressure, and **1 M** ion concentration.

**Two important applications:**

1. **Prediction of Displacement Reactions:** A metal higher in the series (more negative E¬į) displaces a metal lower in the series from its salt solution. Example: Zn displaces Cu from CuSO‚āĄ because E¬į(Zn¬≤‚Āļ/Zn) = ‚ąí0.76 V is more negative than E¬į(Cu¬≤‚Āļ/Cu) = +0.34 V.

2. **Predicting Feasibility of Redox Reactions:** The sign of $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ calculated from the ECS determines whether a reaction is spontaneous (E¬į > 0, $\Delta G¬į$ < 0) or non-spontaneous (E¬į < 0, $\Delta G¬į$ > 0).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for the two applications with brief justification)*

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **What is the significance of the Standard Hydrogen Electrode? Why is its potential fixed at 0.00 V?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** serves as the **universal reference electrode** for measuring the standard electrode potentials of all other elements.

**Significance:**

1. **Universal Reference:** All standard electrode potentials are measured by coupling a half-cell with the SHE, ensuring uniformity in reporting potentials.
2. **Establishes the Zero Point:** By arbitrarily assigning SHE a value of **0.00 V**, a consistent comparative scale is created.
3. **Allows Prediction of Cell EMF:** Knowing the E¬į of any two half-cells allows calculation of the EMF of any galvanic cell.

**Why 0.00 V?**
The zero value is **purely arbitrary** (a convention), adopted by the **International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC)**. Without a reference point, comparing electrode potentials would be impossible. The assignment makes calculations of $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$, $\Delta G^{\circ}$, and $K_c$ straightforward.

*(3 marks: 1 for significance, 1 for explanation of reference role, 1 for IUPAC convention)*

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Sign Convention Errors:** Students frequently mix up reduction and oxidation potentials. **Always use the standard reduction potential from the ECS table and subtract anode from cathode.**
> - **Forgetting the 'n' value:** The number of electrons transferred ($n$) must match the balanced overall equation. A common error is using $n = 1$ when the actual balanced reaction transfers 2 electrons.
> - **Unit Mismatch:** Always express $\Delta G^{\circ}$ in **J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ** when using $F = 96500$ C¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ, or in **kJ¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ** if converting. Mixing units costs 1 mark.
> - **Temperature Condition:** The 0.0591 factor in the Nernst equation is valid **only at 298 K**. If a different temperature is given, use the general form $RT/nF$.
> - **Missing Balanced Equation:** KTU examiners deduct 2 marks if the balanced net ionic equation is not written before calculating E¬į.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern]`

> **(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)**. State the reactions involved.

> **(b)** [7 Marks] Calculate the **standard EMF**, **standard free energy change ($\Delta G^{\circ}$)**, and the **equilibrium constant ($K_c$)** for the cell reaction of the following galvanic cell at 298 K:
> 
> Mg(s) ‚ą£ Mg¬≤‚Āļ(1M) ‚ą• Ag‚Āļ(1M) ‚ą£ Ag(s)
> 
> Given: $E^{\circ}_{\text{Mg}^{2+}/\text{Mg}} = -2.37\,V$ and $E^{\circ}_{\text{Ag}^{+}/\text{Ag}} = +0.80\,V$

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of SHE [7 Marks]**

The Standard Hydrogen Electrode consists of:

1. **A platinum wire** sealed into a glass tube.
2. **A platinum foil** coated with **platinum black** (finely divided Pt) to provide a large surface area and catalyse the reaction.
3. **Pure hydrogen gas** at **1 atm pressure** is bubbled around the platinum foil.
4. The foil is immersed in a **1 M HCl (or H‚āāSO‚āĄ)** solution whose $[\text{H}^+] = 1\,M$.

**Working:** Hydrogen gas is adsorbed on the platinum black surface, establishing an equilibrium between H‚āā gas and H‚Āļ ions. The electrode acts reversibly as either an anode or cathode depending on the other half-cell it is paired with.

**Anode reaction (oxidation, when SHE acts as anode):**

$$\text{H}_2(g, 1\,atm) \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+(aq, 1M) + 2e^-$$

**Cathode reaction (reduction, when SHE acts as cathode):**

$$2\text{H}^+(aq, 1M) + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2(g, 1\,atm)$$

**Net reaction:** $2\text{H}^+ + \text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{H}^+ + \text{H}_2$ (equilibrium, no net change)

The potential of SHE is **defined as 0.00 V at all temperatures** by IUPAC convention.

*(7 marks: 2 for construction, 2 for working, 2 for both electrode reactions, 1 for IUPAC convention)*

**(b) Cell EMF, $\Delta G^{\circ}$, and $K_c$ Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify Anode and Cathode:**

Since $E^{\circ}_{\text{Mg}^{2+}/\text{Mg}} = -2.37\,V$ is **more negative** than $E^{\circ}_{\text{Ag}^{+}/\text{Ag}} = +0.80\,V$:
- **Anode (oxidation):** Mg
- **Cathode (reduction):** Ag

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Write Half-Reactions:**

$$\text{Mg}(s) \rightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+}(aq) + 2e^- \quad \text{(Anode)}$$

$$2\text{Ag}^+(aq) + 2e^- \rightarrow 2\text{Ag}(s) \quad \text{(Cathode)}$$

**Net cell reaction:**

$$\text{Mg}(s) + 2\text{Ag}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+}(aq) + 2\text{Ag}(s)$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Calculate $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$:**

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$$

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = (+0.80) - (-2.37)$$

$$\boxed{E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = +3.17\,V}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Calculate $\Delta G^{\circ}$ (n = 2):**

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = -(2)(96500)(+3.17)$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -611810\,\text{J¬∑mol}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G^{\circ} = -611.81\,\text{kJ¬∑mol}^{-1}}$$

The large negative value confirms the reaction is **highly spontaneous**.

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Calculate $K_c$:**

$$\log_{10}K_c = \dfrac{nE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{0.0591} = \dfrac{2 \times 3.17}{0.0591} = 107.275$$

$$\boxed{K_c = 10^{107.275} \approx 1.88 \times 10^{107}}$$

This enormous $K_c$ shows the reaction goes **virtually to completion**.

*(7 marks: 1 for identification, 1 for net reaction, 1 for E¬įcell, 2 for őĒG¬į, 2 for Kc)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern]`

> **(a)** [7 Marks] State and derive the **Nernst Equation** for a general electrochemical cell reaction:
> $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$
> Show how the equilibrium constant $K_c$ is related to $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$.

> **(b)** [7 Marks] Predict the **feasibility** of the following redox reactions using the Electrochemical Series. Justify your answer with a clear calculation of $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ and $\Delta G^{\circ}$ for each:
> 
> **(i)** $\text{Fe}(s) + \text{Cu}^{2+}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+}(aq) + \text{Cu}(s)$
> 
> **(ii)** $\text{Cu}(s) + \text{Fe}^{2+}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+}(aq) + \text{Fe}(s)$
> 
> Given: $E^{\circ}_{\text{Fe}^{2+}/\text{Fe}} = -0.44\,V$ and $E^{\circ}_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34\,V$

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Nernst Equation Derivation [7 Marks]**

**Statement:** The Nernst equation relates the electrode potential of a half-cell (or EMF of a full cell) to the standard electrode potential, temperature, and concentrations (activities) of the reactants and products.

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1:** For a general reaction, the change in Gibbs free energy at non-standard conditions is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + 2.303\,RT\log_{10}Q$$

**Step 2:** Express $\Delta G$ and $\Delta G^{\circ}$ in terms of cell potential:

$$\Delta G = -nFE \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute these into the equation from Step 1:

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + 2.303\,RT\log_{10}Q$$

**Step 4:** Divide through both sides by $-nF$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10}Q$$

**Step 5:** At **T = 298 K**, substitute numerical values:

$$\dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96500} = 0.0591$$

$$\boxed{E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10}Q}$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Relation Between $K_c$ and $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$:**

At equilibrium, $E = 0$ and $Q = K_c$. Substituting:

$$0 = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10}K_c$$

$$\boxed{E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10}K_c \quad \text{or} \quad \log_{10}K_c = \dfrac{nE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{0.0591}}$$

This shows that a large positive $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ leads to a very large $K_c$ (reaction favours products).

*(7 marks: 1 for statement, 4 for derivation steps, 2 for Kc relation)*

**(b) Feasibility Prediction for the Two Reactions [7 Marks]**

**(i) $\text{Fe}(s) + \text{Cu}^{2+}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+}(aq) + \text{Cu}(s)$**

**Step 1:** Anode = Fe (more negative E¬į), Cathode = Cu (more positive E¬į)

**Step 2:**

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}} = (+0.34) - (-0.44)$$

$$\boxed{E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = +0.78\,V}$$

**Step 3:** $\Delta G^{\circ} = -(2)(96500)(+0.78) = -150540\,\text{J¬∑mol}^{-1} = -150.54\,\text{kJ¬∑mol}^{-1}$

**Step 4:** Since $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} > 0$ and $\Delta G^{\circ} < 0$, the reaction is **SPONTANEOUS** and **feasible**. Iron displaces copper from copper salt solution.

---

**(ii) $\text{Cu}(s) + \text{Fe}^{2+}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+}(aq) + \text{Fe}(s)$**

**Step 1:** Anode = Cu (more positive E¬į in reverse), Cathode = Fe (more negative E¬į in reverse)
*Equivalently:* Cathode = Fe¬≤‚Āļ/Fe = ‚ąí0.44 V, Anode = Cu¬≤‚Āļ/Cu = +0.34 V

**Step 2:**

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = (-0.44) - (+0.34)$$

$$\boxed{E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = -0.78\,V}$$

**Step 3:** $\Delta G^{\circ} = -(2)(96500)(-0.78) = +150540\,\text{J¬∑mol}^{-1} = +150.54\,\text{kJ¬∑mol}^{-1}$

**Step 4:** Since $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} < 0$ and $\Delta G^{\circ} > 0$, the reaction is **NON-SPONTANEOUS** and **NOT feasible** in the forward direction. Copper cannot displace iron from iron salt solution.

> **Engineering Insight:** This pair of reactions demonstrates that the **direction of a redox reaction is governed entirely by the relative position of the metals in the ECS** ‚ÄĒ a fundamental principle applied in selecting sacrificial anodes for cathodic protection.

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks each for E¬įcell, 1 mark each for őĒG¬į, 1 mark each for the feasibility conclusion, 1 mark for engineering insight)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ūüöÄ

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ Electrochemical Series and Applications**

- ‚ö° **Definition:** ECS arranges elements in order of **increasing standard reduction potential** vs. **SHE** at **298 K**, **1 atm**, **1 M**.
- ‚ö° **SHE = 0.00 V** is a **convention (IUPAC)**, not a measured value.
- ‚ö° **Sign Rule:** More negative E¬į = Stronger reducing agent (top of ECS); More positive E¬į = Stronger oxidising agent (bottom of ECS).
- ‚ö° **Cell EMF Formula:** $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$ ‚ÄĒ always use **reduction potentials**.
- ‚ö° **Thermodynamic Link:** $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ ‚ÄĒ negative őĒG¬į means spontaneous reaction.
- ‚ö° **Equilibrium Link:** $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10}K_c$ (at 298 K).
- ‚ö° **Nernst Equation:** $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10}Q$ for non-standard conditions.
- ‚ö° **Faraday's Constant:** **F = 96500 C¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ** (use 96485 for higher precision).
- ‚ö° **Feasibility Rule:** $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} > 0$ ‚Üí Feasible & Spontaneous; $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} < 0$ ‚Üí Not Feasible.
- ‚ö° **Displacement Rule:** A metal **higher** in the series (more negative E¬į) displaces metals **lower** in the series from solution.
- ‚ö° **Key Engineering Applications to Memorise:** Galvanic corrosion prediction, battery design, cathodic protection, electroplating, electrolytic extraction of metals, and fuel cell design.
- ‚ö° **Common Pitfall:** Always confirm the value of **n** (electrons transferred) from the **balanced** net ionic equation, not from the unbalanced reaction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE AND ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Glass Electrode and pH Measurement, Conductivity Measurement using Digital Conductivity Meter, Li-ion Battery and H2-O2 Fuel Cell (Acid Electrolyte Only) Construction and Working, Corrosion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ‚ÄĒ Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overviews

## 1.1 Glass Electrode and pH Measurement

### Formal KTU Definition
A **Glass Electrode** is a type of ion-selective electrode (ISE) used to measure the **pH (potential of Hydrogen)** of an aqueous solution. It consists of a thin, sensitive glass membrane (typically composed of SiO‚āā, Na‚āāO, and CaO/Li‚āāO) that develops a potential proportional to the hydrogen ion activity of the solution it is dipped into, as governed by the **Nernst Equation**.

The pH of a solution is mathematically defined as:

$$pH = -\log_{10} [H^{+}]$$

where $[H^{+}]$ is the hydrogen ion concentration (more precisely, activity) in mol/L.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a thin, transparent "smart window" that can sense how sour (acidic) or bitter (alkaline) a liquid is. When this glass touches a solution, hydrogen ions from the solution "stick" to one side of the glass, creating a tiny electrical pressure difference. By measuring this pressure (potential) using a sensitive voltmeter, we can determine exactly how acidic the solution is. The glass electrode is essentially this "smart window" for acidity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The glass electrode is the **most commonly used** pH sensor in laboratories and industries because of its ability to give accurate readings across a wide pH range (**0 to 14**) without being affected by oxidizing/reducing agents or turbidity.

### Key Physical Constants
- Standard electrode potential of SHE: **E¬į = 0.000 V** at **298 K (25¬įC)**
- Nernst slope factor: **0.0592 V per pH unit** at 25¬įC
- Faraday's constant: **F = 96,485 C/mol**
- Universal gas constant: **R = 8.314 J/(mol¬∑K)**

---

## 1.2 Conductivity Measurement using Digital Conductivity Meter

### Formal KTU Definition
A **Digital Conductivity Meter** is a precision electronic instrument used to measure the **electrical conductivity** of an aqueous solution. Conductivity is a quantitative measure of a solution's ability to conduct electricity, arising from the presence of dissolved ionic species (electrolytes). The SI unit of conductivity is **Siemens per meter (S/m)**.

Electrical Conductivity (őļ or ŌÉ) is defined as:

$$\kappa = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{L}{A \cdot R}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = resistivity (ő©¬∑m)
- $L$ = distance between electrodes (m)
- $A$ = area of electrodes (m¬≤)
- $R$ = measured resistance (ő©)

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a solution like a road, and ions as vehicles. The more vehicles (ions) there are, and the faster they move, the smoother the "traffic flow" (electricity). A digital conductivity meter measures how well this "ionic traffic" flows through the solution, just like a traffic counter on a highway.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept:** Pure water has very low conductivity (~0.055 ¬ĶS/cm at 25¬įC), while seawater has high conductivity (~50,000 ¬ĶS/cm). The conductivity meter detects the total concentration of all dissolved ions.

### Key Metrics
- **Cell Constant** = $L/A$ (in cm‚ĀĽ¬Ļ), typically **1.0 cm‚ĀĽ¬Ļ** for standard cells
- **Molar conductivity** $\Lambda_m = \kappa / c$ (S¬∑m¬≤/mol)
- **Equivalent conductivity** $\Lambda_{eq} = \kappa / N$ (S¬∑m¬≤/equiv)

---

## 1.3 Lithium-Ion (Li-ion) Battery

### Formal KTU Definition
A **Lithium-Ion Battery** is a **rechargeable (secondary) electrochemical cell** in which lithium ions shuttle between the **cathode** (positive electrode) and **anode** (negative electrode) through a non-aqueous electrolyte. During discharge, lithium ions move from the anode (typically graphite) to the cathode (typically a lithium metal oxide such as LiCoO‚āā), while electrons flow through the external circuit producing electrical energy.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a rechargeable "rocking chair" where lithium ions act as the "passengers." When you use the battery (discharge), the lithium ions rock from the graphite side (anode) to the cobalt oxide side (cathode). When you charge it, an external power source pushes the ions back to the graphite side, ready for the next use. The "rocking" is the electrical energy you use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Li-ion batteries have revolutionized portable electronics and electric vehicles because of their **high energy density (~150‚Äď250 Wh/kg)**, low self-discharge, and no memory effect.

---

## 1.4 Hydrogen-Oxygen Fuel Cell (Acid Electrolyte)

### Formal KTU Definition
A **Hydrogen-Oxygen Fuel Cell** is a **galvanic cell** that converts the chemical energy of hydrogen and oxygen directly into electrical energy, with water as the byproduct. In the **acid electrolyte** version (KTU-mandated), the electrolyte is an acidic solution (typically KOH-free; here **H‚āāSO‚āĄ**), and the overall reaction is:

$$2H_{2(g)} + O_{2(g)} \rightarrow 2H_{2}O_{(l)}$$

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a fuel cell as a "controlled bonfire" ‚ÄĒ instead of burning hydrogen and oxygen together in an uncontrolled manner (producing only heat), we separate them into two chambers with an electrolyte between them. This forces the electrons to take a "detour" through an external wire, and along the way, they light up our bulbs and run our motors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Electrolyte:** For the KTU 2024 acid-electrolyte version, the typical schematic uses a **proton-exchange membrane (PEM)** or acidic solution, where H‚Āļ ions are the charge carriers through the electrolyte.

---

## 1.5 Corrosion

### Formal KTU Definition
**Corrosion** is the **destructive and unintentional oxidation of a metal** by its surrounding environment (typically moisture, oxygen, acids, or salts). It is essentially an **electrochemical process** in which the metal acts as the anode and undergoes oxidation, while another site (often the same metal's surface) acts as the cathode where reduction occurs.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine iron rusting as "the metal getting sick" ‚ÄĒ it reacts with oxygen and water (like a virus) and slowly turns into iron oxide (rust). Just as we vaccinate ourselves against disease, we can "vaccinate" metals using coatings, sacrificial anodes, or alloying to prevent corrosion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** Corrosion causes **economic losses** worth trillions of dollars globally every year. It weakens bridges, pipelines, ship hulls, and electronic components, making its prevention a top priority in **electrical and information science engineering** (especially for circuit reliability and signal integrity).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Corrosion is **not just rusting**. It applies to **all metals** (aluminum, copper, zinc, etc.) in various forms. Only iron's specific oxide is called "rust" (Fe‚āāO‚āÉ¬∑nH‚āāO).

# SECTION 2 ‚ÄĒ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Glass Electrode ‚ÄĒ Deep Theoretical Analysis

### Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Membrane Hydration Layer Formation:** When the thin glass membrane (soda-lime or lithium glass) is soaked in water, a thin hydrated gel layer (~10‚ĀĽ‚Āī mm thick) forms on both sides of the membrane. This layer is essential for H‚Āļ sensitivity.

2. **Ion Exchange Process:** Hydrogen ions (H‚Āļ) from the external solution exchange with sodium ions (Na‚Āļ) in the glass surface:
   $$H^{+}_{(solution)} + Na^{+}_{(glass)} \rightleftharpoons H^{+}_{(glass)} + Na^{+}_{(solution)}$$

3. **Phase Boundary Potential Generation:** This selective exchange of H‚Āļ creates a **phase boundary potential** at both the inner and outer surfaces of the glass membrane.

4. **Net Membrane Potential:** The difference in potentials across the membrane depends on the H‚Āļ activity of the external solution:

$$E_{membrane} = E_{outer} - E_{inner} = 0.0592 \times (pH_{inner} - pH_{outer})$$

5. **Complete Cell Setup:** A typical glass electrode cell is:

   $$\text{Reference Electrode} \vert \text{External Solution} \vert \text{Glass Membrane} \vert \text{Internal Solution (pH=7)} \vert \text{Internal Reference (Ag/AgCl)}$$

6. **Measurement by Nernst Equation:** The EMF of the cell is related to pH by:

$$EMF = E_{cell} = K + 0.0592 \times pH \quad \text{(at 25¬įC)}$$

where $K$ is a constant (asymmetry potential) determined during calibration.

### Construction Details
- **Glass Tube Body:** Lead glass or epoxy
- **Sensing Membrane:** Bulb-shaped, made of special glass (e.g., Corning 015)
- **Internal Solution:** 0.1 M HCl (sometimes saturated KCl with Ag/AgCl)
- **Internal Reference Electrode:** Ag/AgCl wire
- **External Reference Electrode:** Calomel (SCE) or Ag/AgCl ‚ÄĒ required to complete the circuit

### Why Use Glass Electrode?
- **High accuracy** (¬Ī0.01 pH units)
- Works in **strong acids and strong bases**
- **Unaffected** by oxidizing/reducing agents
- Suitable for **biological/medical** applications
- **Disadvantage:** Fragile, slow response, requires frequent calibration

---

## 2.2 Digital Conductivity Meter ‚ÄĒ Deep Theoretical Analysis

### Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **AC Voltage Application:** Unlike DC, an **alternating current (AC)** of known frequency (~1 kHz to 10 kHz) is passed through the solution between two inert electrodes (usually platinum-plated).

2. **Why AC?** DC current causes **polarization** (electrolysis) at electrodes, leading to errors. AC avoids this by continuously reversing the direction of current flow.

3. **Resistance Measurement:** The conductivity cell acts as a resistor (R). The instrument measures the voltage drop and uses Ohm's law to determine R.

4. **Cell Constant Application:** The measured resistance is converted to conductivity using:
$$\kappa = \frac{\text{Cell Constant}}{R_{measured}}$$

5. **Temperature Compensation:** Since conductivity depends strongly on temperature (‚Čą2% per ¬įC), a **thermistor** is built into the probe. The instrument automatically compensates to a reference temperature (usually 25¬įC).

6. **Digital Display:** The conductivity is displayed in ¬ĶS/cm or mS/cm directly.

### Key Components of a Digital Conductivity Meter
| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| Conductivity Cell | Two Pt electrodes with known cell constant |
| Oscillator | Generates AC signal (~1 kHz) |
| Wheatstone Bridge | Measures resistance precisely |
| Amplifier | Boosts weak signal |
| Rectifier | Converts AC to DC for digital processing |
| Microcontroller | Calculates őļ, applies temperature correction |
| Digital Display | Shows reading in ¬ĶS/cm or mS/cm |

### Why is Cell Constant Important?
The **cell constant (K)** depends on the **geometry** of the electrodes:
$$K = \frac{L}{A}$$

For a commercial cell, K is determined by calibration with a **standard KCl solution** of known conductivity.

---

## 2.3 Li-ion Battery ‚ÄĒ Deep Theoretical Analysis

### Cell Components
| Component | Material | Function |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| Cathode (+ve) | LiCoO‚āā / LiFePO‚āĄ / LiMn‚āāO‚āĄ | Source of Li‚Āļ during discharge |
| Anode (‚ąíve) | Graphite (C) intercalated with Li) | Stores Li‚Āļ during charge |
| Electrolyte | LiPF‚āÜ in EC + DMC (organic) | Conducts Li‚Āļ ions |
| Separator | Polyethylene/Polypropylene | Prevents short circuit |
| Current Collectors | Al (cathode), Cu (anode) | Conduct electrons externally |

### Working ‚ÄĒ Discharge Process
- **Anode (oxidation):** $Li_xC \rightarrow xLi^{+} + xe^{-} + C$
- **Cathode (reduction):** $Li_{1-x}CoO_2 + xLi^{+} + xe^{-} \rightarrow LiCoO_2$
- **Net Reaction:** $Li_xC + Li_{1-x}CoO_2 \rightarrow C + LiCoO_2$

### Working ‚ÄĒ Charge Process
External power source drives Li‚Āļ back from cathode to anode.

### Why is Li-ion Special?
- **Intercalation Chemistry:** Li‚Āļ ions insert/de-insert into layered structures without major volume change
- **High Voltage:** ~3.7 V (3√ó that of NiCd/NiMH)
- **No Memory Effect:** Can be recharged at any state of discharge
- **Low Self-Discharge:** ~1‚Äď2% per month

---

## 2.4 H‚āā-O‚āā Fuel Cell (Acid Electrolyte) ‚ÄĒ Deep Theoretical Analysis

### Construction Details
- **Anode Chamber:** Porous carbon electrode with Pt catalyst; H‚āā gas is bubbled in
- **Cathode Chamber:** Porous carbon electrode with Pt catalyst; O‚āā gas is bubbled in
- **Electrolyte:** Acidic solution (H‚āāSO‚āĄ) ‚ÄĒ KTU mandates acid electrolyte only
- **Outer Circuit:** Connects anode and cathode through an external load (bulb, motor)

### Working ‚ÄĒ Electrode Reactions
- **Anode (oxidation):** $H_{2(g)} \rightarrow 2H^{+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-}$
- **Cathode (reduction):** $\frac{1}{2}O_{2(g)} + 2H^{+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_{2}O_{(l)}$
- **Net Reaction:** $H_{2(g)} + \frac{1}{2}O_{2(g)} \rightarrow H_{2}O_{(l)} \quad E¬į = +1.23 \text{ V}$

### Why is Efficiency High?
Unlike combustion, fuel cells are **not limited by the Carnot cycle**. The theoretical efficiency is:
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = 83\%$$
(Compared to ~40% for internal combustion engines)

---

## 2.5 Corrosion ‚ÄĒ Deep Theoretical Analysis

### Electrochemical Mechanism
Corrosion occurs by forming a **galvanic cell** on the metal surface:

- **Anodic Reaction (Oxidation):** $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^{-}$
  - Example: $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^{-}$

- **Cathodic Reaction (Reduction):** Depends on environment
  - In **acidic medium:** $2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_{2(g)}$
  - In **neutral/alkaline (oxygen-rich):** $O_{2} + 2H_{2}O + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4OH^{-}$

### Types of Corrosion
1. **Uniform/General Corrosion:** Even attack over the entire surface
2. **Pitting Corrosion:** Localized holes (e.g., stainless steel in chloride media)
3. **Crevice Corrosion:** Occurs in shielded areas (gaskets, joints)
4. **Galvanic Corrosion:** When two dissimilar metals are in contact
5. **Intergranular Corrosion:** Attack at grain boundaries
6. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC):** Combined action of tensile stress and corrosive environment

### Factors Affecting Corrosion
- **Nature of metal:** Position in galvanic series
- **Nature of environment:** pH, humidity, dissolved O‚āā, salts
- **Temperature:** Higher T ‚Üí faster corrosion
- **Presence of inhibitors/passivators**

# SECTION 3 ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Nernst Equation (Foundation of Glass Electrode)

### Step 1: Gibbs Free Energy Change
For a general redox reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$, the Gibbs free energy change is related to the reaction quotient Q:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G¬į + RT \ln Q$$

### Step 2: Relate to Electrical Work
The maximum electrical work obtainable is:
$$W_{max} = -nFE_{cell} \quad \text{and} \quad W¬į_{max} = -nFE¬į_{cell}$$

### Step 3: Substitute into Free Energy Equation
$$-nFE_{cell} = -nFE¬į_{cell} + RT \ln Q$$

### Step 4: Rearrange for E_cell
$$nFE_{cell} = nFE¬į_{cell} - RT \ln Q$$

$$E_{cell} = E¬į_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

### Step 5: Convert to Base-10 Logarithm
Using $\ln Q = 2.303 \log Q$:

$$E_{cell} = E¬į_{cell} - \frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{nF} \log Q$$

### Step 6: Substitute Constants at 25¬įC
At T = 298 K, R = 8.314 J/(mol¬∑K), F = 96,485 C/mol:
$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96485} = 0.0592$$

### Step 7: Final Form
$$E_{cell} = E¬į_{cell} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log Q$$

> This is the **Nernst Equation** ‚ÄĒ the foundation of all electrochemistry including glass electrodes, fuel cells, and corrosion potential calculations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Glass Electrode EMF vs. pH Relationship

### Step 1: EMF of Complete Cell
For a glass electrode cell:
$$EMF = E_{ref} - E_{glass}$$

### Step 2: Glass Electrode Potential
The glass electrode develops a potential given by:
$$E_{glass} = E¬į_{glass} + \frac{0.0592}{1} \log [H^{+}]_{outer}$$

### Step 3: Apply Definition of pH
Since $pH = -\log[H^{+}]$, we have $\log[H^{+}] = -pH$:

$$E_{glass} = E¬į_{glass} - 0.0592 \cdot pH_{outer}$$

### Step 4: Combine Equations
$$EMF = E_{ref} - (E¬į_{glass} - 0.0592 \cdot pH_{outer})$$

$$EMF = (E_{ref} - E¬į_{glass}) + 0.0592 \cdot pH_{outer}$$

### Step 5: Final Compact Form
$$\boxed{EMF = K + 0.0592 \cdot pH}$$

where $K = E_{ref} - E¬į_{glass}$ is the **asymmetry constant** determined by calibration using **buffer solutions of known pH** (typically pH 4, 7, and 10).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Conductivity from Cell Resistance

### Step 1: Resistance of a Conductor
$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

where:
- $R$ = resistance (ő©)
- $\rho$ = resistivity (ő©¬∑m)
- $L$ = length between electrodes (m)
- $A$ = area of electrodes (m¬≤)

### Step 2: Definition of Conductivity
$$\kappa = \frac{1}{\rho}$$

### Step 3: Substitute into Resistance Formula
$$R = \frac{1}{\kappa} \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

### Step 4: Solve for Conductivity
$$\kappa = \frac{L}{A \cdot R} = \frac{K_{cell}}{R}$$

where $K_{cell} = L/A$ is the **cell constant** (with units of cm‚ĀĽ¬Ļ or m‚ĀĽ¬Ļ).

### Step 5: Determination of Cell Constant
Calibrate using a standard KCl solution (e.g., 0.1 M KCl has $\kappa = 1.288$ S/m at 25¬įC):
$$K_{cell} = \kappa_{standard} \times R_{measured}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Thermodynamic Efficiency of H‚āā-O‚āā Fuel Cell

### Step 1: Gibbs Free Energy of Reaction
For the reaction: $H_{2(g)} + \frac{1}{2}O_{2(g)} \rightarrow H_{2}O_{(l)}$

At standard conditions:
- $\Delta G¬į = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H¬į = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (enthalpy change)

### Step 2: Electrical Work Available
The maximum electrical work per mole of H‚āā is:
$$W_{elec} = -\Delta G¬į = 237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

### Step 3: Total Chemical Energy Released
The total energy released (as heat in combustion) is:
$$Q_{combustion} = -\Delta H¬į = 285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

### Step 4: Thermodynamic Efficiency
$$\eta = \frac{W_{elec}}{Q_{combustion}} = \frac{237.2}{285.8}$$

### Step 5: Calculate
$$\eta = 0.830 = 83.0\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:** This 83% is the **maximum theoretical efficiency**. Real fuel cells achieve 40‚Äď60% due to polarization losses (activation, ohmic, concentration).

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Standard EMF of H‚āā-O‚āā Cell (Nernst)

### Step 1: Cell Notation
$$Pt \vert H_{2(g, 1 atm)} \vert H^{+}_{(aq, 1 M)} \vert O_{2(g, 1 atm)} \vert Pt$$

### Step 2: Half-Reactions
- Anode: $H_{2} \rightarrow 2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \quad E¬į = 0.00 \text{ V}$
- Cathode: $O_{2} + 4H^{+} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 2H_{2}O \quad E¬į = +1.23 \text{ V}$

### Step 3: Calculate Standard Cell EMF
$$E¬į_{cell} = E¬į_{cathode} - E¬į_{anode} = 1.23 - 0.00 = +1.23 \text{ V}$$

### Step 4: Verify Using Gibbs Energy
$$E¬į = \frac{-\Delta G¬į}{nF} = \frac{237200 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96485 \text{ C/mol}}$$

$$E¬į = \frac{237200}{192970} = 1.229 \text{ V} \approx 1.23 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## PART A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define pH. Mention the composition of glass membrane used in a glass electrode. `[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**pH Definition (2 marks):** pH is the negative logarithm (base 10) of hydrogen ion concentration (or activity) in a solution. It was introduced by S√łren Peder Lauritz S√łrensen in 1909.

$$pH = -\log_{10} [H^{+}]$$

A pH < 7 indicates acidic, pH = 7 indicates neutral, and pH > 7 indicates alkaline (basic) solution.

**Glass Membrane Composition (1 mark):** The glass membrane used in a glass electrode is a special ion-sensitive glass typically composed of:
- SiO‚āā (~72%) ‚ÄĒ Glass former
- Na‚āāO (~22%) ‚ÄĒ Source of Na‚Āļ for ion exchange
- CaO or Li‚āāO (~6%) ‚ÄĒ Modifier for selective H‚Āļ sensitivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip:** Mention the role of Na‚āāO in ion exchange (Na‚Āļ ‚ÜĒ H‚Āļ). Examiners reward students who show understanding of the mechanism, not just composition.

---

### Question 2: What is electrical conductivity? Mention the SI unit. `[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Electrical conductivity ($\kappa$) of a solution is a measure of its ability to conduct electric current, owing to the presence of dissolved ions. It is defined as the reciprocal of resistivity.

$$\kappa = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{L}{R \cdot A}$$

where $L$ is the distance between electrodes, $A$ is the area of electrodes, and $R$ is the resistance of the solution.

**SI Unit (1 mark):** The SI unit of conductivity is **Siemens per meter (S/m)**. In practice, ¬ĶS/cm is often used for dilute solutions (pure water ~0.055 ¬ĶS/cm) and mS/cm for concentrated solutions (seawater ~50 mS/cm).

---

## PART B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)
