## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between sacrificial anode cathodic protection and impressed current cathodic protection.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Sacrificial Anode CP | Impressed Current CP |
|---|---|---|
| Power source | None (uses galvanic potential) | External DC rectifier |
| Anode material | Active metal (Zn, Mg, Al) | Inert (graphite, Ti/MMO) |
| Current magnitude | Limited (few amperes) | Adjustable (high) |
| Cost | Low initial, periodic anode replacement | High initial, low maintenance |
| Best suited for | Small structures, low-resistivity electrolytes | Large structures, high-resistivity soil |

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Mention any three examples of organic corrosion inhibitors and state their mechanism of action.*

**Model Answer:** Three organic inhibitors and their mechanism:
1. **Amines (e.g., octadecylamine)** ‚ÄĒ form a hydrophobic film on the metal surface via adsorption of the polar nitrogen group, blocking water access.
2. **Imidazolines** ‚ÄĒ adsorb onto metal via the imidazoline ring (N atoms act as electron donors) creating a protective monolayer.
3. **Benzotriazole (BTA)** ‚ÄĒ forms a stable Cu‚ÄďBTA complex on copper surfaces, preventing oxidation; widely used in cooling systems.

---

### PART B ‚ÄĒ Full Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A ‚ÄĒ Cathodic Protection Design Problem
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** *(a)* A buried steel pipeline of length **5 km** and diameter **30 cm** is to be protected by the **sacrificial anode method** using zinc anodes. The soil resistivity is low (seawater-equivalent). Given:
- Required protective current density: $i_{prot} = 10\,mA/m^2$
- Each zinc anode mass: $m_{anode} = 8\,kg$
- $M_{Zn} = 65.38\,g/mol$, $n = 2$
- Anode utilization: $\eta = 0.85$

Calculate the **number of anodes** required for a **20-year** service life. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of **impressed current cathodic protection**. List **two advantages** over sacrificial anode CP. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Part (a):**

Step 1: Calculate the total external surface area of the pipeline:
$$A = \pi \times D \times L$$

$$A = 3.1416 \times 0.30 \times 5000 = 4712.4 \, m^2$$

Step 2: Total protective current required:
$$I_{total} = i_{prot} \times A = 10 \times 10^{-3} \times 4712.4 = 47.124 \, A$$

Step 3: Total charge required over 20 years:
$$t = 20 \times 365 \times 24 \times 3600 = 6.307 \times 10^{8} \, \text{seconds}$$

$$Q_{total} = I_{total} \times t = 47.124 \times 6.307 \times 10^{8} = 2.972 \times 10^{10} \, C$$

Step 4: Mass of zinc theoretically required (Faraday's Law):
$$m_{Zn} = \frac{Q_{total} \times M_{Zn}}{n \times F} = \frac{2.972 \times 10^{10} \times 65.38}{2 \times 96500}$$

Numerator: $2.972 \times 10^{10} \times 65.38 = 1.943 \times 10^{12}$
Denominator: $2 \times 96500 = 193000$

$$m_{Zn} = \frac{1.943 \times 10^{12}}{1.93 \times 10^{5}} = 1.007 \times 10^{7} \, g = 10070 \, kg$$

Step 5: Apply anode utilization factor:
$$m_{actual} = \frac{m_{Zn}}{\eta} = \frac{10070}{0.85} = 11847 \, kg$$

Step 6: Number of anodes:
$$N = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{anode}} = \frac{11847}{8} = 1480.9$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 1481 \, \text{zinc anodes}}$$

**Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Part (b):**

In **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)**, an external DC power source (rectifier) is used to drive current from an **inert anode** (graphite, silicon iron, or Ti/MMO) through the electrolyte into the protected metal structure, forcing it to become the cathode.

**Working Principle:**
1. The AC mains is stepped down and rectified to DC.
2. The **positive terminal** of the rectifier is connected to the **inert anode** placed in the soil/electrolyte.
3. The **negative terminal** is connected to the **structure to be protected** (e.g., buried pipeline).
4. Direct current flows from rectifier ‚Üí inert anode ‚Üí electrolyte ‚Üí pipeline ‚Üí back to rectifier.
5. The pipeline is held at a potential of approximately **‚ąí0.85 V vs Cu/CuSO‚āĄ reference electrode**, ensuring complete cathodic polarization.

**Two Advantages over Sacrificial CP:**
1. **Higher current output** ‚ÄĒ can protect very large structures (pipelines, jetties) where sacrificial anodes would be impractical.
2. **Adjustable voltage/current** ‚ÄĒ protection level can be tuned to environmental changes (soil resistivity shifts, temperature variations).
3. *(Bonus third advantage)* Longer anode life since inert anodes don't dissolve.
4. *(Bonus fourth advantage)* Suitable for high-resistivity soils where galvanic anodes fail.

---

#### QUESTION B ‚ÄĒ Corrosion Rate Calculation + Anodic Protection
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** *(a)* A mild steel coupon of dimensions **5 cm √ó 5 cm √ó 0.5 cm** was exposed to a **3% NaCl solution** for **48 hours**. The mass loss observed was **0.485 g**. Calculate:
- (i) Corrosion rate in **mm/yr**
- (ii) Corrosion current density in **¬ĶA/cm¬≤**
*(7 marks)*

Given: $D_{Fe} = 7.87\,g/cm^3$, $M_{Fe} = 55.85\,g/mol$, $n = 2$, $F = 96500\,C/mol$.

*(b)* With the help of a suitable **anodic polarization curve**, explain the principle of **anodic protection**. Mention **two industrial applications**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Part (a):**

Step 1: Determine the exposed area. The coupon is immersed, so we use total surface area (all six faces):
$$A = 2 \times (5 \times 5) + 4 \times (5 \times 0.5) = 50 + 10 = 60 \, cm^2$$

Step 2: Convert mass loss to mg:
$$W = 0.485 \, g = 485 \, mg$$

Step 3: Apply the corrosion rate formula:
$$CR = \frac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times t}$$

$$CR = \frac{87.6 \times 485}{7.87 \times 60 \times 48}$$

Numerator: $87.6 \times 485 = 42486$
Denominator: $7.87 \times 60 \times 48 = 22665.6$

$$\boxed{CR = \frac{42486}{22665.6} = 1.875 \, mm/yr}$$

Step 4: Convert CR to corrosion current density using:
$$i_{corr} = \frac{CR \times n \times F \times D}{M \times 10^4}$$

where CR is in mm/yr, D in g/cm¬≥, and the $10^4$ factor handles unit conversion.

$$i_{corr} = \frac{1.875 \times 2 \times 96500 \times 7.87}{55.85 \times 10^4}$$

Numerator: $1.875 \times 2 \times 96500 \times 7.87 = 2.847 \times 10^{6}$
Denominator: $55.85 \times 10^4 = 5.585 \times 10^{5}$

$$i_{corr} = \frac{2.847 \times 10^{6}}{5.585 \times 10^{5}} = 5.10 \, \mu A/cm^2$$

$$\boxed{i_{corr} \approx 5.10 \, \mu A/cm^2}$$

**Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Part (b):**

**Anodic Polarization Curve ‚ÄĒ Key Regions:**

A typical anodic polarization curve for a passivating metal (e.g., stainless steel) shows three distinct regions as potential increases:

1. **Active Region** (low potential): Metal dissolves actively, current density rises sharply with potential. Corrosion proceeds uninhibited.

2. **Passive Region** (intermediate potential): The current density drops dramatically (by 3‚Äď6 orders of magnitude) due to formation of a thin, adherent, protective oxide film (e.g., Cr‚āāO‚āÉ on stainless steel).

3. **Transpassive Region** (high potential): The passive film breaks down, often accompanied by oxygen evolution. Current rises again.

**Principle of Anodic Protection:**
By means of a **potentiostat**, the metal's potential is held at a value **within the passive region**. This:
- Causes initial rapid formation of the protective oxide film.
- Maintains the film thereafter with negligible corrosion current.
- The required anodic current (after initial passivation) is extremely small.

**Two Industrial Applications:**
1. **Sulfuric acid storage tanks** ‚ÄĒ carbon steel tanks holding 93‚Äď98% H‚āāSO‚āĄ are protected anodically, dramatically reducing iron contamination of acid and extending tank life.
2. **Ammonium nitrate production vessels** ‚ÄĒ passivation of stainless steel reactors handling hot aggressive solutions.
3. *(Bonus)* Phosphoric acid concentrators and aluminum alloy storage tanks in chemical industry.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap:** Always double-check the units expected. If the question states "mm/yr", do NOT provide mpy without conversion. Examiners deduct 1‚Äď2 marks for ambiguity.
>
> 2. **Sign Convention Trap:** In galvanic series, more negative = more anodic = more active. Many students write it in reverse and lose 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Confusing CP and AP:** Cathodic Protection = apply external current to make metal **cathode**. Anodic Protection = apply controlled current to make metal **anode** in its passive range. Reversing these is a fatal error worth 3‚Äď4 marks.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Utilization Factor:** In anode life problems, omitting $\eta$ (typically 0.85 for Zn) loses 1 mark even if the calculation is otherwise correct.
>
> 5. **Galvanizing vs. Tinning confusion:** Both are coatings on steel, but Zn (galvanizing) is *sacrificial* and protects even when scratched, whereas Sn (tinning) is *cathodic* and accelerates corrosion when scratched. Many students swap these in exams.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Corrosion is electrochemical** ‚ÄĒ always identify the anode, cathode, and electrolyte.
- **Three prevention strategies:** break any one of anode, cathode, or electrolyte.
- **Cathodic Protection (CP):** Make the metal the cathode. Two subtypes ‚ÄĒ *sacrificial* (Zn, Mg, Al) and *impressed current* (rectifier + inert anode).
- **Anodic Protection (AP):** Only for **passivating metals**; uses a potentiostat; shifts potential into passive zone.
- **Coatings:**
  - **Anodic (sacrificial):** Zn on Fe ‚Üí protects even if scratched.
  - **Cathodic (barrier):** Sn on Fe ‚Üí scratches accelerate corrosion.
  - **Organic:** Paints, epoxies.
  - **Inorganic:** Phosphating, chromating, anodizing.
- **Inhibitors:** Anodic (chromates, nitrites), cathodic (polyphosphates, Zn¬≤‚Āļ), mixed (amines), vapor-phase (VCI).
- **Design:** Avoid crevices, galvanic couples, and stagnant zones.
- **Key Constants:** $F = 96500\,C/mol$; $E^0_{Fe} = -0.44\,V$; $E^0_{Zn} = -0.76\,V$; $E^0_{Cu} = +0.34\,V$.
- **Corrosion Rate Formulas:**
  - $CR = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ in mm/yr (SI)
  - $CR = \dfrac{534 \times W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ in mpy (Imperial)
- **Conversions:** 1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr.
- **Industrial Relevance:** Mandatory in pipelines, ships, ICs, transformers, server cooling.
- **Examiner hot buttons:** Sign convention, utilization factor, anode vs. cathode in CP/AP, scratched coating behavior.
- **Always pair theory with diagram** in 14-mark answers ‚ÄĒ visual representation earns 1‚Äď2 easy marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ūüĒč Module 1 ‚ÄĒ Cathodic Protection

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### ūüďė Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Cathodic Protection (CP)** is an electrochemical corrosion-control technique used to mitigate the corrosion of a metal surface by making it the **cathode** of an electrochemical cell. This is achieved either by coupling the protected metal with a more electrochemically active (sacrificial) **anode**, or by driving an external **direct current (DC)** from an inert anode through an electrolyte onto the metal structure, thereby suppressing its anodic (oxidative) dissolution.

In the standard KTU 2024 Scheme context (GXCYT122), cathodic protection is studied as a practical application of the **electrochemical series**, **Nernst equation**, and **Faraday's laws of electrolysis** to real-world metallic structures (buried pipelines, ship hulls, offshore platforms, storage tanks, and reinforced concrete).

### ūüß† Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of cathodic protection as a **"bodyguard sacrifice"** for your metal structure. Imagine an expensive iron sculpture (your "protected asset"). Instead of letting rust eat it away, you tie a cheap, more reactive metal block (a **sacrificial anode** like zinc or magnesium) to it and bury them both in damp soil. When the corrosive environment attacks, the **cheap block corrodes first** ‚ÄĒ it "takes the bullet" ‚ÄĒ while the valuable iron sculpture remains untouched. The block is gradually consumed and eventually replaced, but the structure is preserved. This is the **galvanic (sacrificial anode)** method.

The **impressed current** variant is like installing a small **water pump** that continuously pushes electrons into the iron structure, forcing it to behave like a cathode. An inert electrode (e.g., graphite or titanium) acts as the pump's other end, and the power comes from an external **DC rectifier** (like a battery charger).

### ūüĒĎ Core Physical Constants & Standards

| Quantity | Symbol | Value / Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Faraday constant | $F$ | $\mathbf{96500 \; C \, mol^{-1}}$ |
| Standard reference temperature | $T$ | $\mathbf{298.15 \; K}$ (25 ¬įC) |
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | $\mathbf{8.314 \; J \, mol^{-1} \, K^{-1}}$ |
| Typical CP current density (steel in soil) | $j$ | $\mathbf{10 \text{ to} 30 \; mA \, m^{-2}}$ |
| Protective potential (steel vs Cu/CuSO‚āĄ) | $E_{prot}$ | $\mathbf{-0.85 \; V}$ or more negative |
| Protective potential (steel vs Ag/AgCl) | $E_{prot}$ | $\mathbf{-0.78 \; V}$ or more negative |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must remember the **two principal CP methods** (Galvanic and Impressed Current), the role of the **sacrificial anode**, the application of **Faraday's law** for mass calculations, and the use of **reference electrodes** (Cu/CuSO‚āĄ, Ag/AgCl, saturated calomel) for monitoring protective potential.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## ūüß™ Electrochemical Basis of Corrosion & Cathodic Protection

When iron is exposed to a moist, aerated, slightly acidic environment, the following **half-cell reactions** spontaneously occur:

**At the Anode (oxidation ‚ÄĒ metal dissolves):**

$$Fe_{(s)} \rightarrow Fe^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-}$$

**At the Cathode (reduction ‚ÄĒ oxygen is consumed in neutral/alkaline media):**

$$O_{2(g)} + 2H_{2}O_{(l)} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4OH^{-}_{(aq)}$$

The mixed potential of this corrosion cell lies near the corrosion potential $E_{corr}$, where the anodic and cathodic currents are equal. **Cathodic protection works by shifting the metal's potential in the negative direction**, making the anodic current negligibly small.

### ‚öôÔłŹ Method 1: Sacrificial Anode (Galvanic) Method

In this passive method, the structure to be protected is electrically connected to a metal that is **more anodic (more negative reduction potential)** in the galvanic series. Common sacrificial anodes include:

- **Magnesium (Mg)** ‚ÄĒ most active, used in high-resistivity soils
- **Zinc (Zn)** ‚ÄĒ most common, used in seawater and low-resistivity soils
- **Aluminium alloys (Al-Zn-In)** ‚ÄĒ lightweight, used offshore

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Principle:** The sacrificial anode has a more negative standard reduction potential than iron ($E¬į_{Fe^{2+}/Fe} = -0.44\;V$ vs SHE). For example, $E¬į_{Zn^{2+}/Zn} = -0.76\;V$, so zinc preferentially oxidises and "protects" iron.

**Reactions on Zinc anode:**

$$Zn_{(s)} \rightarrow Zn^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-}$$

**Reactions on Iron (now forced to be cathode):**

$$O_{2(g)} + 2H_{2}O_{(l)} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4OH^{-}_{(aq)}$$

### ‚öôÔłŹ Method 2: Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)

In this active method, an **external DC power source** (a rectifier) is used. The **negative terminal** of the rectifier is connected to the structure, and the **positive terminal** is connected to an **inert anode** (graphite, high-silicon cast iron, platinised titanium, or mixed-metal-oxide coated titanium).

**Reactions at the inert anode (e.g., in chloride media):**

$$2Cl^{-}_{(aq)} \rightarrow Cl_{2(g)} + 2e^{-}$$

$$2H_{2}O_{(l)} \rightarrow O_{2(g)} + 4H^{+}_{(aq)} + 4e^{-}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why inert anode?** Because the current is driven *externally*, the anode does not need to corrode. It only needs to be electrically conductive and chemically stable.

### ūüďä KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$ | Gibbs free energy & cell potential relation | $\Delta G$ in J/mol |
| 2 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | Nernst equation (general) | $E$ in Volts |
| 3 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10} Q$ | Nernst equation at 25 ¬įC (KTU favourite) | $E$ in Volts |
| 4 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's First Law (mass consumed/deposited) | $m$ in grams |
| 5 | $Q = I \cdot t = n \cdot z \cdot F$ | Faraday's Second Law (charge relation) | $Q$ in Coulombs |
| 6 | $j = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | Current density | $j$ in A/m¬≤ |
| 7 | $\tau = \dfrac{m_{anode} \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M}$ | Theoretical lifetime of sacrificial anode | $\tau$ in seconds |
| 8 | $\eta_{cathode} = E_{prot} - E_{corr}$ | Polarisation shift required for protection | $\eta$ in Volts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for CP:**
> - Iron in soil: $E_{prot} \leq -0.85\;V$ vs Cu/CuSO‚āĄ reference electrode
> - Iron in concrete: $E_{prot} \leq -0.77\;V$ vs Cu/CuSO‚āĄ
> - Avoid over-protection: potentials more negative than $-1.20\;V$ vs Cu/CuSO‚āĄ can cause **hydrogen embrittlement** in high-strength steels

### ūüŹóÔłŹ Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Buried oil & gas pipelines** (cross-country, hundreds of km long)
- **Marine ship hulls** and offshore oil platforms
- **Reinforced concrete bridges** (rebar protection)
- **Water storage tanks** (interior surfaces)
- **Underground cable sheaths** and earthing systems

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## ūüßģ Derivation A: Mass of Sacrificial Anode Required (Faraday's Law Application)

### ūüďč Problem Context
A buried steel pipeline requires a total cathodic protection current of **$I = 5.0 \; A$** to be uniformly applied for **$t = 5$ years**. If zinc anodes ($M_{Zn} = 65.4 \; g/mol$, $n = 2$) are used, calculate:
1. The total mass of zinc consumed.
2. The number of moles of zinc dissolved.
3. Verify the equivalent charge passed.

### ūüĒĘ Step 1: Convert time to seconds

$$t = 5 \; \text{years} \times 365 \; \text{days/year} \times 24 \; \text{h/day} \times 3600 \; \text{s/h}$$

$$t = 5 \times 365 \times 24 \times 3600$$

$$t = 1.577 \times 10^{8} \; \text{s}$$

### ūüĒĘ Step 2: Apply Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

Substitute the values:

$$m = \frac{(5.0 \; A) \times (1.577 \times 10^{8} \; s) \times (65.4 \; g/mol)}{(2) \times (96500 \; C/mol)}$$

Compute the numerator step-by-step:

$$I \cdot t = 5.0 \times 1.577 \times 10^{8} = 7.885 \times 10^{8} \; C$$

$$I \cdot t \cdot M = 7.885 \times 10^{8} \times 65.4 = 5.157 \times 10^{10} \; g \cdot C/mol$$

Compute the denominator:

$$n \cdot F = 2 \times 96500 = 1.930 \times 10^{5} \; C/mol$$

Final mass calculation:

$$m = \frac{5.157 \times 10^{10}}{1.930 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 2.672 \times 10^{5} \; g = 267.2 \; kg \; \text{of zinc}}$$

### ūüĒĘ Step 3: Number of moles dissolved

$$n_{moles} = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{2.672 \times 10^{5} \; g}{65.4 \; g/mol}$$

$$\boxed{n_{moles} \approx 4086 \; \text{mol}}$$

### ūüĒĘ Step 4: Verification by Faraday's Second Law

$$Q = n_{moles} \cdot n \cdot F$$

$$Q = 4086 \times 2 \times 96500$$

$$Q = 7.886 \times 10^{8} \; C \;\;\checkmark$$

This matches $I \cdot t = 7.885 \times 10^{8} \; C$ (within rounding error). **Calculation verified.**

---

## ūüßģ Derivation B: Nernst Equation Application for Protective Potential

### ūüďč Problem Context
A steel structure is buried in soil containing dissolved oxygen at partial pressure $P_{O_2} = 0.21 \; atm$ and $pH = 7.0$. Using the standard reduction potential of the oxygen reduction reaction $E^{\circ} = +0.40 \; V$ (vs SHE), calculate the reversible cathode potential at 25 ¬įC.

### ūüĒĘ Step 1: Write the half-reaction

$$O_{2(g)} + 2H_{2}O_{(l)} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4OH^{-}_{(aq)}$$

### ūüĒĘ Step 2: Write the reaction quotient

$$Q = \frac{[OH^{-}]^{4}}{P_{O_2}}$$

$$[OH^{-}] = \frac{K_w}{[H^{+}]} = \frac{10^{-14}}{10^{-7}} = 10^{-7} \; M$$

$$Q = \frac{(10^{-7})^{4}}{0.21} = \frac{10^{-28}}{0.21} = 4.76 \times 10^{-28}$$

### ūüĒĘ Step 3: Apply the Nernst equation (n = 4)

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10} Q$$

$$E = 0.40 - \frac{0.0591}{4} \log_{10}(4.76 \times 10^{-28})$$

$$\log_{10}(4.76 \times 10^{-28}) = \log_{10}(4.76) + (-28) = 0.678 - 28 = -27.322$$

$$E = 0.40 - \frac{0.0591}{4} \times (-27.322)$$

$$E = 0.40 - (0.01478 \times -27.322)$$

$$E = 0.40 + 0.4037$$

$$\boxed{E_{cathode} \approx +0.804 \; V \; \text{vs SHE}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For the iron structure to be fully cathodic, its potential must be pushed **below this value** by an amount equal to the corrosion potential of iron ($\approx -0.44\;V$ vs SHE). The ICCP system must supply enough current to drive the surface potential into the protective range.

---

## ūüßģ Derivation C: Lifetime of a Sacrificial Anode

### ūüďč Problem Context
A magnesium anode weighing **$m_0 = 8.0 \; kg$** is connected to a cathodic protection circuit drawing **$I = 0.8 \; A$**. Magnesium has $M = 24.3 \; g/mol$ and $n = 2$. Find the anode lifetime in years.

### ūüĒĘ Step 1: Use the lifetime formula derived from Faraday's law

$$\tau = \frac{m_{anode} \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M}$$

### ūüĒĘ Step 2: Substitute (convert $m_0$ to grams: $m_0 = 8000 \; g$)

$$\tau = \frac{8000 \times 2 \times 96500}{0.8 \times 24.3}$$

Numerator: $8000 \times 2 \times 96500 = 1.544 \times 10^{9}$

Denominator: $0.8 \times 24.3 = 19.44$

$$\tau = \frac{1.544 \times 10^{9}}{19.44} = 7.942 \times 10^{7} \; s$$

### ūüĒĘ Step 3: Convert to years

$$\tau = \frac{7.942 \times 10^{7}}{3.154 \times 10^{7}} \; \text{years}$$

$$\boxed{\tau \approx 2.52 \; \text{years}}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## ūüóļÔłŹ Diagram 1: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Cathodic Protection System

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the functional flow of both CP methods and the role of monitoring electrodes. Since the topic involves physical electrodes and buried structures, a **functional block architecture** is the safest, most accurate representation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Protected Metal Structure] --> B1{Protection Method}
    B1 -->|Galvanic| C1[Sacrificial Anode - Mg, Zn, Al]
    B1 -->|Impressed Current| C2[Inert Anode - Graphite or Ti]
    C1 --> D1[Electrolyte - Soil, Seawater, Concrete Pore Water]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Reference Electrode - Cu/CuSO4 or Ag/AgCl]
    E1 --> F1[Potential Monitoring and Control Unit]
    F1 -->|Galvanic| G1[No External Power Required]
    F1 -->|Impressed Current| G2[DC Rectifier Power Source]
    G2 --> A1
    C2 --> G2
    A1 --> H1[Protective Current Flows Through Electrolyte]
    H1 --> C1
    H1 --> C2
```

## ūüóļÔłŹ Diagram 2: Comparison Flow Between Sacrificial and Impressed Current Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SAC["SACRIFICIAL ANODE METHOD"]
        direction TB
        s1[More Active Metal - Anode] --> s2[Corrodes First - Zn, Mg, Al]
        s2 --> s3[Structure Becomes Cathode]
        s3 --> s4[No External Power Source Needed]
        s4 --> s5[Best for Low Resistivity Environments]
    end

    subgraph ICCP["IMPRESSED CURRENT METHOD"]
        direction TB
        i1[External DC Rectifier] --> i2[Inert Auxiliary Anode]
        i2 --> i3[Forced Electron Flow to Structure]
        i3 --> i4[Structure Polarised Cathodically]
        i4 --> i5[Best for Large Structures and High Resistivity]
    end

    SAC --> COMMON[Both Methods Achieve Protective Potential at Structure Surface]
    ICCP --> COMMON
```

## ūüóļÔłŹ Diagram 3: Electrochemical Reaction Flow at Protected Surface

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Anode Reaction - Oxidation of Sacrificial Metal] --> P2[Electrons Released Travel Through Wire]
    P2 --> P3[Reach Protected Steel Surface]
    P3 --> P4[Steel Forced to Act as Cathode]
    P4 --> P5[Cathode Reaction - Oxygen Reduction]
    P5 --> P6[Hydroxide Ions Form Protective Passive Layer]
    P6 --> P7[Iron Does Not Dissolve - No Corrosion]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagrams:** In all three flowcharts, follow the **electron flow direction** ‚ÄĒ it always originates from the **anode** (where oxidation occurs) and terminates at the **protected structure** (cathode). This is the fundamental directionality of every cathodic protection system.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## ūüďĚ Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ‚Ěď Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define cathodic protection. Mention its two principal methods.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Cathodic protection** is an electrochemical technique employed to control the corrosion of a metal surface by converting it from an anode to a cathode in an electrochemical cell. This is achieved by supplying electrons to the metal, thereby suppressing its anodic dissolution.

**The two principal methods are:**

1. **Sacrificial Anode (Galvanic) Method:** A more electrochemically active metal (such as zinc, magnesium, or aluminium) is electrically connected to the structure to be protected. The sacrificial anode corrodes preferentially, supplying electrons to the protected structure.

2. **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP):** An external DC power source (rectifier) drives current from an inert auxiliary anode through the electrolyte onto the protected structure, forcing it to behave as a cathode.

---

### ‚Ěď Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Write a short note on reference electrodes used in cathodic protection.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference electrodes** are used to measure the potential of the protected structure against a stable, reproducible standard. They allow the operator to verify that the structure has reached the required protective potential.

**Common reference electrodes include:**

- **Copper/Copper Sulphate (Cu/CuSO‚āĄ) electrode:** Most widely used for buried pipelines and marine applications. Standard half-cell: $\text{Cu} \mid \text{CuSO}_{4(\text{sat})} \mid \text{Cu}^{2+}$.
- **Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl) electrode:** Used in seawater applications.
- **Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE):** Used in laboratory measurements.

**Typical protective criterion for steel:** $-0.85 \; V$ vs Cu/CuSO‚āĄ (more negative = better protection, within safe limits).

---

## ūüďĚ Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ‚ÄĒ Module Internal Choice

### ‚Ěď Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **sacrificial anode method** of cathodic protection with a neat diagram. Discuss the criteria for selecting a sacrificial anode and list any two commonly used sacrificial anode materials with their key properties.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A buried pipeline requires a protection current of $1.5 \; A$. A zinc anode ($M = 65.4 \; g/mol$, $n = 2$) of mass $5.0 \; kg$ is used. Calculate **(i)** the lifetime of the anode in days, and **(ii)** the total charge passed during its lifetime.

---

#### ‚úÖ Model Solution to Question A(a)

**Definition:** The sacrificial anode method is a passive cathodic protection technique in which a metal more electrochemically active than the protected structure is electrically bonded to it. The active metal preferentially corrodes (acts as the anode), while the protected structure acts as the cathode.

**Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4, Diagram 3 for the reaction flow.)*

**Selection criteria for sacrificial anode:**

- Must have a **more negative standard reduction potential** than the metal to be protected.
- Must have a **high electrochemical capacity** (ampere-hours per kg).
- Should be **uniformly consumed** (no localised pitting).
- Must be **economically viable** and readily available.
- Should have **good electrical conductivity** and be easy to install.

**Two commonly used sacrificial anode materials:**

| Anode | $E^{\circ}$ (V vs SHE) | Typical Use | Capacity (Ah/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Magnesium (Mg) | $-2.37$ | High-resistivity soils | $\approx 1230$ |
| Zinc (Zn) | $-0.76$ | Seawater, low-resistivity soil | $\approx 820$ |
| Aluminium (Al) | $-1.66$ | Offshore marine | $\approx 2700$ |

---

#### ‚úÖ Model Solution to Question A(b)

**Given:**
- $I = 1.5 \; A$
- $M_{Zn} = 65.4 \; g/mol$
- $n = 2$
- $m = 5.0 \; kg = 5000 \; g$

**Part (i): Lifetime of the anode in days**

Use the lifetime formula:

$$\tau = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M}$$

$$\tau = \frac{5000 \times 2 \times 96500}{1.5 \times 65.4}$$

$$\tau = \frac{9.65 \times 10^{8}}{98.1}$$

$$\tau = 9.837 \times 10^{6} \; s$$

Convert to days:

$$\tau = \frac{9.837 \times 10^{6}}{86400} \; \text{days}$$

$$\boxed{\tau \approx 113.85 \; \text{days}}$$

**Part (ii): Total charge passed**

$$Q = I \cdot t$$

$$Q = 1.5 \; A \times 9.837 \times 10^{6} \; s$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 1.476 \times 10^{7} \; C = 14.76 \; MC}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ‚ÄĒ Common Mistakes:**
> - Forgetting to convert the anode mass from **kg to g** (mismatch in units = full marks lost).
> - Confusing the **lifetime formula** with the mass formula. The numerator of the lifetime formula contains $m \cdot n \cdot F$ (not $I \cdot t$).
> - Not converting seconds to days. Always check the **final unit** requested.

---

### ‚Ěď Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, describe the **impressed current cathodic protection (ICCP) system**. Compare its advantages and disadvantages with the sacrificial anode method.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A steel structure buried in aerated soil ($pH = 7$, $P_{O_2} = 0.21 \; atm$) is to be cathodically protected. Given $E^{\circ}_{Fe^{2+}/Fe} = -0.44 \; V$ and $E^{\circ}_{O_2/OH^{-}} = +0.40 \; V$ (both vs SHE), calculate:
- **(i)** The theoretical corrosion cell EMF.
- **(ii)** The reversible cathode potential at 25 ¬įC using the Nernst equation.
- **(iii)** The minimum protective current density required if the structure's surface area is $200 \; m^2$ and the design current is $0.6 \; A$.

---

#### ‚úÖ Model Solution to Question B(a)

**Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP):**

In the ICCP method, an **external DC rectifier** is used to drive a current between an **inert auxiliary anode** (placed in the electrolyte) and the **protected structure** (made the cathode). The rectifier's negative terminal is connected to the structure, and the positive terminal to the inert anode.

**Block diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4, Diagram 1 for the system block architecture.)*

**Anode reactions (on inert anode in chloride/neutral media):**

$$2Cl^{-} \rightarrow Cl_{2(g)} + 2e^{-}$$

$$2H_{2}O \rightarrow O_{2(g)} + 4H^{+} + 4e^{-}$$

**Cathode reaction (on protected structure):**

$$O_{2(g)} + 2H_{2}O_{(l)} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4OH^{-}_{(aq)}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Sacrificial Anode | Impressed Current |
|---|---|---|
| External power | Not required | DC rectifier required |
| Anode material | Active (Zn, Mg, Al) | Inert (graphite, Ti, Si-Fe) |
| Current capacity | Limited | Adjustable, very high |
| Initial cost | Low | High |
| Operating cost | Periodic anode replacement | Power consumption |
| Best suited for | Small structures, low resistivity | Large structures, high resistivity |
| Voltage range | $\approx 0.2$‚Äď$0.5 \; V$ | Up to $50 \; V$ |
| Monitoring | Simple | Continuous with rectifier |

---

#### ‚úÖ Model Solution to Question B(b)

**Part (i): Theoretical corrosion cell EMF**

$$E_{cell} = E_{cathode}^{\circ} - E_{anode}^{\circ} = E_{O_2/OH^{-}}^{\circ} - E_{Fe^{2+}/Fe}^{\circ}$$

$$E_{cell} = (+0.40) - (-0.44)$$

$$\boxed{E_{cell} = +0.84 \; V}$$

**Part (ii): Reversible cathode potential using Nernst equation**

Reaction: $O_{2(g)} + 2H_{2}O_{(l)} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4OH^{-}_{(aq)}$

At $pH = 7$: $[OH^{-}] = 10^{-7} \; M$, $P_{O_2} = 0.21 \; atm$, $n = 4$

$$Q = \frac{[OH^{-}]^{4}}{P_{O_2}} = \frac{(10^{-7})^{4}}{0.21} = 4.76 \times 10^{-28}$$

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10} Q$$

$$E = 0.40 - \frac{0.0591}{4} \log_{10}(4.76 \times 10^{-28})$$

$$E = 0.40 - 0.01478 \times (-27.32)$$

$$E = 0.40 + 0.4038$$

$$\boxed{E_{cathode} = +0.804 \; V \; \text{vs SHE}}$$

**Part (iii): Minimum protective current density**

$$j = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{0.6 \; A}{200 \; m^2}$$

$$\boxed{j = 3.0 \times 10^{-3} \; A/m^2 = 3.0 \; mA/m^2}$$

This falls within the **typical CP design range of 10‚Äď30 mA/m¬≤** for steel in soil, confirming the design is reasonable.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ‚ÄĒ Common Mistakes:**
> - Writing the Nernst equation without specifying the **number of electrons transferred** ($n = 4$ for O‚āā reduction). Wrong $n$ = full marks lost.
> - Forgetting to include the **logarithm sign** (must be $\log_{10}$, not natural log).
> - Confusing **corrosion current density** with **protective current density** ‚ÄĒ they are not the same quantity.
> - Not checking if the protective potential criterion ($-0.85 \; V$ vs Cu/CuSO‚āĄ) is satisfied in the final answer.

---

## ūüďĆ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ Cathodic Protection**

- ‚úÖ **Definition:** CP is a corrosion control technique that converts the protected metal into a cathode, suppressing anodic dissolution.
- ‚úÖ **Two methods:** (1) **Sacrificial Anode** ‚ÄĒ passive, uses active metals like Zn, Mg, Al. (2) **Impressed Current** ‚ÄĒ active, uses DC rectifier with inert anodes.
- ‚úÖ **Selection of sacrificial anode:** Must be **more anodic** (more negative $E^{\circ}$) than the protected metal, with high Faradaic capacity (Ah/kg).
- ‚úÖ **Faraday's First Law:** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ ‚ÄĒ use this to calculate anode mass consumed or required.
- ‚úÖ **Faraday's Second Law:** $Q = I \cdot t = n \cdot z \cdot F$ ‚ÄĒ used to relate moles of electrons to charge.
- ‚úÖ **Anode lifetime:** $\tau = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M}$ ‚ÄĒ convert seconds to days/years as required.
- ‚úÖ **Nernst equation at 25 ¬įC:** $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10} Q$ ‚ÄĒ the **0.0591** constant is a KTU favourite.
- ‚úÖ **Protective potential for steel:** $\leq -0.85 \; V$ vs Cu/CuSO‚āĄ reference electrode.
- ‚úÖ **Over-protection warning:** Potentials more negative than $-1.20 \; V$ vs Cu/CuSO‚āĄ can cause **hydrogen embrittlement**.
- ‚úÖ **Reference electrodes:** Cu/CuSO‚āĄ (soil/pipelines), Ag/AgCl (seawater), SCE (lab use).
- ‚úÖ **ICCP advantages:** Adjustable current, suitable for large structures, high-resistivity environments.
- ‚úÖ **Sacrificial anode advantages:** No external power, simple, low maintenance, ideal for remote/offshore sites.
- ‚úÖ **Common anodic reaction (steel):** $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^{-}$
- ‚úÖ **Common cathodic reaction (neutral/alkaline):** $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4OH^{-}$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sacrificial Anodic Protection and Impressed Current Cathodic Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE AND ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sacrificial Anodic Protection and Impressed Current Cathodic Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Corrosion? The Electrochemical Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Corrosion is the **destructive and irreversible** oxidation of metals by an electrochemical reaction with their surrounding environment (electrolyte), which causes the metal to lose electrons, change its oxidation state, and form stable compounds (oxides, hydroxides, sulfides, or carbonates).

From a thermodynamic perspective, corrosion is a **spontaneous process** for most engineering metals because their standard reduction potentials are more positive than the reduction potential of the environment's oxidizing agent. This essentially means nature "wants" these metals to revert to their stable ore states.

Every corrosion reaction in an aqueous/electrolytic medium has **four mandatory components**:

| Component | Description | Role |
|-----------|-------------|------|
| **Anode** | Site of metal dissolution (oxidation) | $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$ |
| **Cathode** | Site of reduction (electron consumption) | Oxidizer + $e^- \rightarrow$ Reduced species |
| **Electrolyte** | Ionic medium (moisture, soil, salt water) | Conducts ions between electrodes |
| **Metallic Path** | Direct electrical contact | Conducts electrons between electrodes |

## 1.2 Cathodic Protection ‚ÄĒ The Core Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Cathodic Protection (CP)** is an **electrochemical corrosion-control technique** that suppresses the anodic reaction of a metal by converting the entire metal structure into the **cathode** of an electrochemical cell. By forcing all metal surfaces to receive electrons (rather than lose them), oxidation (rusting) is thermodynamically and kinetically suppressed.

There are **two industrial methods** of implementing Cathodic Protection:
1. **Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP)** ‚ÄĒ also called *Galvanic Anode Cathodic Protection*
2. **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)** ‚ÄĒ also called *Forced Current Cathodic Protection*

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: "The Sacrificial Bodyguard"

Imagine a king (the iron pipeline/structure) walking through a dangerous forest (the corrosive electrolyte). Every night, bandits (corrosive ions like Cl‚ĀĽ, O‚āā) try to attack the king.

- **Sacrificial Anode Method:** Hire a **bodyguard (Zinc/Magnesium)** who is **stronger, louder, and more aggressive** than the king. The bodyguard willingly **draws all attacks onto himself** and gets destroyed instead. When he dies, you simply replace him. The king stays untouched.

- **Impressed Current Method:** Install an **invisible electric force field** powered by a **generator (DC rectifier)** that continuously repels attackers and pushes protective electrons onto the king. The generator is not consumed; only the **inert electrodes** that emit the field slowly degrade. This is the equivalent of having an "indestructible power source" that shields the king.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** 
> - *SACP = "Zinc is willing to die for Iron"* (more active metal acts as sacrificial anode)
> - *ICCP = "Use external power to FORCIBLY protect iron"* (rectifier pushes electrons)

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics

- **Faraday's Constant (F) = 96,485 C/mol** (‚Čą 96,500 C/mol for board calculations)
- **Standard potential of Iron (Fe¬≤‚Āļ/Fe) = ‚ąí0.44 V** (vs SHE)
- **Standard potential of Zinc (Zn¬≤‚Āļ/Zn) = ‚ąí0.76 V** (vs SHE)
- **Standard potential of Magnesium (Mg¬≤‚Āļ/Mg) = ‚ąí2.37 V** (vs SHE)
- **Protection Potential for Iron in soil/water ‚Čą ‚ąí0.85 V (vs Cu/CuSO‚āĄ reference electrode)**
- **Typical CP current density: 10‚Äď30 mA/m¬≤** (soil) ; **100‚Äď500 mA/m¬≤** (seawater)
- **Hydrogen overpotential** on protective surfaces is critical to avoid H‚āā evolution damage.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP)

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle

In SACP, a metal that is **electrochemically more active** (more negative reduction potential) than the metal to be protected is **electrically connected** to the protected structure. This forms a **galvanic cell** in which the sacrificial anode acts as the **anode** and the protected structure acts as the **cathode**.

Because the sacrificial anode is more active, it **preferentially corrodes** (oxidizes), supplying a continuous flow of electrons to the protected metal. This electron flow drives the cathodic reaction (typically oxygen reduction) on the protected surface, suppressing the metal's anodic dissolution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the protected metal becomes immune:**
> When the entire structure is forced to act as a cathode, the **anodic dissolution reaction (Fe ‚Üí Fe¬≤‚Āļ + 2e‚ĀĽ) is thermodynamically forbidden** because the impressed electrons cannot accumulate ‚ÄĒ they are consumed by the cathodic reaction faster than they are generated.

### 2.1.2 Selection of Sacrificial Anode Materials

The anode must satisfy **three engineering criteria**:

1. **More negative electrode potential** than the structure metal (driving force).
2. **High electrochemical capacity** (Ampere-hours per kg) ‚ÄĒ long life.
3. **Uniform corrosion behavior** ‚ÄĒ does not form passive films that would stop current flow.

| Sacrificial Anode | Theoretical Capacity (A¬∑h/kg) | Practical Capacity (A¬∑h/kg) | Efficiency (%) | Driving Voltage (V) | Best Use |
|-------------------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|----------------|---------------------|----------|
| **Magnesium (Mg)** | 2,200 | 1,100‚Äď1,200 | ~50% | **0.70 V** | High-resistivity soils (>1000 ő©¬∑cm) |
| **Zinc (Zn)** | 820 | 780 | ~95% | **0.25 V** | Seawater, low-resistivity soils |
| **Aluminum alloys** | 2,980 | 1,500‚Äď2,500 | ~85% | **0.30 V** | Seawater, offshore structures |

> [!NOTE]
> **Aluminum anodes** cannot be used in alkaline or highly resistive soils because they form a passive Al‚āāO‚āÉ layer. Aluminum anodes are activated with **Indium (In) or Mercury (Hg) additives** to break this passive film.

### 2.1.3 Design Logic of SACP

The design of an SACP system follows these **stepwise engineering logic steps**:

- **Step 1: Determine total surface area (A)** of the structure to be protected (m¬≤).
- **Step 2: Select current density (i)** required for the environment (mA/m¬≤).
- **Step 3: Calculate total current requirement** using $I_{total} = i \times A$.
- **Step 4: Calculate mass of anode** using Faraday's law.
- **Step 5: Determine number of anodes** and their placement geometry.
- **Step 6: Verify design life** matches the planned replacement cycle.

### 2.1.4 Advantages & Limitations of SACP

**Advantages:**
- **No external power source** required (self-driven galvanic action).
- **Simple installation** ‚ÄĒ minimal electrical expertise.
- **Low maintenance** ‚ÄĒ periodic anode replacement only.
- **Safe in explosive environments** (no sparking risk).
- **Excellent for small to medium structures**.

**Limitations:**
- **Limited driving voltage (~0.25 V for Zn)** ‚ÄĒ cannot overcome high soil resistivity.
- **Anode must be periodically replaced** ‚ÄĒ adds long-term cost.
- **Low current output** ‚ÄĒ unsuitable for very large structures.
- **Anode efficiency may drop** if it passivates in the environment.

---

## 2.2 Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle

In ICCP, a **direct current (DC) power source** (typically a transformer-rectifier that converts AC mains to DC) is used to **force protective current** from an **inert (imperishable) auxiliary anode** through the electrolyte to the structure being protected.

The DC source's positive terminal connects to the **inert anode**, and the negative terminal connects to the **protected structure** (cathode). The rectifier is adjusted to maintain the structure's potential at a value **more negative than its equilibrium potential**, ensuring the anodic reaction is suppressed.

### 2.2.2 Components of an ICCP System

| Component | Function | Common Materials |
|-----------|----------|------------------|
| **DC Power Source** | Provides current | Transformer-rectifier (AC‚ÜíDC), solar panels with battery, thermoelectric generators |
| **Auxiliary Anode** | Completes the circuit through electrolyte | Graphite, high-silicon cast iron, platinum-coated titanium, mixed metal oxide (MMO) coated Ti |
| **Reference Electrode** | Monitors structure potential | Cu/CuSO‚āĄ (most common in soil), Ag/AgCl (seawater), Zn (seawater) |
| **Cables** | Carry current | PVC-insulated copper, with proper color coding |
| **Protected Structure** | The cathode | Buried pipe, tank bottom, ship hull, etc. |

### 2.2.3 Selection of Auxiliary Anodes (Inert Anodes)

Unlike sacrificial anodes, ICCP anodes are designed to **withstand anodic dissolution**. They must have:

- **High electrical conductivity**
- **Low consumption rate** (kg per Ampere-year)
- **Mechanical durability** in the electrolyte

| Inert Anode Material | Consumption Rate (kg/A¬∑yr) | Maximum Current Density (A/m¬≤) | Typical Application |
|----------------------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|---------------------|
| **Graphite** | 0.4‚Äď1.0 | 10‚Äď30 | Underground pipelines, fresh water |
| **High-Silicon Cast Iron** | 0.1‚Äď0.5 | 30‚Äď50 | Marine, deep well |
| **Platinum-coated Titanium** | ~0.000008 | 100‚Äď1000 | Marine, high-chloride |
| **Mixed Metal Oxide (MMO) Ti** | ~0.000005 | 100‚Äď600 | Modern preferred choice |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Note:** Platinum is a noble metal, so it does not dissolve anodically. It catalyzes the oxygen evolution reaction ($2H_2O \rightarrow O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^-$) at low overpotentials, making it an excellent inert anode.

### 2.2.4 Design Logic of ICCP

- **Step 1: Survey soil resistivity, structure geometry, coating condition.**
- **Step 2: Select design current density** based on environment and coating type.
- **Step 3: Calculate total current demand** = current density √ó bare area.
- **Step 4: Determine rectifier voltage output** = (Anode-to-cathode resistance √ó current) + back-EMF + cable drops.
- **Step 5: Select anode type, size, and backfill.**
- **Step 6: Specify rectifier capacity** with ~10‚Äď20% safety margin.
- **Step 7: Plan reference electrode locations for monitoring.**

### 2.2.5 Advantages & Limitations of ICCP

**Advantages:**
- **High current output** ‚ÄĒ protects very large structures.
- **Adjustable voltage and current** ‚ÄĒ flexible for varying conditions.
- **Wide range of applications** ‚ÄĒ soil, marine, concrete.
- **Long anode life** ‚ÄĒ no frequent replacement.
- **Lower long-term operating cost** for large installations.

**Limitations:**
- **Requires continuous external power** (operational cost).
- **More complex installation** and maintenance.
- **Risk of overprotection** ‚Üí causes **hydrogen embrittlement** or **coating disbondment**.
- **Stray currents** can damage nearby metallic structures.
- **Cannot be used in hazardous (explosive) areas** unless specially certified.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **mandatory** for any numerical problem on Cathodic Protection in KTU ESE.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis | $m$ = mass dissolved (g) ; $I$ = current (A) ; $t$ = time (s) ; $M$ = molar mass (g/mol) ; $n$ = electrons transferred ; $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol |
| 2 | $I_{total} = i_c \times A$ | Total protection current | $i_c$ = current density (A/m¬≤) ; $A$ = bare surface area (m¬≤) |
| 3 | $R_{anode} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{2L}{r}\right)$ | Anode-to-earth resistance (single vertical anode) | $\rho$ = soil resistivity (ő©¬∑m) ; $L$ = anode length (m) ; $r$ = anode radius (m) |
| 4 | $V_{rectifier} = I \cdot (R_{cable} + R_{anode}) + E_{back}$ | Rectifier output voltage | $R_{cable}$, $R_{anode}$ in ő© ; $E_{back}$ = back-EMF (‚Čą 2 V for soil) |
| 5 | $E_{protection} = E_{Fe} - \eta$ | Protection criterion | $E_{Fe}$ = ‚ąí0.44 V (Fe/Fe¬≤‚Āļ) ; $\eta$ = overpotential |
| 6 | $I_{Mg} = I_{Fe} \cdot \dfrac{n_{Mg} \cdot F \cdot M_{Fe}}{n_{Fe} \cdot F \cdot M_{Mg}}$ | Equivalent mass conversion | For replacing Zn/Mg with Fe equivalents |
| 7 | $E_{corr} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | Driving EMF of galvanic cell | Higher $E_{corr}$ ‚áí stronger protection |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse **mass of anode consumed (theoretical)** with **mass actually consumed**. Always multiply by a **utilization factor (typically 0.85 for Zn, 0.50 for Mg)** to get practical anode mass required.

---

| **Oil & Gas Pipelines** | Short branch lines, river crossings | Cross-country trunk pipelines (>100 km) |
| **Marine (Ships & Offshore)** | Ship hulls, jetties (with Zn bracelets) | FPSOs, offshore platforms, subsea pipelines |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass of Sacrificial Anode Required (Faraday's Law Application)

**Problem Context (KTU Standard):** A buried iron pipeline of surface area **150 m¬≤** is to be protected by **zinc sacrificial anodes** in a soil environment requiring a current density of **20 mA/m¬≤**. Calculate the mass of zinc required for a design life of **5 years**. Assume zinc has n = 2, M = 65.4 g/mol, and anode utilization factor = 0.85.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify given data**

We are given:
- $A = 150 \text{ m}^2$ (surface area of pipeline)
- $i_c = 20 \text{ mA/m}^2 = 0.020 \text{ A/m}^2$ (required current density)
- $t = 5 \text{ years} = 5 \times 365 \times 24 \times 3600 \text{ seconds}$
- $M_{Zn} = 65.4 \text{ g/mol}$
- $n_{Zn} = 2$ (since $Zn \rightarrow Zn^{2+} + 2e^-$)
- $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$
- $\eta_{utilization} = 0.85$ (for zinc)

**Step 2: Convert time to seconds**

$$t = 5 \times 365 \times 24 \times 3600$$

$$t = 5 \times 365 \times 86{,}400$$

$$t = 5 \times 31{,}536{,}000$$

$$t = 157{,}680{,}000 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the total protective current required**

$$I_{total} = i_c \times A$$

$$I_{total} = 0.020 \text{ A/m}^2 \times 150 \text{ m}^2$$

$$I_{total} = 3.0 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4: Calculate total charge (Q) passed in 5 years**

$$Q = I_{total} \times t$$

$$Q = 3.0 \text{ A} \times 157{,}680{,}000 \text{ s}$$

$$Q = 4.7304 \times 10^{8} \text{ C}$$

**Step 5: Apply Faraday's First Law to find theoretical mass**

The anodic reaction is: $Zn \rightarrow Zn^{2+} + 2e^-$

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{Q \times M_{Zn}}{n_{Zn} \times F}$$

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{4.7304 \times 10^{8} \times 65.4}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

**Numerator computation:**

$$4.7304 \times 10^{8} \times 65.4 = 3.0937 \times 10^{10}$$

**Denominator computation:**

$$2 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970$$

**Final division:**

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{3.0937 \times 10^{10}}{192{,}970}$$

$$m_{theoretical} = 160{,}315.6 \text{ g}$$

$$m_{theoretical} = 160.32 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6: Apply the utilization factor**

The utilization factor accounts for the fact that not all of the anode is consumed before it must be replaced (corrosion becomes non-uniform at the end of life).

$$m_{practical} = \frac{m_{theoretical}}{\eta_{utilization}}$$

$$m_{practical} = \frac{160.32 \text{ kg}}{0.85}$$

$$m_{practical} = 188.61 \text{ kg}$$

### Final Result

> **The total mass of zinc sacrificial anode required = 188.6 kg (approximately 189 kg).**

**Engineering Practical Step (Bonus):** If standard 12 kg zinc anodes are used, number of anodes required:

$$N_{anodes} = \frac{188.61}{12} = 15.72 \Rightarrow 16 \text{ anodes (rounded up)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Driving EMF of a SACP Cell

**Problem Context:** An iron structure is protected by a **magnesium sacrificial anode** in soil. Calculate the theoretical driving EMF of the galvanic cell.

**Step 1: Identify the half-reactions**

At the **Magnesium Anode (oxidation):**

$$Mg \rightarrow Mg^{2+} + 2e^- \quad E^0_{anode} = -2.37 \text{ V (vs SHE)}$$

At the **Iron Cathode (oxygen reduction in neutral soil):**

$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^- \quad E^0_{cathode} = +0.40 \text{ V (vs SHE)}$$

**Step 2: Apply the cell EMF formula**

$$E^0_{cell} = E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode}$$

$$E^0_{cell} = (+0.40) - (-2.37)$$

$$E^0_{cell} = +0.40 + 2.37$$

$$\boxed{E^0_{cell} = +2.77 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 3: Engineering Interpretation**

This **2.77 V** is the **theoretical maximum** driving force. In practice, due to:
- IR drop in soil (soil resistance)
- Polarization at electrodes
- Concentration overpotentials

‚Ä¶the **effective driving voltage** is typically reduced to **0.3‚Äď0.7 V** for Mg-based SACP systems. This is why **Mg anodes work even in high-resistivity soils** (driving force is huge), while **Zn anodes (only 0.25 V practical)** fail in such soils.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Current Output of a Single Vertical Anode (ICCP Design)

**Problem Context:** A single high-silicon cast iron anode of length **L = 1.5 m** and radius **r = 0.05 m** is installed vertically in soil of resistivity **ŌĀ = 50 ő©¬∑m**. Calculate the anode-to-soil resistance.

**Step 1: Apply Dwight's formula for a single vertical anode**

$$R_v = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{2L}{r}\right)$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$R_v = \frac{50}{2\pi \times 1.5} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{2 \times 1.5}{0.05}\right)$$

**Step 3: Simplify the argument of the logarithm**

$$\frac{2 \times 1.5}{0.05} = \frac{3.0}{0.05} = 60$$

**Step 4: Calculate the logarithm**

$$\ln(60) = 4.094$$

**Step 5: Calculate the prefactor**

$$\frac{50}{2\pi \times 1.5} = \frac{50}{9.425} = 5.305$$

**Step 6: Final multiplication**

$$R_v = 5.305 \times 4.094$$

$$\boxed{R_v = 21.72 \text{ ő©}}$$

**Step 7: Calculate the current this anode can deliver at 30 V rectifier output**

$$I_{anode} = \frac{V_{rectifier} - E_{back}}{R_v}$$

$$I_{anode} = \frac{30 - 2}{21.72} = \frac{28}{21.72}$$

$$I_{anode} = 1.289 \text{ A}$$

> **Conclusion:** A single anode delivers ~1.29 A. For 10 A protection current, the system needs **8 anodes in parallel**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (SACP vs ICCP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is **exam-critical** for KTU ESE. Memorize it thoroughly.

| Parameter | SACP (Sacrificial Anode CP) | ICCP (Impressed Current CP) |
|-----------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Power source** | Self-driven (galvanic) | External DC source (rectifier) |
| **Driving force** | 0.2‚Äď0.7 V (galvanic difference) | 5‚Äď50 V (rectifier adjustable) |
| **Anode material** | Active metal (Zn, Mg, Al) | Inert material (MMO-Ti, graphite, Pt-Ti) |
| **Anode consumption** | Sacrificial (high) | Very low (inert) |
| **Anode replacement** | Every 1‚Äď10 years | Every 10‚Äď30 years |
| **Current capacity** | Low to medium (mA to few A) | High (up to hundreds of A) |
| **Suitable for** | Small, low-resistivity environments | Large, high-resistivity, complex structures |
| **Soil resistivity tolerance** | Best for ŌĀ < 3000 ő©¬∑cm | Works up to ŌĀ = 50,000 ő©¬∑cm |
| **Cost structure** | Low installation, high recurring | High installation, low recurring |
| **Monitoring** | Simple (voltmeter check) | Continuous (auto-potential control) |
| **Risk of stray currents** | Negligible | Must be managed carefully |
| **Hazardous area use** | Safe (no sparking) | Requires intrinsically safe design |
| **Examples of use** | Ship hulls, hot water tanks, small pipelines | Trans-continental pipelines, offshore platforms, refineries |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Cathodic Protection. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2023]

**Model Answer:**

> **Cathodic Protection (CP)** is an electrochemical method of corrosion control in which the metal structure to be protected is made the **cathode** of an electrochemical cell, thereby suppressing its anodic dissolution reaction. The two industrial methods of implementing CP are:
> 1. **Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP)** ‚ÄĒ using a more active metal (e.g., Zn, Mg) that corrodes preferentially.
> 2. **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)** ‚ÄĒ using an external DC source and inert anodes to force protective current onto the structure.

### Question 2: Why is zinc used as a sacrificial anode for protecting iron structures? [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

Zinc is used as a sacrificial anode for iron because:
1. **Zinc has a more negative standard reduction potential (‚ąí0.76 V)** compared to iron (‚ąí0.44 V). This potential difference creates a **galvanic cell** where zinc becomes the anode and iron becomes the cathode.
2. **Zinc corrodes uniformly** and does not form passive films under normal conditions.
3. **Zinc is inexpensive, readily available, and has high electrochemical efficiency (~95%)**.
4. The galvanic cell reaction delivers a continuous protective current of **electrons** to the iron, suppressing its anodic reaction: $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every module question carries **internal choice**. Both options are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ SACP-Based Design Problem

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] A buried iron pipeline of length **2 km** and diameter **30 cm** is protected by magnesium sacrificial anodes. The soil resistivity is **1500 ő©¬∑cm**, and the protective current density required is **15 mA/m¬≤**. Calculate:

- (i) The total protective current required.
- (ii) The total charge required for a design life of **8 years**.
- (iii) The theoretical mass of magnesium required.

Given: $M_{Mg} = 24.3 \text{ g/mol}$, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$. Take utilization factor = 0.50.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare the **mechanism, materials used, advantages, and limitations** of Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection and Impressed Current Cathodic Protection. Mention at least **three specific applications** of each.

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Given Data:**
- Length $L_p = 2 \text{ km} = 2000 \text{ m}$
- Diameter $d = 30 \text{ cm} = 0.30 \text{ m}$
- Current density $i_c = 15 \text{ mA/m}^2 = 0.015 \text{ A/m}^2$
- Design life $t = 8 \text{ years}$
- $M_{Mg} = 24.3 \text{ g/mol}$, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$, $\eta = 0.50$

**Step 1: Calculate the surface area of the pipeline**

The pipeline is cylindrical, so the lateral surface area is:

$$A = \pi \times d \times L_p$$

$$A = \pi \times 0.30 \times 2000$$

$$A = 1884.96 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate the total protective current**

$$I_{total} = i_c \times A$$

$$I_{total} = 0.015 \times 1884.96$$

$$I_{total} = 28.27 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Convert time to seconds**

$$t = 8 \times 365 \times 24 \times 3600$$

$$t = 8 \times 31{,}536{,}000 = 2.5229 \times 10^8 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: Calculate total charge Q**

$$Q = I \times t = 28.27 \times 2.5229 \times 10^8$$

$$Q = 7.131 \times 10^9 \text{ C}$$

**Step 5: Calculate theoretical mass of Mg using Faraday's law**

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{Q \times M_{Mg}}{n \times F}$$

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{7.131 \times 10^9 \times 24.3}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

**Numerator:** $7.131 \times 10^9 \times 24.3 = 1.733 \times 10^{11}$

**Denominator:** $2 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970$

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{1.733 \times 10^{11}}{192{,}970} = 8.98 \times 10^5 \text{ g} = 898 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6: Apply utilization factor**

$$m_{practical} = \frac{898}{0.50} = 1796 \text{ kg}$$

#### Final Answers for Part (a)

- (i) Total protective current = **28.27 A**
- (ii) Total charge = **7.13 √ó 10‚ĀĻ C**
- (iii) Practical mass of Mg required = **1796 kg** (or theoretical 898 kg)

#### Model Solution to Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Comparison Table

| Parameter | SACP | ICCP |
|-----------|------|------|
| **Mechanism** | Galvanic coupling with more active metal | External DC source forces protective current |
| **Anode Material** | Active (Zn, Mg, Al) | Inert (MMO-Ti, Pt-Ti, graphite) |
| **Power** | None | AC mains + rectifier |
| **Anode Life** | Short (consumed) | Long (inert) |
| **Cost** | Low installation, high recurring | High installation, low recurring |
| **Hazardous Use** | Safe | Requires safety certification |

**SACP Applications:** (1) Ship hulls, (2) Hot water tank interiors, (3) Small water mains, (4) Underground storage tank bottoms in low-resistivity soil.

**ICCP Applications:** (1) Cross-country oil/gas pipelines, (2) Offshore platforms, (3) Reinforced concrete bridges, (4) Refinery tank farms, (5) Power plant condenser cooling water boxes.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ ICCP-Based Design + Mechanism

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat schematic, explain the **working principle of Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)**. Mention the materials used for the auxiliary anode and reference electrode, and the role of the transformer-rectifier.

**(b)** [7 Marks] An ICCP system is to be designed for a marine jetty structure in seawater. The bare steel area to be protected is **800 m¬≤**, and the current density required is **200 mA/m¬≤**. If the rectifier has a back-EMF of **2.5 V** and the total circuit resistance is **0.8 ő©**, calculate the rectifier DC voltage rating required. Also, calculate the total charge passed in **30 days** of operation.

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Working Principle of ICCP:**

In Impressed Current Cathodic Protection, an **external DC source** (transformer-rectifier unit) supplies a regulated direct current that flows from the **auxiliary (inert) anode**, through the **electrolyte (soil or seawater)**, to the **protected structure (cathode)**. The rectifier's **positive terminal** is connected to the auxiliary anode, and the **negative terminal** is connected to the protected structure.

At the auxiliary anode, an oxidation reaction occurs (typically oxygen evolution: $2H_2O \rightarrow O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^-$). The electrons flow through the external circuit (cable) to the structure, where they participate in the cathodic reaction ($O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$). This continuous electron supply keeps the structure at a potential negative enough to suppress the anodic reaction $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$.

**Auxiliary Anode Materials:** MMO-coated titanium, platinum-coated titanium, graphite, high-silicon cast iron. These are **inert** ‚ÄĒ they resist dissolution under anodic polarization.

**Reference Electrode Materials:** Cu/CuSO‚āĄ (for soil applications), Ag/AgCl or saturated calomel (for seawater/laboratory use), pure zinc (in seawater as a portable reference). Used to **measure the protected structure's potential** against a stable reference.

**Role of Transformer-Rectifier:** Converts high-voltage AC mains to low-voltage DC at the required current level, with **adjustable output** to maintain the structure at the correct protection potential. Modern units include **automatic potential controllers** that adjust output based on reference electrode feedback.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- Bare area $A = 800 \text{ m}^2$
- Current density $i_c = 200 \text{ mA/m}^2 = 0.200 \text{ A/m}^2$
- Back-EMF $E_{back} = 2.5 \text{ V}$
- Circuit resistance $R_{total} = 0.8 \text{ ő©}$
- Operating time $t = 30 \text{ days}$

**Step 1: Calculate the total current required**

$$I_{total} = i_c \times A = 0.200 \times 800 = 160 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law for the rectifier output**

$$V_{rectifier} = I \times R_{total} + E_{back}$$

$$V_{rectifier} = (160 \times 0.8) + 2.5$$

$$V_{rectifier} = 128 + 2.5$$

$$\boxed{V_{rectifier} = 130.5 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 3: Add a 20% safety margin for engineering design**

$$V_{design} = 130.5 \times 1.20 = 156.6 \text{ V}$$

A standard 160 V DC rating rectifier would be specified.

**Step 4: Calculate the total charge passed in 30 days**

$$t = 30 \times 24 \times 3600 = 2{,}592{,}000 \text{ s}$$

$$Q = I \times t = 160 \times 2{,}592{,}000$$

$$\boxed{Q = 4.147 \times 10^8 \text{ C} = 414.7 \text{ MC}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This charge corresponds to dissolving **‚Čą 120 kg of iron equivalent** if the structure were unprotected. With ICCP, this iron dissolution is **prevented**, and only a small amount of anode material is slowly consumed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not forget the utilization factor** in SACP mass calculations. Most students lose **1‚Äď2 marks** by quoting theoretical mass instead of practical mass.
> 2. **Do not confuse anode vs cathode in cell EMF.** Always remember: $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$. Reversing this costs full marks.
> 3. **For ICCP, ALWAYS add safety margin** (typically 10‚Äď20%) to rectifier voltage ‚ÄĒ this is a real engineering practice and examiners expect it.
> 4. **Always specify units** ‚ÄĒ writing "160" without "A" or "V" is a common deduction.
> 5. **For ICCP, mention reference electrode purpose** ‚ÄĒ many students forget to explain *why* the potential is monitored.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- [x] **Corrosion is an electrochemical process** with anode, cathode, electrolyte, and metallic path.
- [x] **Cathodic Protection (CP) = making the structure the cathode** of an electrochemical cell.
- [x] **SACP uses active metals (Zn, Mg, Al)** as sacrificial anodes ‚ÄĒ they dissolve preferentially.
- [x] **ICCP uses inert anodes (MMO-Ti, Pt-Ti, graphite)** + external DC source (rectifier).
- [x] **Zn potential = ‚ąí0.76 V**, Fe = ‚ąí0.44 V, Mg = ‚ąí2.37 V (all vs SHE).
- [x] **Driving voltage for SACP:** ~0.25 V (Zn), ~0.7 V (Mg).
- [x] **ICCP can use 5‚Äď50 V** DC, much more flexible.
- [x] **Faraday's Law:** $m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$, where **F = 96,485 C/mol**.
- [x] **Anode utilization factor:** Zn = 0.85, Mg = 0.50, Al = 0.85.
- [x] **Protection criterion for steel:** potential more negative than **‚ąí0.85 V vs Cu/CuSO‚āĄ**.
- [x] **ICCP risks:** hydrogen embrittlement, coating disbondment, stray current damage.
- [x] **SACP advantages:** no power, simple, safe; **limitations:** low current, anode replacement.
- [x] **ICCP advantages:** high current, adjustable, long life; **limitations:** needs power, complex.
- [x] **Typical current densities:** 10‚Äď30 mA/m¬≤ (soil), 100‚Äď500 mA/m¬≤ (seawater).
- [x] **SACP = Galvanic Protection = Passive CP**; **ICCP = Forced Current = Active CP**.
- [x] **Always include safety margin** in rectifier design (10‚Äď20%).
- [x] **Reference electrodes:** Cu/CuSO‚āĄ (soil), Ag/AgCl (seawater), SCE (laboratory), Zn (marine portable).
- [x] **Common applications:** pipelines, ships, tanks, bridges, offshore platforms, water mains.
- [x] **KTU favorite numerical:** Faraday's law applied to SACP mass calculation with utilization factor.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electroplating of Copper
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE AND ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electroplating of Copper

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ‚ÄĒ Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Electroplating of Copper** is a precisely controlled *electrolytic deposition process* in which a thin, adherent, and uniform layer of copper metal is electrodeposited onto the surface of a conducting substrate (the *cathode*) from an aqueous solution of a copper salt, typically **acidified copper sulfate (CuSO‚āĄ¬∑5H‚āāO in dilute H‚āāSO‚āĄ)**, by passing a direct current (DC) through the cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Electroplating is a direct application of **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**. Copper electroplating is the *most industrially relevant* example because copper possesses the second-highest electrical conductivity among metals (after silver) and is far cheaper ‚ÄĒ making it indispensable to the **electrical and information science industries** (PCB fabrication, semiconductor packaging, busbars, RF shields).

## 1.2 The Electrochemical Cell Components

| Component | Role in Copper Electroplating |
|---|---|
| **Anode** | A pure copper metal plate (‚Č• 99.9% Cu) that *dissolves* into the solution to replenish Cu¬≤‚Āļ ions. |
| **Cathode** | The *object to be plated* (e.g., a steel screw, an etched PCB, a plastic piece pre-coated with conductive graphite). |
| **Electrolyte** | Acidified CuSO‚āĄ solution (‚Čą 200 g/L CuSO‚āĄ + 50 g/L H‚āāSO‚āĄ). |
| **DC Power Source** | A rectifier delivering a low-voltage, high-current DC supply (typically 1‚Äď6 V, 10‚Äď500 A). |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ "The Copper Conveyor Belt"

Imagine the electrolyte solution as a **busy one-way street**, and the Cu¬≤‚Āļ ions as tiny copper-coated marbles rolling along it. The **anode is the marble factory** at one end that constantly creates and pushes these copper marbles into the street. The **DC power source is the traffic policeman** who shouts "move forward!" and forces the marbles to march in one direction. The **cathode is the magnet at the other end of the street** that catches the marbles and *welds them onto itself* in a thin, shiny coat. The police officer's whistle (current) must blow for a specific duration (time) to deposit the right thickness of copper on the magnet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Copper for Electrical Engineers?**
> - Resistivity of copper ‚Čą **1.68 √ó 10‚ĀĽ‚Āł ő©¬∑m** (only silver is better at 1.59 √ó 10‚ĀĽ‚Āł ő©¬∑m).
> - Copper electroplating is the **backbone of the PCB (Printed Circuit Board) industry** ‚ÄĒ every signal trace on your phone's motherboard is built by controlled copper electroplating into photoresist-defined channels.
> - Faraday's constant used: **F = 96,485 C mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ** (universally) and often rounded to **96,500 C mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ** for KTU numerical problems.

## 1.4 Standard Reduction Potential ‚ÄĒ The Thermodynamic Driving Force

The reduction half-reaction for copper deposition is:

$$
\text{Cu}^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^- \longrightarrow \text{Cu}_{(s)}
$$

The **standard reduction potential** is:

$$
E^{\circ}_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34 \text{ V}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Because E¬į is **positive**, copper ions are *noble* ‚ÄĒ they readily accept electrons and plate out without needing very high voltages. This is what makes copper electroplating so *energy-efficient* compared to plating less-noble metals like zinc (E¬į = ‚ąí0.76 V) or chromium.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ‚ÄĒ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step

The electroplating process follows a strict, ordered sequence of electrochemical events. Below is the structured logic as expected in KTU ESE answers.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Establishing the Electrolytic Circuit**
The DC power source is switched ON. The anode is connected to the **positive terminal**, the cathode to the **negative terminal**. The external circuit is completed through the conducting wires; the internal circuit is completed through the ion-conducting electrolyte.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Anodic Oxidation (Dissolution of the Copper Anode)**
At the anode, copper atoms lose two electrons each and enter the solution as Cu¬≤‚Āļ ions:

$$
\text{Cu}_{(s)} \longrightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^- \quad (\text{Oxidation, } E^{\circ} = -0.34 \text{ V})
$$

This is the *reverse* of the reduction reaction. The anode must be made of pure copper so that the Cu¬≤‚Āļ concentration remains constant (otherwise the solution would be depleted of copper over time).

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Migration of Cu¬≤‚Āļ Ions**
The Cu¬≤‚Āļ ions formed at the anode migrate through the bulk electrolyte toward the negatively charged cathode, driven by the electric field. Hydronium (H‚Āļ) and sulfate (SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ) ions carry the remaining ionic current.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Cathodic Reduction (Copper Deposition)**
At the cathode, each Cu¬≤‚Āļ ion gains two electrons and is reduced to a copper atom, which embeds itself into the crystal lattice of the cathode surface:

$$
\text{Cu}^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^- \longrightarrow \text{Cu}_{(s)} \quad (\text{Reduction, } E^{\circ} = +0.34 \text{ V})
$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Hydrogen Suppression**
Ideally, only copper is deposited. However, water can also be reduced:

$$
2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \longrightarrow \text{H}_{2(g)} + 2\text{OH}^- \quad (E^{\circ} = -0.83 \text{ V})
$$

Because the reduction potential of Cu¬≤‚Āļ is *more positive* than that of water, copper deposits *preferentially*. The addition of H‚āāSO‚āĄ increases [H‚Āļ], which further suppresses H‚āā evolution. The **hydrogen overpotential** on copper is also relatively high, which helps.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Critical Concept ‚ÄĒ Current Efficiency (ő∑):**
> In practice, a small percentage of the current is wasted on side reactions (H‚āā evolution, O‚āā at anode). The **cathode current efficiency** is defined as:
>
> $$
> \eta = \frac{\text{Actual mass deposited}}{\text{Theoretical mass from Faraday's law}} \times 100\%
> $$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's **First Law** ‚ÄĒ mass deposited (m) | grams (g) |
| 2 | $Z = \dfrac{M}{n \cdot F}$ | Electrochemical equivalent of a substance | g C‚ĀĽ¬Ļ |
| 3 | $m = Z \cdot I \cdot t$ | Compact form of Faraday's first law | grams (g) |
| 4 | $\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2}$ | Faraday's **Second Law** ‚ÄĒ equivalent weights ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $Q = I \cdot t = n \cdot F$ | Charge in coulombs = moles of electrons √ó Faraday | C (coulombs) |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | Cathode current efficiency | % |
| 7 | $\text{Thickness } d = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A}$ | Coating thickness from deposited mass | cm or ¬Ķm |
| 8 | $E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$ | Cell EMF (for electroplating, $E_{\text{cell}} < 0$, so external voltage is applied) | Volts (V) |
| 9 | $W = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Electrical energy consumed during plating | Joules (J) or kWh |
| 10 | $\text{Charge density} = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | Critical to uniform plating (A dm‚ĀĽ¬≤ typical) | A dm‚ĀĽ¬≤ |

**Constants you MUST memorise for KTU:**

| Symbol | Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $F$ | 96,485 C mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ (or **96,500 C mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ** rounded) | Faraday constant |
| $M_{\text{Cu}}$ | 63.5 g mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ (sometimes 63.546) | Molar mass of copper |
| $n$ | 2 | Electrons transferred per Cu¬≤‚Āļ |
| $Z_{\text{Cu}}$ | $63.5 / (2 \times 96500) = 3.294 \times 10^{-4}$ g C‚ĀĽ¬Ļ | ECE of copper |
| $E^{\circ}_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}}$ | +0.34 V | Standard reduction potential |
| $\rho_{\text{Cu}}$ | 8.96 g cm‚ĀĽ¬≥ | Density of copper |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Prohibition in Tables:** Inside the markdown tables above, the absolute-value-style symbol $\vert x \vert$ would break the rendering. We have used **forward slashes, parentheses, and "dimensionless"** instead. Always replace $\vert$ with `\vert` or `\mid` if needed in KTU answer sheets.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Copper Electroplating |
|---|---|
| **PCB Manufacturing** | Through-hole plating and via filling in multilayer PCBs. |
| **Semiconductor Packaging** | Damascene copper plating for sub-10 nm interconnects. |
| **Automotive Electrical** | Coating busbars, connectors, and battery terminals. |
| **RF & Microwave** | Waveguide interior plating for low signal loss. |
| **Decorative** | Antique finishes, jewellery base-coats. |
| **Anti-corrosion** | Undercoat before nickel/chrome on steel. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Faraday's First Law Applied to Copper

We will derive the **mass of copper deposited (m)** as a function of current (I) and time (t).

**Starting premise:** 1 mole of electrons = 96,500 coulombs (Faraday constant F).

**Step A ‚ÄĒ Charge per copper atom**
Each Cu¬≤‚Āļ ion requires **n = 2 electrons** to be reduced to Cu. Therefore, the charge needed to deposit 1 atom of Cu = $2e$, where $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C.

**Step B ‚ÄĒ Charge for 1 mole of Cu**
Number of Cu atoms in 1 mole = Avogadro's number $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$.

$$
Q_{1\,\text{mole Cu}} = n \cdot N_A \cdot e = n \cdot F
$$

where $F = N_A \cdot e = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 96{,}485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$.

**Step C ‚ÄĒ Mass for 1 mole of Cu**
Molar mass of Cu = $M = 63.5$ g mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ.

So the mass deposited per coulomb (the **electrochemical equivalent** Z) is:

$$
Z = \frac{M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{63.5}{2 \times 96{,}500} = 3.294 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g C}^{-1}
$$

**Step D ‚ÄĒ Generalization for charge Q**
If a total charge $Q = I \cdot t$ passes through the cell, the mass deposited is:

$$
\boxed{m = Z \cdot I \cdot t = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}}
$$

This is **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis** applied to copper.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Derivations (KTU-Style)

### **Problem 1 ‚ÄĒ Mass of Copper Deposited**

**Question:** A current of **2.5 A** is passed through a copper sulfate solution for **1 hour 20 minutes**. Calculate the mass of copper deposited. (Take $M_{\text{Cu}} = 63.5$ g mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ, $F = 96{,}500$ C mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Convert time to seconds**
$$
t = 1 \text{ h } 20 \text{ min} = (60 + 20) \times 60 = 80 \times 60 = 4800 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Total charge passed**
$$
Q = I \cdot t = 2.5 \times 4800 = 12{,}000 \text{ C}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Apply Faraday's First Law**
$$
m = \frac{M \cdot Q}{n \cdot F} = \frac{63.5 \times 12{,}000}{2 \times 96{,}500}
$$

$$
m = \frac{7.62 \times 10^{5}}{1.93 \times 10^{5}} = 3.9487 \text{ g}
$$

$$
\boxed{m \approx 3.95 \text{ g of copper}}
$$

### **Problem 2 ‚ÄĒ Coating Thickness Calculation**

**Question:** The same charge is used to coat a flat steel plate of area **250 cm¬≤** on one side. Assuming 100% current efficiency, calculate the **average thickness** of the copper coating. ($\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 8.96$ g cm‚ĀĽ¬≥).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Volume of copper deposited**
$$
V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{3.9487}{8.96} = 0.4407 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Thickness = Volume / Area**
$$
d = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{0.4407}{250} = 1.763 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Convert to micrometres**
$$
\boxed{d \approx 17.63 \text{ ¬Ķm}}
$$

### **Problem 3 ‚ÄĒ Current Efficiency**

**Question:** In an actual run, only **3.50 g** of copper was deposited when theoretically **3.95 g** was expected. Calculate the cathode current efficiency.

**Solution:**

$$
\eta = \frac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100 = \frac{3.50}{3.95} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta \approx 88.61\%}
$$

The remaining ~11.4% of the current was lost to hydrogen evolution at the cathode.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Comparison of Common Plating Metals

| Property | Copper (Cu) | Silver (Ag) | Zinc (Zn) | Nickel (Ni) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| E¬į (M‚ĀŅ‚Āļ/M) | +0.34 V | +0.80 V | ‚ąí0.76 V | ‚ąí0.25 V |
| n (electrons) | 2 | 1 | 2 | 2 |
| M (g mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ) | 63.5 | 107.87 | 65.38 | 58.69 |
| Z (g C‚ĀĽ¬Ļ) | $3.29 \times 10^{-4}$ | $1.118 \times 10^{-3}$ | $3.39 \times 10^{-4}$ | $3.04 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| Plating ease | Very easy | Easy (noble) | Hard (active) | Moderate |
| KTU weight | **‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ** | ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ | ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ | ‚ėÖ‚ėÖ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The most common numerical in ESE Module 1 is "mass of Cu deposited" using Faraday's law. You will almost certainly see a 7-mark numerical where you must derive, plug in, and state the final answer with units.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 ‚ÄĒ Structural Diagrams & Schematics
