- [x] **Hydrometallurgy:** Aqua regia leaching of Au; electrowinning recovers pure metal.
- [x] **Biometallurgy:** Uses *Acidithiobacillus* spp. to bioleach Cu from low-grade e-waste; eco-friendly but slow.
- [x] **Multi-stage recovery formula:** $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \eta_{i}$ (multiplicative cascade).
- [x] **Mass balance equation:** $m_{\text{in}} = m_{\text{out}} + m_{\text{loss}} + m_{\text{retained}}$.
- [x] **Embodied energy savings:** Recycled Al saves **~95%**, recycled Cu saves **~85%**, recycled steel saves **~60%** vs. primary production.
- [x] **Aquarium regia reaction (must memorize):** $\text{Au} + 4\,\text{HCl} + \text{HNO}_{3} \rightarrow \text{HAuCl}_{4} + 2\,\text{H}_{2}\text{O} + \text{NO}$
- [x] **Microbial leaching reaction (must memorize):** $2\,\text{Fe}^{2+} + 0.5\,\text{O}_{2} + 2\,\text{H}^{+} \xrightarrow{\text{bacteria}} 2\,\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O}$
- [x] **Key KPI to remember:** Global e-waste recycling rate is only **~22.3%** ‚ÄĒ Kerala's target is **30% by 2026** (KSPCB).
- [x] **Two flagship case studies:** Sweden's PRO model (99% recycling) and Japan's Home Appliance Recycling Law (mandatory EPR).
- [x] **EPR** = Extended Producer Responsibility ‚ÄĒ manufacturer's legal obligation to take back end-of-life products.
- [x] **Always include a labeled diagram in 14-mark answers** to secure the 2-mark diagram allocation.
- [x] **Circular Economy ‚Č† Recycling:** Circular economy is the *broader* framework (including design, reuse, repair, remanufacture, recycle), while recycling is *one* operation within it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemistry of Climate Change: Greenhouse Gases, Ozone Depletion, Sustainable Development, Introduction to Sustainable Development Goals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE AND ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chemistry of Climate Change: Greenhouse Gases, Ozone Depletion, Sustainable Development, Introduction to Sustainable Development Goals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Climate Change (KTU 2024 Definition)

**Climate Change** is a statistically significant variation in either the mean state of the climate or in its variability, persisting for an extended period (typically decades or longer). From a **chemistry perspective**, climate change refers to alterations in Earth's energy balance caused by anthropogenic emissions of radiatively active (greenhouse) gases and aerosols that modify the atmospheric composition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The emphasis is on the *chemical* origin, *molecular mechanism*, and *sustainable remediation* of climate change ‚ÄĒ not on meteorological modelling.

---

## 1.2 Greenhouse Gases (GHGs) ‚ÄĒ Formal Definition

A **Greenhouse Gas (GHG)** is any gaseous molecule in the atmosphere capable of absorbing and re-emitting **infrared (IR) radiation** in the wavelength range of approximately **4 ¬Ķm to 100 ¬Ķm**, thereby trapping outgoing terrestrial long-wave radiation and contributing to the warming of the lower atmosphere (troposphere) and Earth's surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Syllabus Highlight):**
> A greenhouse gas is a polyatomic, IR-active molecule possessing a **permanent or transient dipole moment** that undergoes vibrational-rotational transitions matching the IR emission spectrum of Earth (peak at ~10 ¬Ķm).

The principal GHGs covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

| GHG | Chemical Formula | Primary Source |
|---|---|---|
| Carbon Dioxide | $CO_2$ | Fossil fuel combustion, deforestation |
| Methane | $CH_4$ | Rice paddies, livestock, natural gas leaks |
| Nitrous Oxide | $N_2O$ | Fertilizers, combustion |
| Ozone (tropospheric) | $O_3$ | Photochemical smog, NOx + VOC reactions |
| Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) | $CFCl_3$, $CF_2Cl_2$ | Refrigerants, aerosols |
| Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs) | $CH_2FCF_3$ | Refrigerant substitutes |
| Water Vapour | $H_2O$ | Evaporation (natural amplifier) |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Blanket at Night" Analogy**
> Imagine Earth wearing a **transparent thermal blanket** made of trace gas molecules. **Sunlight (short-wave, visible)** passes right through the blanket during the day, warming the ground. At night, the warm ground tries to **release heat as infrared radiation** ‚ÄĒ but the blanket molecules (GHGs) **trap and re-radiate** that heat back down. The thicker the blanket (more GHGs), the warmer you stay. Human industrial activity is effectively making the blanket thicker, year after year.

**Why polyatomic and not monoatomic?** A monoatomic gas like $N_2$ or $Ar$ has **no vibrational modes** that change the dipole moment ‚ÄĒ IR radiation passes straight through. Polyatomic GHGs like $CO_2$ and $CH_4$ have **bending and stretching vibrations** that create oscillating dipoles, which **resonantly absorb** IR photons of matching energy. This is the heart of the chemistry.

---

## 1.4 Ozone Layer Depletion ‚ÄĒ Definition

**Ozone Depletion** refers to the chemical destruction of stratospheric ozone ($O_3$, concentrated in the **ozone layer** at altitudes of **15‚Äď35 km**) by catalytically active free radical species, particularly **chlorine radicals ($Cl^¬∑$)** and **bromine radicals ($Br^¬∑$)** released from anthropogenic halogenated source gases (chiefly CFCs and halons).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Style):**
> Ozone depletion is the reduction in the **total columnar ozone abundance** (measured in **Dobson Units, DU**) of the stratosphere due to heterogeneous and homogeneous chemical reactions initiated by photolysis of halogenated organohalogen source gases.

---

## 1.5 Sustainable Development ‚ÄĒ Brundtland Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brundtland Commission (WCED, 1987) Definition:**
> *"Sustainable development is the development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs."*

This is the **canonical, exam-defining definition** for KTU. It rests on two pillars:
1. **Needs** ‚ÄĒ especially the essential needs of the world's poor
2. **Limitations** ‚ÄĒ imposed by technology and social organization on the environment's ability to meet present and future needs

---

## 1.6 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) ‚ÄĒ Overview

In **September 2015**, the UN General Assembly adopted **Resolution A/RES/70/1 ‚ÄĒ "Transforming our World: The 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development"** which established **17 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)** with **169 targets** and **231 indicators**. They succeeded the 8 Millennium Development Goals (MDGs, 2000‚Äď2015).

The 17 SDGs are: (1) No Poverty, (2) Zero Hunger, (3) Good Health and Well-being, (4) Quality Education, (5) Gender Equality, (6) Clean Water and Sanitation, (7) Affordable and Clean Energy, (8) Decent Work and Economic Growth, (9) Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure, (10) Reduced Inequalities, (11) Sustainable Cities and Communities, (12) Responsible Consumption and Production, (13) Climate Action, (14) Life Below Water, (15) Life on Land, (16) Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions, (17) Partnerships for the Goals.

---

## 1.7 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Relevance:** Electrical and information science engineers contribute to climate change both positively (low-power computing, renewable grids, energy-efficient algorithms) and negatively (data centre energy use, refrigerant leaks from cooling systems, e-waste). Understanding the *underlying chemistry* of GHGs and ozone-depleting substances is essential for designing **environmentally benign** hardware, refrigerants, and energy systems aligned with SDG 7, SDG 12, and SDG 13.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Chemistry of the Greenhouse Effect ‚ÄĒ Mechanism

The greenhouse effect is a **radiative, vibrational-resonance phenomenon**. The chemistry can be broken into the following operational logic:

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Short-wave solar input:** The Sun emits radiation peaking at ~**0.5 ¬Ķm** (visible). Earth's atmosphere is largely **transparent** to this radiation, so the surface absorbs ~70% of incoming solar energy.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Surface re-emission (long-wave IR):** A warm Earth (effective blackbody at **T ‚Čą 288 K**) re-emits energy by **Wien's displacement law** at peak wavelength:

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T}$$

where $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m¬∑K}$ is **Wien's displacement constant**.

For T = 288 K, this gives $\lambda_{max} \approx 10.06 \, \mu m$, which falls in the **mid-infrared atmospheric window**.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ IR absorption by GHGs:** Polyatomic molecules with IR-active vibrational modes **resonantly absorb** photons matching their vibrational transition energies. For a simple harmonic oscillator, the selection rule is $\Delta v = \pm 1$, and the energy gap is:

$$E_{vib} = \left(v + \frac{1}{2}\right) h \nu$$

where $\nu$ is the vibrational frequency. For real molecules, the **anharmonic oscillator** with overtone bands ($\Delta v = \pm 2, \pm 3, \dots$) gives weaker absorptions at lower frequencies.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Re-emission in all directions:** The excited molecule **collides with neighbours** (collision deactivation, lifetime ~$10^{-5}$‚Äď$10^{-9}$ s) and re-emits an IR photon with **equal probability in all directions**. ~50% of the re-emitted photons travel **downward**, warming the surface further ‚ÄĒ this is the **enhanced greenhouse effect**.

---

## 2.2 Why CO‚āā is a Greenhouse Gas (Molecular Symmetry Argument)

A molecule is **IR-active** if its vibration causes a change in **dipole moment ($\mu$)**.

- $CO_2$ is **linear** ($O=C=O$). The symmetric stretch ($v_1$) leaves $\mu = 0$ unchanged ‚ÄĒ **IR-INACTIVE**. The asymmetric stretch ($v_3$) and the bending modes ($v_2$, doubly degenerate) **create a transient dipole** ‚ÄĒ **IR-ACTIVE**, absorbing at **4.26 ¬Ķm** and **15 ¬Ķm** respectively.
- $N_2$ and $O_2$ are **homonuclear diatomics** ‚ÄĒ no dipole change possible ‚ÄĒ **IR-inactive** (transparent).
- $CH_4$ (tetrahedral) has 4 IR-active modes including the strong bending at **7.66 ¬Ķm**.
- $N_2O$ (linear, asymmetric) has a permanent dipole and is **strongly IR-active**.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Takeaway:** *The dipole-moment-change rule* is the single most-tested concept. Be ready to justify why CO‚āā is a GHG but N‚āā is not.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Standard Units | Exam Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\lambda_{max} = b/T$ | Wien's displacement law | m | Earth's peak IR emission ~10 ¬Ķm |
| $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$ | Photon energy | J | Used to match vibrational transitions |
| $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | Stefan‚ÄďBoltzmann constant | W¬∑m‚ĀĽ¬≤¬∑K‚ĀĽ‚Āī | Use in $P = \sigma A T^4$ |
| $GWP_x = \dfrac{\int_0^{TH} a_x \cdot F_x(t) \, dt}{\int_0^{TH} a_{CO_2} \cdot F_{CO_2}(t) \, dt}$ | Global Warming Potential (over horizon TH) | dimensionless | TH = 20, 100, 500 yr typically |
| $CO_2e = m_x \times GWP_{100}(x)$ | CO‚āā-equivalent emissions | kg CO‚āāe | Used in carbon accounting |
| $RF = \Delta F$ | Radiative forcing | W¬∑m‚ĀĽ¬≤ | Positive = warming |
| $\tau_x = \dfrac{m_x}{S_x}$ | Atmospheric lifetime | years | Methane ~12 yr, N‚āāO ~114 yr |
| $1 \text{ DU} = 2.687 \times 10^{16} \text{ molec¬∑cm}^{-2}$ | Dobson Unit | molec¬∑cm‚ĀĽ¬≤ | 300 DU ‚Čą 3 mm pure O‚āÉ at STP |
| $\dot{X}_{destruction} = k[X][O]$ | Bimolecular rate law | molec¬∑cm‚ĀĽ¬≥¬∑s‚ĀĽ¬Ļ | Used in Chapman + catalytic cycles |
| $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ | Arrhenius rate constant | cm¬≥¬∑molec‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑s‚ĀĽ¬Ļ | Stratospheric T ~ 220 K |
| $C_p - C_v = R$ | Mayer's relation | J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ | For diatomic ideal gases, $C_p = 7R/2$ |

---

## 2.4 Stratospheric Ozone Chemistry (Chapman + Catalytic Cycles)

### 2.4.1 Chapman Cycle (Natural Ozone Formation)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Oxygen Photolysis:** UV-C ($\lambda < 240$ nm) photolyses $O_2$:

$$O_2 + h\nu \, (\lambda \le 240 \text{ nm}) \rightarrow 2O^¬∑$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Ozone Formation (Three-body reaction):** Ground-state oxygen atom combines with $O_2$ in the presence of a third body M ($N_2$ or $O_2$):

$$O^¬∑ + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Natural Ozone Destruction:** $O_3$ photolysis ($\lambda < 320$ nm):

$$O_3 + h\nu \rightarrow O_2 + O^¬∑$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Ozone‚ÄďO Atom recombination:**

$$O_3 + O^¬∑ \rightarrow 2O_2$$

Chapman's cycle alone predicts **~10‚Äď12 DU higher** stratospheric ozone than observed ‚ÄĒ proving that **catalytic destruction cycles** dominate.

### 2.4.2 Catalytic Destruction by Chlorine (CFC Pathway)

**Step A ‚ÄĒ CFC Photolysis in stratosphere** ($\lambda \le 220$ nm):

$$CFCl_3 + h\nu \rightarrow CFCl_2^¬∑ + Cl^¬∑$$

$$CF_2Cl_2 + h\nu \rightarrow CF_2Cl^¬∑ + Cl^¬∑$$

**Step B ‚ÄĒ Ozone destruction (chain cycle):**

$$Cl^¬∑ + O_3 \rightarrow ClO^¬∑ + O_2$$

$$O_3 + h\nu \rightarrow O_2 + O^¬∑$$

$$ClO^¬∑ + O^¬∑ \rightarrow Cl^¬∑ + O_2$$

**Step C ‚ÄĒ Net reaction:**

$$2O_3 \rightarrow 3O_2 \quad \text{(repeat } 10^5 \text{ times per Cl atom)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The chlorine radical is **regenerated** in Step C ‚ÄĒ it acts as a true **catalyst**. A single Cl atom can destroy ~**100,000 O‚āÉ molecules** before being sequestered by reservoir species (HCl, ClONO‚āā).

### 2.4.3 Reservoir Species and Heterogeneous Reactions (Polar Ozone Hole)

Over Antarctica, Polar Stratospheric Clouds (PSCs) provide surfaces for:

$$ClONO_2 + HCl_{(s)} \rightarrow Cl_2 + HNO_3$$

$$Cl_2 + h\nu \rightarrow 2Cl^¬∑$$

These reactions release photolytically active chlorine, producing the **Antarctic ozone hole** observed since the 1980s (lowest ~**73 DU** in September 2000).

---

## 2.5 Global Warming Potential (GWP) ‚ÄĒ Detailed Derivation

The **GWP** is an index measuring the **time-integrated radiative forcing** of a unit mass of a GHG relative to that of $CO_2$ over a chosen time horizon $TH$ (typically **100 years** for policy).

$$GWP_x = \frac{\int_0^{TH} a_x \cdot F_x(t) \, dt}{\int_0^{TH} a_{CO_2} \cdot F_{CO_2}(t) \, dt}$$

where:
- $a_x$ = radiative efficiency per unit mass of gas $x$ (W¬∑m‚ĀĽ¬≤¬∑kg‚ĀĽ¬Ļ)
- $F_x(t)$ = fraction of an emitted pulse remaining at time $t$ (accounts for atmospheric decay)
- $F_{CO_2}(t)$ uses the **Bern carbon cycle model** for $CO_2$ removal

**Standard GWP‚āĀ‚āÄ‚āÄ values (IPCC AR6):**

| Gas | GWP‚āĀ‚āÄ‚āÄ | Atmospheric Lifetime |
|---|---|---|
| $CO_2$ | 1 | centuries |
| $CH_4$ | 27.9 (incl. climate-carbon feedback) | 11.8 yr |
| $N_2O$ | 273 | 109 yr |
| $CFC-11$ ($CCl_3F$) | 6,300 | 52 yr |
| $CFC-12$ ($CCl_2F_2$) | 12,500 | 102 yr |
| $HFC-134a$ | 1,300 | 14 yr |
| $SF_6$ | 25,200 | 3,200 yr |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why an Electrical Engineer Should Care:**
> 1. **Refrigerant Selection:** Replacing CFC-12 with HFC-134a (GWP 1,300) is *good* but still problematic ‚ÄĒ new alternatives are HFO-1234yf (GWP <1) and $CO_2$ (R-744) used in transcritical cycles.
> 2. **Data Centre Cooling:** Server farms use millions of kg of refrigerants ‚ÄĒ leakage directly impacts SDG 13 (Climate Action).
> 3. **Semiconductor Manufacturing:** $SF_6$ is used in plasma etching; with GWP 25,200 it is the **most potent GHG** known.
> 4. **Carbon-Aware Computing:** Green software engineering can reduce electricity demand, indirectly cutting $CO_2$ emissions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Wavelength of Peak IR Emission from Earth

**Problem:** A KTU question typically asks: "At what wavelength does Earth emit maximum thermal radiation?"

**Given:**
- Effective radiating temperature of Earth's surface, $T = 288 \text{ K}$
- Wien's displacement constant, $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m¬∑K}$

**Step 1:** State Wien's displacement law.

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the known values with units explicit.

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m¬∑K}}{288 \text{ K}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the quotient.

$$\lambda_{max} = 1.00625 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to micrometres (standard unit for IR spectroscopy).

$$\lambda_{max} = 10.06 \, \mu m$$

**Step 5:** Physical interpretation.

> The peak IR emission at **10.06 ¬Ķm** lies in the **vibrational bending mode of $CO_2$** (15 ¬Ķm is its strongest band, with a wing extending into 10 ¬Ķm) and the **bending band of $CH_4$** (7.66 ¬Ķm). This *coincidence* between Earth's blackbody peak and the absorption band of GHGs is the molecular basis of the greenhouse effect.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: $CO_2$-equivalent emissions of a mixed-gas release

**Problem:** A factory releases **500 kg of $CH_4$** and **200 kg of $N_2O$** per year. Calculate the total $CO_2$-equivalent annual emission using GWP‚āĀ‚āÄ‚āÄ.

**Step 1:** Recall the formula for $CO_2$-equivalent.

$$CO_2e = m_x \times GWP_x$$

**Step 2:** For methane.

$$CO_2e_{CH_4} = 500 \text{ kg} \times 27.9 = 13{,}950 \text{ kg CO}_2e$$

**Step 3:** For nitrous oxide.

$$CO_2e_{N_2O} = 200 \text{ kg} \times 273 = 54{,}600 \text{ kg CO}_2e$$

**Step 4:** Sum the contributions.

$$CO_2e_{total} = 13{,}950 + 54{,}600 = 68{,}550 \text{ kg CO}_2e = 68.55 \text{ t CO}_2e$$

**Step 5:** Discuss the dominance.

> Although 500 kg of $CH_4$ is 2.5√ó the mass of 200 kg of $N_2O$, the $N_2O$ contribution is **3.9√ó larger** in $CO_2e$ terms. This illustrates why GWP-weighted accounting ‚ÄĒ not mass accounting ‚ÄĒ is the standard.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Atmospheric Lifetime from Exponential Decay

The atmospheric concentration of a pulse-released gas decays approximately as:

$$C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

For $CH_4$, the mean atmospheric lifetime is $\tau \approx 11.8$ yr. After 50 years, the residual fraction is:

**Step 1:** Plug in.

$$\frac{C(50)}{C_0} = e^{-50/11.8}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the exponent.

$$-\frac{50}{11.8} = -4.237$$

**Step 3:** Compute the exponential.

$$\frac{C(50)}{C_0} = e^{-4.237} = 0.01445 \approx 1.45\%$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.

> After 50 years, only ~1.45% of an initial $CH_4$ pulse remains. Contrast with $SF_6$ ($\tau = 3{,}200$ yr), where ~99% of a pulse remains after a human lifetime ‚ÄĒ hence its astronomical GWP.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Arrhenius Rate Constant for Stratospheric Ozone Destruction

A key catalytic reaction: $Cl^¬∑ + O_3 \rightarrow ClO^¬∑ + O_2$

Given (literature value): $A = 2.8 \times 10^{-11} \text{ cm}^3 \text{ molec}^{-1} \text{ s}^{-1}$, $E_a/R = 250 \text{ K}$, stratospheric T = 220 K.

**Step 1:** Arrhenius equation.

$$k = A \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$k = 2.8 \times 10^{-11} \times \exp\left(-\frac{250}{220}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the exponent.

$$-\frac{250}{220} = -1.1364$$

**Step 4:** Compute the exponential.

$$e^{-1.1364} = 0.3209$$

**Step 5:** Multiply.

$$k = 2.8 \times 10^{-11} \times 0.3209 = 8.99 \times 10^{-12} \text{ cm}^3 \text{ molec}^{-1} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 6:** Interpretation.

> At 220 K, $Cl^¬∑$ destroys $O_3$ at a rate of ~$9 \times 10^{-12}$ cm¬≥¬∑molec‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑s‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. This is a fast bimolecular rate ‚ÄĒ catalytic chains are extremely efficient.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Two Environmental Crises

| Dimension | Climate Change (GHG) | Ozone Depletion |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Molecules | $CO_2$, $CH_4$, $N_2O$, CFCs, HFCs | $O_3$ (destroyed by $Cl^¬∑$, $Br^¬∑$, $NO_x$, $HO_x$) |
| Atmospheric Layer | Troposphere (warming) + stratosphere | Stratosphere (15‚Äď35 km) |
| Source Mechanism | Combustion, agriculture, refrigerants | Photolysis of CFCs, halons |
| Key Reaction Type | IR vibrational absorption | Radical chain catalysis |
| Quantification Metric | GWP (100-yr), $CO_2e$ | Dobson Units (DU), O‚āĄ (ozone trend) |
| International Treaty | UNFCCC (1992), Paris Agreement (2015) | Vienna Convention (1985), Montreal Protocol (1987) |
| Status (2024) | Worsening ‚ÄĒ 421 ppm $CO_2$ | Recovering ‚ÄĒ ozone hole shrinking |
| Key SDGs | 7, 12, 13 | 3, 13, 14, 15 |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Converting CFC Leakage to $CO_2e$

A 1990s-era chiller leaks **2 kg of CFC-12 ($CCl_2F_2$)** per year. Compute its $CO_2$ equivalent.

**Step 1:** Use GWP‚āĀ‚āÄ‚āÄ of CFC-12 = 12,500.

**Step 2:** Apply formula.

$$CO_2e = m \times GWP = 2 \text{ kg} \times 12{,}500$$

**Step 3:** Result.

$$CO_2e = 25{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2e = 25 \text{ tonnes CO}_2e$$

**Step 4:** Equivalence to daily emissions.

> An average passenger car emits ~**4.6 t CO‚āā/year**. A 2 kg leak of CFC-12 is equivalent to **~5.4 years of one car's emissions** ‚ÄĒ a stark illustration of why Montreal Protocol matters.

---

## 3.7 Derivation: Estimating Radiative Forcing from $CO_2$ Increase

The simplified IPCC formula for $CO_2$ radiative forcing is:

$$\Delta F = 5.35 \ln\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right) \quad \text{[W¬∑m}^{-2}\text{]}$$

If $CO_2$ rises from 280 ppm (pre-industrial) to 421 ppm (2023):

**Step 1:** Form the ratio.

$$\frac{C}{C_0} = \frac{421}{280} = 1.5036$$

**Step 2:** Take natural log.

$$\ln(1.5036) = 0.4078$$

**Step 3:** Multiply.

$$\Delta F = 5.35 \times 0.4078 = 2.182 \text{ W¬∑m}^{-2}$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.

> The radiative forcing from $CO_2$-alone is **+2.18 W¬∑m‚ĀĽ¬≤**, contributing to observed global warming of ~+1.1 ¬įC above pre-industrial.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The Greenhouse Effect ‚ÄĒ Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SunA[SUN Shortwave Visible 0.5 micrometer] --> AtmosA
    AtmosA[Atmospheric Layer Mostly Transparent to Visible]
    AtmosA --> SurfA[Earth Surface Absorbs 70 percent of Insolation]
    SurfA --> HeatOut[Surface Re-emits Longwave IR Peak 10 micrometer]
    HeatOut --> GHGA[Polyatomic GHG Molecules CO2 CH4 N2O H2O]
    GHGA --> Absorb[IR Active Vibrational Modes Absorb Photons]
    Absorb --> Collide[Collisional Deactivation Lifetime 10 power minus 5 s]
    Collide --> Reemit[Re-emit IR Photons Isotropically]
    Reemit --> HalfDown[50 percent Downward to Surface = Additional Warming]
    Reemit --> HalfUp[50 percent Upward to Space = Energy Loss]
    HalfDown --> SurfA
    HalfUp --> SpaceB[Outer Space Final Sink]
    SurfA --> HumAct[Human Activity Fossil Fuels Agriculture Refrigerants]
    HumAct --> Emit[Anthropogenic GHG Emissions]
    Emit --> GHGA
```

---

## 4.2 Stratospheric Ozone Destruction ‚ÄĒ Catalytic Chain Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CFCSrc[Anthropogenic CFCs Released at Surface] --> TropoDelay[Long Tropospheric Lifetime Years to Decades]
    TropoDelay --> Strato[Transport to Stratosphere 15 to 35 km]
    Strato --> UVPhoto[UV Photolysis lambda less than 220 nm]
    UVPhoto --> ClRad[Chlorine Radical Cl dot Released]
    ClRad --> Rxn1[Step 1 Cl dot plus O3 gives ClO dot plus O2]
    Rxn1 --> ClOrad[Chlorine Monoxide Radical ClO dot]
    ClOrad --> Rxn2[Step 2 ClO dot plus O dot gives Cl dot plus O2]
    Rxn2 --> ClRadRegen[Chlorine Regenerated Catalyst]
    ClRadRegen --> ClRad
    ClRadRegen --> NetRxn[Net Reaction 2 O3 gives 3 O2]
    NetRxn --> OzoneHole[Ozone Column Reduction Measured in Dobson Units]
    PSC[Polar Stratospheric Clouds Heterogeneous Surface] --> ActivateRes[Activate Reservoir Species HCl and ClONO2]
    ActivateRes --> ClRad
```

---

## 4.3 Sustainable Development Framework ‚ÄĒ Three-Pillar Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SDCenter[Sustainable Development Brundtland 1987] --> P1[Pillar 1 Environmental Protection]
    SDCenter --> P2[Pillar 2 Economic Development]
    SDCenter --> P3[Pillar 3 Social Equity]
    P1 --> GHG[GHG Reduction Carbon Neutrality]
    P1 --> O3[Ozone Layer Protection Montreal Protocol]
    P1 --> Biodiv[Biodiversity Conservation SDG 14 15]
    P2 --> CleanE[Clean Energy Access SDG 7]
    P2 --> Industry[Sustainable Industry SDG 9]
    P2 --> Work[Decent Work SDG 8]
    P3 --> Poverty[No Poverty SDG 1]
    P3 --> Hunger[Zero Hunger SDG 2]
    P3 --> Health[Good Health SDG 3]
    P3 --> Education[Quality Education SDG 4]
    P3 --> Gender[Gender Equality SDG 5]
    GHG --> Paris[Paris Agreement 2015 SDG 13]
    O3 --> Montreal[Montreal Protocol 1987 Kigali Amendment 2016]
    CleanE --> RE[Renewable Energy Solar Wind Hydrogen]
    RE --> ClimateTech[Climate Technology and Green Computing]
```

---

## 4.4 The 17 SDGs ‚ÄĒ Hierarchical Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SDG17[UN 2030 Agenda 17 SDGs 169 Targets] --> People[People Goals 1 to 5]
    SDG17 --> Planet[Planet Goals 6 plus 13 to 15]
    SDG17 --> Prosp[Prosperity Goals 7 to 12]
    SDG17 --> Peace[Peace Goals 16]
    SDG17 --> Part[Partnership Goal 17]
    People --> G1[1 No Poverty]
    People --> G2[2 Zero Hunger]
    People --> G3[3 Good Health]
    People --> G4[4 Quality Education]
    People --> G5[5 Gender Equality]
    Planet --> G6[6 Clean Water]
    Planet --> G13[13 Climate Action]
    Planet --> G14[14 Life Below Water]
    Planet --> G15[15 Life on Land]
    Prosp --> G7[7 Affordable Clean Energy]
    Prosp --> G8[8 Decent Work]
    Prosp --> G9[9 Industry Innovation]
    Prosp --> G10[10 Reduced Inequalities]
    Prosp --> G11[11 Sustainable Cities]
    Prosp --> G12[12 Responsible Consumption]
    Peace --> G16[16 Peace Justice Institutions]
    Part --> G17[17 Partnerships]
```

---

## 4.5 Comparative Radiative Forcing Contributions (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RF[Total Anthropogenic Radiative Forcing 2.72 W per m squared] --> CO2R[CO2 Contribution 1.68 W per m squared]
    RF --> CH4R[CH4 Contribution 0.54 W per m squared]
    RF --> N2OR[N2O Contribution 0.21 W per m squared]
    RF --> HaloR[Halogenated Gases CFC HFC 0.41 W per m squared]
    RF --> O3R[Tropospheric O3 0.47 W per m squared]
    RF --> AerR[Aerosols minus 0.95 W per m squared Cooling]
    RF --> LandR[Land Use Change minus 0.20 W per m squared]
    CO2R --> EndState[Net Positive Forcing Global Warming]
    AerR --> EndState
    EndState --> TempRise[Observed Temperature Rise plus 1.1 C above pre industrial]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024 Model]**
*Define a greenhouse gas. Why is $CO_2$ a greenhouse gas while $N_2$ is not, even though both are triatomic/polyatomic in the broader sense?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A greenhouse gas is a molecule in the atmosphere that **absorbs and re-emits infrared radiation** in the 4‚Äď100 ¬Ķm range, thereby trapping outgoing terrestrial long-wave radiation. (1 mark)

$CO_2$ is a polyatomic molecule with **IR-active vibrational modes** ‚ÄĒ the asymmetric stretch ($\nu_3$ at 4.26 ¬Ķm) and the doubly degenerate bending mode ($\nu_2$ at 15 ¬Ķm) ‚ÄĒ which cause an **oscillating dipole moment** that couples with IR photons. (1 mark)

$N_2$ is a **homonuclear diatomic** with no dipole moment and no dipole change possible during vibration; it is therefore **IR-inactive** and transparent to thermal radiation. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023 Model]**
*State the Brundtland definition of sustainable development. Name any four of the 17 SDGs.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** *"Sustainable development is the development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs."* ‚ÄĒ World Commission on Environment and Development, 1987.

**Four SDGs (¬Ĺ mark each):**
1. SDG 7 ‚ÄĒ Affordable and Clean Energy
2. SDG 12 ‚ÄĒ Responsible Consumption and Production
3. SDG 13 ‚ÄĒ Climate Action
4. SDG 14 ‚ÄĒ Life Below Water

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. *Never* write $CO_2$ as "carbon dioxide gas" without specifying it is a GHG and the dominant anthropogenic forcing agent.
> 2. When asked for the chemistry of ozone depletion, students often write only the **Chapman cycle** ‚ÄĒ but KTU examiners specifically demand the **catalytic Cl cycle** (Marks awarded: Chapman = 3 of 7; Cl cycle = 4 of 7).
> 3. For SDG questions, students *lose marks* by listing the 17 goals without a one-line description. Always provide a brief descriptor of any 4‚Äď5 mentioned.
> 4. In GWP numericals, **state the GWP value** you are using (IPCC AR4 / AR5 / AR6 values differ). Failure to specify the source = ‚Äď0.5 mark deduction.
> 5. Avoid confusing $O_3$ **stratospheric good ozone** with $O_3$ **tropospheric bad ozone (pollutant)**. Examiners deduct for this.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024 Model]**
*(a)* Explain the **chemistry of the greenhouse effect** with reference to the molecular structure and IR activity of $CO_2$, $CH_4$ and $N_2O$. Discuss the dipole-moment criterion for IR activity. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* What is **Global Warming Potential (GWP)**? A power plant emits 1000 kg of $CH_4$ and 500 kg of $N_2O$ annually. Calculate the $CO_2$-equivalent emissions using IPCC AR6 GWP‚āĀ‚āÄ‚āÄ values. Comment on which gas contributes more per unit mass. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Chemistry of the Greenhouse Effect (7 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The greenhouse effect is the trapping of outgoing terrestrial long-wave (IR) radiation by polyatomic IR-active gases, raising the surface temperature above the effective radiative equilibrium.

- **Earth's emission spectrum (1 mark):** From Wien's law $\lambda_{max} = b/T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} / 288 \approx 10 \, \mu m$, Earth emits peak IR at 10 ¬Ķm. GHG vibrational bands must overlap with this region.

- **IR Activity criterion (2 marks):** A vibrational mode is IR-active **iff** it produces a change in dipole moment $\partial \mu / \partial q \neq 0$. $CO_2$ (linear, symmetric ‚ÄĒ only asymmetric stretch & bend change $\mu$). $CH_4$ (tetrahedral ‚ÄĒ 4 IR-active modes: $\nu_1$ symmetric stretch active, $\nu_3$ asymmetric stretch, $\nu_4$ bend all IR-active). $N_2O$ (linear but asymmetric ‚ÄĒ permanent dipole, all 3 modes IR-active).

- **Mechanism of absorption‚Äďre-emission (1 mark):** Molecule absorbs IR photon ‚Üí excited vibrational state ‚Üí collisional deactivation (10‚ĀĽ‚ĀĶ s) ‚Üí re-emits IR photon isotropically; 50% returns to surface, enhancing warming.

- **Key absorption bands (1 mark):** $CO_2$ at 4.26 ¬Ķm and 15 ¬Ķm; $CH_4$ at 3.3 ¬Ķm, 7.66 ¬Ķm; $N_2O$ at 4.5 ¬Ķm, 7.8 ¬Ķm.

- **Conclusion (1 mark):** The selective absorption by polyatomic molecules with IR-active modes, in the band overlap with Earth's IR spectrum, is the chemical foundation of the greenhouse effect.

**(b) GWP and $CO_2$-equivalent calculation (7 marks):**

- **GWP definition (2 marks):** GWP is the time-integrated radiative forcing of a unit mass of a gas relative to $CO_2$ over a chosen time horizon (commonly 100 years).

$$GWP_x = \frac{\int_0^{TH} a_x F_x(t) \, dt}{\int_0^{TH} a_{CO_2} F_{CO_2}(t) \, dt}$$

- **Numerical calculation (4 marks):** Using IPCC AR6 GWP‚āĀ‚āÄ‚āÄ values: $GWP_{CH_4} = 27.9$, $GWP_{N_2O} = 273$.

$$CO_2e_{CH_4} = 1000 \times 27.9 = 27{,}900 \text{ kg CO}_2e = 27.9 \text{ t CO}_2e$$

$$CO_2e_{N_2O} = 500 \times 273 = 136{,}500 \text{ kg CO}_2e = 136.5 \text{ t CO}_2e$$

$$CO_2e_{total} = 27.9 + 136.5 = 164.4 \text{ t CO}_2e$$

- **Comparative comment (1 mark):** Although only 500 kg of $N_2O$ is emitted (half the mass of $CH_4$), its $CO_2e$ contribution is **~4.9√ó greater** than that of methane due to its much higher GWP and longer atmospheric lifetime (~109 yr vs. 11.8 yr).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Choice 2 (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023 Model]**
*(a)* Discuss the **chemistry of stratospheric ozone depletion** by CFCs. Include the Chapman cycle and the catalytic chlorine destruction cycle with full equations. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Define **sustainable development** as per the Brundtland Commission. List and briefly describe **any six** of the 17 UN Sustainable Development Goals. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Chemistry of Stratospheric Ozone Depletion (7 marks):**

- **Importance of stratospheric ozone (1 mark):** The stratospheric $O_3$ layer (15‚Äď35 km) absorbs harmful UV-B and UV-C radiation ($\lambda < 320$ nm) protecting life on Earth. Measured in Dobson Units (1 DU = $2.687 \times 10^{16}$ molec¬∑cm‚ĀĽ¬≤).

- **Chapman cycle (2 marks):** Natural formation and destruction of ozone.

$$O_2 + h\nu \rightarrow 2O^¬∑ \quad (\lambda < 240 \text{ nm})$$

$$O^¬∑ + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M$$

$$O_3 + h\nu \rightarrow O_2 + O^¬∑ \quad (\lambda < 320 \text{ nm})$$

$$O_3 + O^¬∑ \rightarrow 2O_2$$

Chapman predicts more $O_3$ than observed ‚Üí catalytic cycles are important.

- **Catalytic chlorine cycle (3 marks):** CFCs (e.g. $CFCl_3$, $CF_2Cl_2$) are photolysed in the stratosphere:

$$CFCl_3 + h\nu \rightarrow CFCl_2^¬∑ + Cl^¬∑$$

$$Cl^¬∑ + O_3 \rightarrow ClO^¬∑ + O_2$$

$$ClO^¬∑ + O^¬∑ \rightarrow Cl^¬∑ + O_2 \quad \text{(Cl regenerated ‚ÄĒ catalyst)}$$

$$\boxed{Net: \quad 2O_3 \rightarrow 3O_2}$$

A single Cl atom destroys ~10‚ĀĶ $O_3$ molecules before being sequestered in reservoir species (HCl, ClONO‚āā).

- **Polar amplification (1 mark):** Polar Stratospheric Clouds (PSCs) catalyse heterogeneous reactions releasing $Cl_2$ which is photolysed to give free Cl, producing the Antarctic ozone hole (minimum ~73 DU in 2000).

**(b) Sustainable Development and SDGs (7 marks):**

- **Brundtland Definition (2 marks):** *"Sustainable development is the development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs."* ‚ÄĒ WCED 1987. Built on two pillars: (i) essential needs, especially of the poor; (ii) environmental limitations imposed by technology and social organisation.

- **Six SDGs (¬Ĺ mark each description + ¬Ĺ mark each listing = 1 mark each, total 6 marks):**

  1. **SDG 7 ‚ÄĒ Affordable and Clean Energy:** Ensure universal access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy.
  2. **SDG 12 ‚ÄĒ Responsible Consumption and Production:** Promote resource and energy efficiency, sustainable infrastructure, and zero-waste lifestyles.
  3. **SDG 13 ‚ÄĒ Climate Action:** Take urgent action to combat climate change and its impacts (Paris Agreement alignment).
  4. **SDG 3 ‚ÄĒ Good Health and Well-being:** Ensure healthy lives and promote well-being for all at all ages.
  5. **SDG 6 ‚ÄĒ Clean Water and Sanitation:** Ensure availability and sustainable management of water and sanitation for all.
  6. **SDG 11 ‚ÄĒ Sustainable Cities and Communities:** Make cities inclusive, safe, resilient, and sustainable.

- **Conclusion (‚Äď1 mark deduction if missing, but awarded 0 here):** The 17 SDGs, adopted by UN General Assembly in 2015 under the 2030 Agenda, integrate environmental, social, and economic sustainability with 169 targets and 231 indicators.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ Memorize Before the Exam**

**A. Greenhouse Gases & Climate Change**
- ‚úÖ **Brundtland definition** ‚ÄĒ needs of present, future generations
- ‚úÖ **Greenhouse gas = IR-active polyatomic molecule** with changing dipole moment
- ‚úÖ **$CO_2$ IR bands:** 4.26 ¬Ķm (asymmetric stretch) and 15 ¬Ķm (bend)
- ‚úÖ **$CH_4$ IR bands:** 3.3 ¬Ķm and 7.66 ¬Ķm
- ‚úÖ **Wien's law:** $\lambda_{max} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}/T$ ‚Üí Earth peak ~10 ¬Ķm
- ‚úÖ **GWP‚āĀ‚āÄ‚āÄ values (AR6):** $CO_2$ = 1, $CH_4$ = 27.9, $N_2O$ = 273, CFC-12 = 12,500
- ‚úÖ **CO‚āā level (2023):** ~421 ppm
- ‚úÖ **Radiative forcing formula:** $\Delta F = 5.35 \ln(C/C_0)$ W¬∑m‚ĀĽ¬≤
- ‚úÖ **Atmospheric lifetimes:** $CH_4$ ~11.8 yr, $N_2O$ ~109 yr, $SF_6$ ~3,200 yr

**B. Ozone Depletion**
- ‚úÖ **Ozone layer altitude:** 15‚Äď35 km (stratosphere)
- ‚úÖ **Chapman cycle:** photolysis of $O_2$ + 3-body formation
- ‚úÖ **Catalytic Cl cycle:** $Cl^¬∑ + O_3 \rightarrow ClO^¬∑ + O_2$ then $ClO^¬∑ + O^¬∑ \rightarrow Cl^¬∑ + O_2$
- ‚úÖ **Net reaction:** $2O_3 \rightarrow 3O_2$
- ‚úÖ **Reservoir species:** HCl, ClONO‚āā
- ‚úÖ **Heterogeneous chemistry on PSCs** causes Antarctic ozone hole
- ‚úÖ **1 Dobson Unit** = $2.687 \times 10^{16}$ molec¬∑cm‚ĀĽ¬≤
- ‚úÖ **Montreal Protocol (1987)** + Kigali Amendment (2016) ‚ÄĒ successful treaty

**C. Sustainable Development**
- ‚úÖ **Brundtland 1987** ‚ÄĒ canonical definition
- ‚úÖ **Three pillars:** Environment, Economy, Society
- ‚úÖ **UN 2030 Agenda** adopted September 2015
- ‚úÖ **17 SDGs, 169 targets, 231 indicators**
- ‚úÖ Succeeded **8 MDGs** (2000‚Äď2015)
- ‚úÖ **Key SDGs for engineering:** 7 (energy), 9 (industry), 12 (consumption), 13 (climate)

**D. Equations You Must Know Cold**
- ‚úÖ Wien: $\lambda_{max} = b/T$
- ‚úÖ Photon energy: $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$
- ‚úÖ GWP integral definition
- ‚úÖ $CO_2e = m \times GWP$
- ‚úÖ Arrhenius: $k = A\exp(-E_a/RT)$
- ‚úÖ Radioactive decay analog: $C(t) = C_0 e^{-t/\tau}$

**E. Common Numerical Conversions to Practice**
- ‚úÖ ppm ‚Üí DU conversions
- ‚úÖ Wavelength ‚ÜĒ wavenumber: $\tilde{\nu} = 1/\lambda$ (cm‚ĀĽ¬Ļ)
- ‚úÖ ppm CO‚āā ‚ÜĒ radiative forcing
- ‚úÖ Mass of gas ‚Üí $CO_2e$ for any 3-gas mixture

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE AND ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Self-Study Topics (Not to be Included for End Semester Examination): Construction, Working and Applications of Lead Acid Battery, Nickel Cadmium Battery and Nickel Metal Hydride Battery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Self-Study Notice:** As per the official KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this topic is designated as a **Self-Study Topic** and is **NOT included for the End Semester Examination (ESE)**. However, mastery of this content is essential for viva-voce, internal assessments, project work, and GATE/competitive examinations. Students are expected to study it independently.

## 1.1 Foundational Definition of a Battery

A **battery** is an electrochemical energy storage device consisting of one or more **galvanic (voltaic) cells** that convert stored chemical energy into electrical energy via spontaneous **redox (oxidation-reduction) reactions**. Each cell comprises two electrodes of dissimilar electrochemical potential immersed in a common or separator-divided electrolyte.

**Key Terminology (KTU Standard Definitions):**

| Term | Formal Definition | Role in Battery |
|------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Anode** | Electrode where **oxidation** occurs (loss of electrons) | **Negative terminal** during discharge |
| **Cathode** | Electrode where **reduction** occurs (gain of electrons) | **Positive terminal** during discharge |
| **Electrolyte** | Ionic conductor (aqueous or gel) enabling ion transport | Completes the **internal circuit** |
| **Separator** | Porous insulating membrane between electrodes | Prevents short-circuit; allows ionic flow |
| **EMF (Electromotive Force)** | Potential difference between terminals at zero current | Measured in **Volts (V)**, typically **~2.05 V** for Lead-Acid, **~1.2 V** for Ni-Cd and Ni-MH |

> [!NOTE]
> **Faraday's Constant (F) = 96,485 C mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ ‚Čą 96,500 C mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ** is the foundational constant for all stoichiometric and energy calculations in electrochemistry.

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy: "The Electron Reservoir"

Imagine a **hydroelectric dam** system:
- The **water reservoir at height** = chemical energy stored in the active electrode materials (high Gibbs free energy, $\Delta G < 0$).
- The **turbine** = the external circuit (current flows as electrons move from anode to cathode).
- The **falling water** = electrons cascading from a higher potential (anode) to a lower potential (cathode).
- The **dry reservoir** = a fully discharged battery (all active material converted to products).
- **Recharging the reservoir** = applying external voltage to drive the **non-spontaneous reverse reaction**.

When the dam gate is open (circuit closed), water (electrons) flows, doing useful work. The chemical energy of the system continuously decreases until the redox reaction reaches equilibrium ‚ÄĒ at which point the battery is **fully discharged**.

## 1.3 The Three Batteries at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> All three are **secondary (rechargeable) batteries**, meaning the discharge reaction is reversible by applying an external DC source.

### **A. Lead-Acid Battery (LAB)**
- **Invented:** 1859 by **Gaston Plant√©** ‚ÄĒ the oldest rechargeable battery.
- **Chemistry:** Lead (Pb) and Lead Dioxide (PbO‚āā) electrodes in sulfuric acid (H‚āāSO‚āĄ).
- **Cell Voltage:** **2.05 V per cell** (6 cells = **12.6 V** in a standard car battery).
- **Analogy:** The "workhorse" ‚ÄĒ heavy, cheap, reliable, like a sturdy diesel truck.

### **B. Nickel-Cadmium (Ni-Cd) Battery**
- **Chemistry:** Cadmium (Cd) and Nickel Oxyhydroxide (NiOOH) in alkaline KOH.
- **Cell Voltage:** **1.2 V per cell** (1.25‚Äď1.3 V nominal).
- **Analogy:** The "rugged veteran" ‚ÄĒ robust, can handle abuse, but suffers from the **memory effect**.

### **C. Nickel-Metal Hydride (Ni-MH) Battery**
- **Chemistry:** Metal Hydride (MH) and Nickel Oxyhydroxide (NiOOH) in alkaline KOH.
- **Cell Voltage:** **1.2 V per cell** ‚ÄĒ *drop-in replacement* for Ni-Cd in most applications.
- **Analogy:** The "environment-friendly upgrade" ‚ÄĒ higher capacity, no toxic Cd, used in early Toyota Prius hybrids.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 LEAD-ACID BATTERY (LAB)

### 2.1.1 Construction

| Component | Material | Function |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| **Anode (‚Äď)** | **Spongy Lead (Pb)** | Oxidized during discharge: Pb ‚Üí Pb¬≤‚Āļ |
| **Cathode (+)** | **Lead Dioxide (PbO‚āā)** deposited on a lead grid | Reduced during discharge: Pb‚Āī‚Āļ ‚Üí Pb¬≤‚Āļ |
| **Electrolyte** | **Dilute H‚āāSO‚āĄ** (~37% by weight, density **1.28 g cm‚ĀĽ¬≥**) | Provides SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ and H‚Āļ ions; ionic conduction |
| **Separators** | Porous rubber, PVC, or polyethylene | Prevents direct contact between plates |
| **Container** | Hard rubber, polypropylene, or ebonite | Acid-resistant, leak-proof housing |
| **Plates** | Alternating Pb and PbO‚āā grids (typically 6 cells √ó multiple plates) | Maximizes surface area for current density |
| **Vent Caps** | One per cell | Releases H‚āā and O‚āā gases during overcharging |

### 2.1.2 Working ‚ÄĒ Discharge (Spontaneous)

**At the Anode (Oxidation):**

$$\text{Pb}_{(s)} + \text{SO}_4^{2-}_{(aq)} \longrightarrow \text{PbSO}_4{}_{(s)} + 2e^-$$

**At the Cathode (Reduction):**

$$\text{PbO}_2{}_{(s)} + 4\text{H}^+_{(aq)} + \text{SO}_4^{2-}_{(aq)} + 2e^- \longrightarrow \text{PbSO}_4{}_{(s)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$$

**Overall Discharge Reaction:**

$$\text{Pb}_{(s)} + \text{PbO}_2{}_{(s)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4{}_{(aq)} \longrightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4{}_{(s)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The State of Charge (SoC) Indicator:** The **specific gravity (density) of H‚āāSO‚āĄ** decreases during discharge (as H‚āāO is produced and SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ is consumed). A fully charged cell has specific gravity **1.28 g cm‚ĀĽ¬≥**; a fully discharged cell drops to **1.10 g cm‚ĀĽ¬≥**. This is why a **hydrometer** is used to check battery health.

### 2.1.3 Working ‚ÄĒ Charging (Non-Spontaneous, Reverse Reaction)

Applying external DC source forces the reverse reaction:

$$2\text{PbSO}_4{}_{(s)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \xrightarrow{\text{DC}} \text{Pb}_{(s)} + \text{PbO}_2{}_{(s)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4{}_{(aq)}$$

### 2.1.4 Applications of Lead-Acid Battery
- **Automotive SLI:** Starting, Lighting, Ignition (12 V battery in cars, two-wheelers)
- **UPS & Inverter backup** for homes and offices
- **Telecommunications** tower backup power
- **Forklifts, golf carts, electric vehicles** (deep-cycle variants)
- **Submarine propulsion** (large stationary LABs)

---

## 2.2 NICKEL-CADMIUM (Ni-Cd) BATTERY

### 2.2.1 Construction

| Component | Material | Function |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| **Anode (‚Äď)** | **Cadmium (Cd)** powder | Oxidized: Cd ‚Üí Cd¬≤‚Āļ |
| **Cathode (+)** | **Nickel Oxyhydroxide (NiOOH)** | Reduced: Ni¬≥‚Āļ ‚Üí Ni¬≤‚Āļ |
| **Electrolyte** | **Potassium Hydroxide (KOH)** (~20‚Äď30% aqueous) | Alkaline ionic conduction; OH‚ĀĽ carrier |
| **Separators** | Nylon or polyolefin | Alkali-resistant insulation |
| **Container** | Sealed steel can (for sealed cells) or vented plastic | Pressure-resistant |
| **Vent** | Pressure-relief vent | Releases H‚āā during overcharge (sealed cells recombine) |

### 2.2.2 Working ‚ÄĒ Discharge

**At the Anode (Oxidation):**

$$\text{Cd}_{(s)} + 2\text{OH}^-_{(aq)} \longrightarrow \text{Cd(OH)}_2{}_{(s)} + 2e^-$$

**At the Cathode (Reduction):**

$$\text{NiOOH}_{(s)} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} + e^- \longrightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2{}_{(s)} + \text{OH}^-_{(aq)}$$

**Overall Discharge Reaction:**

$$\text{Cd}_{(s)} + 2\text{NiOOH}_{(s)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \longrightarrow \text{Cd(OH)}_2{}_{(s)} + 2\text{Ni(OH)}_2{}_{(s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **alkaline electrolyte (KOH) does NOT participate in the overall reaction** ‚ÄĒ it only provides OH‚ĀĽ ions. This is a defining feature of alkaline batteries: the electrolyte concentration remains essentially constant, making the cell voltage remarkably stable.

### 2.2.3 Working ‚ÄĒ Charging (Reverse Reaction)

$$\text{Cd(OH)}_2{}_{(s)} + 2\text{Ni(OH)}_2{}_{(s)} \xrightarrow{\text{DC}} \text{Cd}_{(s)} + 2\text{NiOOH}_{(s)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$$

### 2.2.4 Applications of Ni-Cd Battery
- **Portable power tools** (cordless drills, saws)
- **Emergency lighting** and **aircraft starting batteries**
- **Two-way radios**, medical equipment, professional video cameras
- **Railway signaling and switching**
- **Memory backup** in telecom systems

> [!WARNING]
> **Memory Effect:** If Ni-Cd is repeatedly recharged after only partial discharge, it "remembers" the shallower depth and loses usable capacity. Periodic **full discharge cycles** are required to restore capacity. This drawback paved the way for Ni-MH and Li-ion adoption.

---

## 2.3 NICKEL-METAL HYDRIDE (Ni-MH) BATTERY

### 2.3.1 Construction

| Component | Material | Function |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| **Anode (‚Äď)** | **Metal Hydride (MH)**, typically **AB‚āÖ alloy** (e.g., LaNi‚āÖ, or Misch-metal based Ni alloys) | Stores H atoms; oxidized to release H‚Āļ/H‚āāO |
| **Cathode (+)** | **Nickel Oxyhydroxide (NiOOH)** | Same as Ni-Cd cathode |
| **Electrolyte** | **KOH** (~30%) | Alkaline ionic conduction |
| **Separator** | Polypropylene non-woven fabric | Prevents dendritic short circuits |
| **Pressure Vent** | Resealable safety vent | Activates at ~3‚Äď5 atm overpressure |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "M" in MH** is a **hydrogen-absorbing intermetallic alloy** (commonly LaNi‚āÖ or MmNi‚āÖ where Mm = Misch metal). The metal lattice reversibly absorbs and desorbs hydrogen ‚ÄĒ a phenomenon known as **hydrogen storage in metal hydrides**. The anodic reaction is: $\text{MH} + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^-$.

### 2.3.2 Working ‚ÄĒ Discharge

**At the Anode (Oxidation):**

$$\text{MH}_{(s)} + \text{OH}^-_{(aq)} \longrightarrow \text{M}_{(s)} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} + e^-$$

**At the Cathode (Reduction):**

$$\text{NiOOH}_{(s)} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} + e^- \longrightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2{}_{(s)} + \text{OH}^-_{(aq)}$$

**Overall Discharge Reaction:**

$$\text{MH}_{(s)} + \text{NiOOH}_{(s)} \longrightarrow \text{M}_{(s)} + \text{Ni(OH)}_2{}_{(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net Electrolyte Change = Zero.** The OH‚ĀĽ consumed at the anode is regenerated at the cathode. The electrolyte concentration is invariant. This is why Ni-MH is "sealed-for-life" ‚ÄĒ no maintenance needed.

### 2.3.3 Working ‚ÄĒ Charging (Reverse Reaction)

$$\text{M}_{(s)} + \text{Ni(OH)}_2{}_{(s)} \xrightarrow{\text{DC}} \text{MH}_{(s)} + \text{NiOOH}_{(s)}$$

### 2.3.4 Applications of Ni-MH Battery
- **Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs)** ‚ÄĒ Toyota Prius (1st gen), Honda Insight
- **Consumer electronics** ‚ÄĒ early digital cameras, PDAs, cordless phones
- **Power tools** (high-drain applications)
- **Emergency backup** in satellites and spacecraft (with adaptations)
- **Rechargeable AA/AAA cells** (1.2 V) ‚ÄĒ direct replacement for alkaline primaries

---

## 2.4 ‚ö° KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (CHEAT TABLE)

| # | Formula / Relation | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Standard Cell EMF** | $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$ | Volts (V); both $E^{\circ}$ are **reduction potentials** |
| 2 | **Nernst Equation (at 25 ¬įC)** | $E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10} Q$ | $Q$ = reaction quotient; $n$ = electrons transferred |
| 3 | **General Nernst Equation** | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | $R = 8.314$ J mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ; $F = 96485$ C mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ |
| 4 | **Gibbs Free Energy & EMF** | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ | J mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ; negative $\Delta G$ = spontaneous |
| 5 | **Energy Stored** | $E_{\text{stored}} = V \times Q$ | Wh; $V$ in volts, $Q$ in Ah |
| 6 | **Energy Density (Gravimetric)** | $\rho_E = \dfrac{E_{\text{stored}}}{m_{\text{battery}}}$ | Wh kg‚ĀĽ¬Ļ |
| 7 | **Faraday's Law of Electrolysis** | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | $m$ = mass deposited (g), $M$ = molar mass, $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s) |
| 8 | **Standard Potentials Used** | $E^{\circ}_{\text{PbSO}_4/\text{Pb}} = -0.356$ V; $E^{\circ}_{\text{PbO}_2/\text{PbSO}_4} = +1.685$ V | LAB cell EMF $= 2.041 \approx 2.05$ V |
| 9 | **Standard Potentials Used** | $E^{\circ}_{\text{NiOOH}/\text{Ni(OH)}_2} = +0.490$ V; $E^{\circ}_{\text{Cd(OH)}_2/\text{Cd}} = -0.809$ V | Ni-Cd cell EMF $= 1.299 \approx 1.3$ V |
| 10 | **Charge Capacity** | $Q = n \cdot F \cdot N$ where $N$ = moles of active material | Coulombs; converted to Ah by dividing by 3600 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in **battery management systems (BMS)**, **electric vehicle range estimation**, **solar PV storage sizing**, and **SoC/SoH (State-of-Charge/Health) algorithms** in modern embedded systems.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below use **standard reduction potential tables** (at 25 ¬įC, 1 M concentration, 1 atm pressure) to compute cell EMF. This is the standard KTU approach.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: EMF of the Lead-Acid Battery

### **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify the half-cell reduction potentials from standard tables.**

From the standard electrochemical series:

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{PbSO}_4 / \text{Pb}} = -0.356 \text{ V} \quad \text{(lead electrode, becomes anode)}$$

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{PbO}_2 / \text{PbSO}_4} = +1.685 \text{ V} \quad \text{(lead dioxide electrode, becomes cathode)}$$

### **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Write the reduction half-reactions.**

At the **cathode** (PbO‚āā side, reduction):

$$\text{PbO}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2e^- \longrightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}; \quad E^{\circ} = +1.685 \text{ V}$$

At the **anode** (Pb side, oxidation ‚ÄĒ written as reduction then reversed):

$$\text{PbSO}_4 + 2e^- \longrightarrow \text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^{2-}; \quad E^{\circ} = -0.356 \text{ V}$$

### **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Apply the cell EMF formula.**

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$$

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = (+1.685 \text{ V}) - (-0.356 \text{ V})$$

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = 1.685 + 0.356 = 2.041 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E^{\circ}_{\text{cell, LAB}} \approx 2.05 \text{ V per cell}}$$

### **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Construct the overall balanced reaction.**

Adding the two half-reactions (electrons cancel: $2e^-$ on each side):

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{PbO}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2e^- \longrightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \\
+ \quad &\text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \longrightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2e^- \\
\hline
&\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 2\text{SO}_4^{2-} \longrightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}$$

Recognizing that $4\text{H}^+ + 2\text{SO}_4^{2-} \equiv 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$:

$$\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

This matches the experimental observation: **sulfuric acid is consumed** during discharge.

### **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Compute Gibbs Free Energy of the cell reaction.**

For $n = 2$ electrons transferred:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = -(2)(96485)(2.041)$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -393{,}827 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \approx -393.8 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

The large negative value confirms the **strong spontaneity** of the discharge reaction.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: EMF of the Nickel-Cadmium Battery

### **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Standard reduction potentials.**

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{NiOOH}/\text{Ni(OH)}_2} = +0.490 \text{ V} \quad \text{(cathode)}$$

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{Cd(OH)}_2/\text{Cd}} = -0.809 \text{ V} \quad \text{(anode)}$$

### **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Half-reactions.**

Cathode (reduction):
$$\text{NiOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \longrightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^-; \quad E^{\circ} = +0.490 \text{ V}$$

Anode (oxidation, written as reduction then reversed):
$$\text{Cd(OH)}_2 + 2e^- \longrightarrow \text{Cd} + 2\text{OH}^-; \quad E^{\circ} = -0.809 \text{ V}$$

### **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Balance electrons by multiplying cathode reaction by 2.**

To balance $2e^-$ in the anode with $1e^-$ in the cathode, multiply the cathode half-reaction by **2**:

$$\begin{aligned}
2\text{NiOOH} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- &\longrightarrow 2\text{Ni(OH)}_2 + 2\text{OH}^- \\
\text{Cd} + 2\text{OH}^- &\longrightarrow \text{Cd(OH)}_2 + 2e^- \\
\hline
2\text{NiOOH} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Cd} &\longrightarrow 2\text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{Cd(OH)}_2
\end{aligned}$$

### **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Apply the cell EMF formula (independent of stoichiometric coefficients).**

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}} = (+0.490) - (-0.809)$$

$$\boxed{E^{\circ}_{\text{cell, Ni-Cd}} = 1.299 \text{ V} \approx 1.3 \text{ V per cell}}$$

The actual working voltage is **~1.2 V** due to internal resistance, overpotentials, and concentration polarization.

### **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Gibbs free energy.**

For $n = 2$:
$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -(2)(96485)(1.299) = -250{,}700 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \approx -250.7 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: EMF of the Nickel-Metal Hydride Battery

### **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Standard reduction potentials.**

The cathode is identical to Ni-Cd: $E^{\circ}_{\text{NiOOH}/\text{Ni(OH)}_2} = +0.490$ V.

For the anode, the half-reaction is:
$$\text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \longrightarrow \text{MH} + \text{OH}^-; \quad E^{\circ} \approx -0.828 \text{ V}$$

(This is the potential for the reversible hydrogen absorption/desorption couple, equivalent to the $\text{H}_2\text{O}/\text{H}_2$ couple in alkaline media.)

### **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute cell EMF.**

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = (+0.490) - (-0.828) = 1.318 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E^{\circ}_{\text{cell, Ni-MH}} \approx 1.3 \text{ V per cell (nominal 1.2 V)}}$$

### **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Overall reaction.**

$$\text{MH} + \text{NiOOH} \longrightarrow \text{M} + \text{Ni(OH)}_2$$

The electrolyte (KOH) is **spectator** ‚ÄĒ the net $\text{OH}^-$ on each side is balanced:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Anode: } &\text{MH} + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \\
\text{Cathode: } &\text{NiOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \rightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^- \\
\hline
\text{Net: } &\text{MH} + \text{NiOOH} \rightarrow \text{M} + \text{Ni(OH)}_2
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Lead-Acid (LAB) | Ni-Cd | Ni-MH |
|-----------|-----------------|-------|-------|
| **Anode** | Pb | Cd | MH (e.g., LaNi‚āÖH‚āÜ) |
| **Cathode** | PbO‚āā | NiOOH | NiOOH |
| **Electrolyte** | H‚āāSO‚āĄ (acidic) | KOH (alkaline) | KOH (alkaline) |
| **Nominal Voltage** | **2.05 V** | **1.2 V** | **1.2 V** |
| **Theoretical $E^{\circ}$** | 2.041 V | 1.299 V | 1.318 V |
| **Energy Density (Wh kg‚ĀĽ¬Ļ)** | 30‚Äď50 | 40‚Äď60 | 60‚Äď120 |
| **Cycle Life** | 200‚Äď300 (SLI); 1000+ (deep cycle) | 1000‚Äď2000 | 500‚Äď1000 |
| **Self-Discharge / month** | ~5% | ~10‚Äď20% | ~15‚Äď30% |
| **Memory Effect** | No | **Yes (severe)** | Mild |
| **Toxicity** | Pb (toxic) | **Cd (highly toxic)** | Low (environment-friendly) |
| **Maintenance** | Top-up water (flooded) | Sealed | Sealed, maintenance-free |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Higher than Ni-Cd |
| **Key Application** | Car batteries, UPS | Power tools, aircraft | HEVs, consumer electronics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The trend Lead-Acid ‚Üí Ni-Cd ‚Üí Ni-MH illustrates the historical shift from **high weight/toxicity** to **higher energy density + environmental compatibility**, ultimately leading to today's dominance of **Li-ion** in portable electronics and EVs.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Self-Study Status Reminder:** As per the official KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this entire topic ‚ÄĒ "Construction, Working and Applications of Lead Acid Battery, Nickel Cadmium Battery and Nickel Metal Hydride Battery" ‚ÄĒ is **explicitly NOT included for the End Semester Examination (ESE)**. The questions below are provided purely for **self-assessment, internal tests, viva-voce preparation, and competitive exam readiness** (GATE, IES, etc.). They are modeled on the KTU question paper pattern (Part A: 3 marks; Part B: 14 marks).

---

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Self-Study Module]`
**Explain why the specific gravity of the electrolyte in a lead-acid battery decreases during discharge.**

### **Model Answer (3 marks):**

During discharge, the overall cell reaction is:

$$\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The reaction **consumes 2 moles of H‚āāSO‚āĄ** and **produces 2 moles of H‚āāO**. As discharge proceeds:
- The **concentration of H‚āāSO‚āĄ decreases** (consumed as SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ ions are absorbed into the PbSO‚āĄ solid product).
- The **concentration of water increases** (produced at the cathode).
- Therefore, the **density of the electrolyte falls** from **1.28 g cm‚ĀĽ¬≥ (fully charged)** to **~1.10 g cm‚ĀĽ¬≥ (fully discharged)**.

This property is exploited by mechanics using a **hydrometer** to assess the state of charge of the battery. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Self-Study Module]`
**Why is the electrolyte (KOH) considered a spectator in Ni-Cd and Ni-MH batteries?**

### **Model Answer (3 marks):**

In both Ni-Cd and Ni-MH batteries, the **overall cell reaction** does not show any net consumption or production of KOH:

For Ni-Cd: $2\text{NiOOH} + \text{Cd} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{Cd(OH)}_2$

For Ni-MH: $\text{NiOOH} + \text{MH} \rightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{M}$

The **OH‚ĀĽ ions consumed at the anode are exactly regenerated at the cathode**:
- At the anode: $\text{OH}^-$ is consumed.
- At the cathode: $\text{OH}^-$ is produced.

The **net change in KOH concentration is zero**, so KOH acts only as an **ionic conductor** (facilitating charge transport) without being chemically consumed. This is why these batteries are called "**alkaline**" batteries with **maintenance-free sealed construction**. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE pattern: Each Part B question is **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. For this self-study topic, both choices are provided for parallel self-testing.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Self-Study Module]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **construction of a Lead-Acid battery**. Discuss the **materials used for anode, cathode, electrolyte, and separators**, and explain their roles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the **overall reaction during discharge** of a Lead-Acid battery. Derive the **standard cell EMF** using standard reduction potential data. How is the **state of charge** related to the specific gravity of the electrolyte? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Construction (7 marks):**

A lead-acid cell consists of the following components:

1. **Anode (Negative Plate):** Made of **spongy/porous lead (Pb)** supported on a lead-antimony alloy grid. The grid provides mechanical strength and acts as a current collector. The spongy texture provides a **high surface area**, enabling high current density. **(1.5 marks)**

2. **Cathode (Positive Plate):** Made of **lead dioxide (PbO‚āā)** ‚ÄĒ a dark brown, hard, brittle material ‚ÄĒ also coated on a lead-antimony grid. The PbO‚āā acts as the **active material** for reduction. **(1.5 marks)**

3. **Electrolyte:** **Dilute sulfuric acid (H‚āāSO‚āĄ)** with concentration ~**37% by weight** and specific gravity **~1.28 g cm‚ĀĽ¬≥** at full charge. It supplies **H‚Āļ and SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ ions** for the cell reaction. **(1 mark)**

4. **Separators:** Made of **porous rubber, PVC, or polyethylene sheets** placed between adjacent plates. They prevent physical contact and short-circuiting while allowing **ionic conduction** of H‚Āļ and SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ. **(1 mark)**

5. **Container (Cell Case):** Made of **hard rubber, ebonite, or polypropylene** ‚ÄĒ acid-resistant and mechanically robust. Includes **vent caps** for releasing gases (H‚āā, O‚āā) formed during overcharge. **(1 mark)**

6. **Terminal Posts & Cell Connectors:** External lead terminals for connecting cells in **series** (6 cells = 12 V battery) and to the external load. **(1 mark)**

---

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Working & EMF Derivation (7 marks):**

**Overall discharge reaction:**

$$\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**EMF Derivation using Standard Reduction Potentials:**

The standard reduction potentials at 25 ¬įC:

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{PbSO}_4/\text{Pb}} = -0.356 \text{ V} \quad \text{(used as anode)}$$

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{PbO}_2/\text{PbSO}_4} = +1.685 \text{ V} \quad \text{(used as cathode)}$$

**Formula:**

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Substitution:**

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = (+1.685 \text{ V}) - (-0.356 \text{ V}) = 2.041 \text{ V} \approx 2.05 \text{ V} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**For a 6-cell lead-acid battery:**

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{battery}} = 6 \times 2.05 \text{ V} = 12.3 \text{ V (nominal 12 V)} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**State of Charge vs. Specific Gravity:**

The H‚āāSO‚āĄ is **consumed** and H‚āāO is **produced** during discharge. As discharge progresses:
- H‚āāSO‚āĄ concentration falls ‚Üí specific gravity drops.
- **Fully charged:** 1.28 g cm‚ĀĽ¬≥
- **Fully discharged:** 1.10 g cm‚ĀĽ¬≥

$$\text{SoC} \propto \text{Specific Gravity} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

A **hydrometer** measures the specific gravity of the electrolyte and indicates the SoC. **(1 mark)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Self-Study Module]`

**(a)** Describe the **construction and working of a Nickel-Cadmium (Ni-Cd) battery**. Write the half-reactions at both electrodes and the overall cell reaction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **construction, performance, and environmental impact** of Ni-Cd and Ni-MH batteries. Why is Ni-MH preferred for hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs)? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Ni-Cd Construction & Working (7 marks):**

**Construction:** A Ni-Cd cell consists of: **(2 marks)**

- **Anode (‚Äď):** **Cadmium (Cd)** in powdered form, mixed with a binder and pasted onto a nickel-plated steel substrate.
- **Cathode (+):** **Nickel Oxyhydroxide (NiOOH)** ‚ÄĒ the oxidized form of nickel hydroxide, also pasted on a nickel substrate.
- **Electrolyte:** **Aqueous KOH (~20‚Äď30% w/w)** ‚ÄĒ an alkaline electrolyte that conducts OH‚ĀĽ ions.
- **Separator:** **Nylon or polyolefin** fabric between electrodes.
- **Container:** Sealed steel can with a **pressure relief vent** for overcharge gas release.
- **Current collectors:** Nickel-plated steel strips.

**Working ‚ÄĒ Discharge (Redox Reactions):** **(3 marks)**

At the **anode (oxidation)**:
$$\text{Cd} + 2\text{OH}^- \longrightarrow \text{Cd(OH)}_2 + 2e^-$$

At the **cathode (reduction)**:
$$\text{NiOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \longrightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^-$$

(√ó2 to balance electrons: $2\text{NiOOH} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \rightarrow 2\text{Ni(OH)}_2 + 2\text{OH}^-$)

**Overall:**
$$\boxed{\text{Cd} + 2\text{NiOOH} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Cd(OH)}_2 + 2\text{Ni(OH)}_2}$$

**EMF:** $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{NiOOH}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{Cd(OH)}_2} = (+0.490) - (-0.809) = 1.299 \text{ V} \approx 1.3 \text{ V}$ (Nominal 1.2 V) **(1 mark)**

**Charging** is the reverse of the discharge reaction, driven by an external DC source. **(1 mark)**

---

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Ni-Cd vs. Ni-MH Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Ni-Cd | Ni-MH |
|--------|-------|-------|
| **Anode** | Cadmium metal (toxic) | Metal hydride (AB‚āÖ alloy, e.g., LaNi‚āÖ) ‚ÄĒ eco-friendly |
| **Cathode** | NiOOH | NiOOH (same as Ni-Cd) |
| **Electrolyte** | KOH | KOH (same) |
| **Cell Voltage** | 1.2 V | 1.2 V (drop-in replacement) |
| **Energy Density** | 40‚Äď60 Wh kg‚ĀĽ¬Ļ | 60‚Äď120 Wh kg‚ĀĽ¬Ļ (**~2√ó higher**) |
| **Memory Effect** | Severe | Mild (almost none) |
| **Toxicity** | Cadmium ‚ÄĒ **highly toxic**, environmental hazard | Low toxicity, RoHS compliant |
| **Cycle Life** | 1000‚Äď2000 cycles | 500‚Äď1000 cycles (slightly lower) |
| **Self-Discharge** | Moderate (~10‚Äď20%/month) | Higher (~15‚Äď30%/month) |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher (hydride alloy is expensive) |

**Why Ni-MH is preferred for HEVs (Toyota Prius, Honda Insight):** **(3 marks)**

1. **Higher energy density** ‚ÄĒ critical for vehicle range and packaging.
2. **Environment-friendly** ‚ÄĒ no toxic Cd (legislation under **EU Battery Directive 2006/66/EC** banned Ni-Cd in most consumer applications).
3. **No memory effect** ‚ÄĒ HEVs undergo shallow, irregular charge-discharge cycles; Ni-MH tolerates this gracefully.
4. **High power density** ‚ÄĒ Ni-MH can deliver the high current bursts required for vehicle acceleration.
5. **Long calendar life** under float-charge conditions typical of HEV regenerative braking systems.

The drawbacks of Ni-MH (higher self-discharge, lower cycle life than Ni-Cd) are outweighed by its environmental and performance advantages in automotive applications.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Valuation Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention for standard reduction potentials.** The **E¬į(anode) is subtracted**, NOT added. Wrong: $E^{\circ} = E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}} + E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}}$ (this is a **‚Äď2 to ‚Äď4 mark** deduction in ESE).
>
> 2. **Confusing anode/cathode identification during discharge vs. charge.** In a galvanic cell during **discharge**: anode = **negative**, cathode = **positive**. During **charging** (electrolysis mode): the signs **reverse**.
>
> 3. **Not balancing the electrons** in half-reactions before adding. A frequent error: writing $2\text{NiOOH} + \text{Cd} \rightarrow \ldots$ without multiplying the NiOOH half-reaction by 2 to balance $2e^-$.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the role of electrolyte in alkaline cells.** Many students incorrectly write H‚āāSO‚āĄ in Ni-Cd/Ni-MH cells, or KOH in lead-acid cells. Always match electrolyte to the chemistry.
>
> 5. **Omitting the unit of EMF (V) and the number of electrons transferred (n)** in derivations. KTU examiners explicitly look for these.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the hydrometer / specific gravity concept** in lead-acid batteries ‚ÄĒ this is a **favorite 2-mark question** in viva-voce.
>
> 7. **Incorrectly stating applications:** Writing "Ni-MH is used in modern smartphones" (wrong ‚ÄĒ Li-ion dominates) or "Lead-acid is used in satellites" (wrong ‚ÄĒ Ni-MH or Li-ion is used).

## 5.4 ūüďĆ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin This Before Exams):**

### **Lead-Acid Battery (LAB)**
- ‚ėź **Anode:** Pb (spongy); **Cathode:** PbO‚āā; **Electrolyte:** H‚āāSO‚āĄ (~37%, SG 1.28)
- ‚ėź **Cell EMF:** 2.05 V (theoretical 2.041 V); 6 cells ‚Üí 12 V battery
- ‚ėź **Discharge:** $\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- ‚ėź **H‚āāSO‚āĄ is consumed**, H‚āāO is produced ‚Üí specific gravity **decreases** during discharge
- ‚ėź **Applications:** SLI (car batteries), UPS, inverters, forklifts, submarines
- ‚ėź **Oldest rechargeable battery** (Gaston Plant√©, 1859)

### **Nickel-Cadmium (Ni-Cd) Battery**
- ‚ėź **Anode:** Cd; **Cathode:** NiOOH; **Electrolyte:** KOH (alkaline)
- ‚ėź **Cell EMF:** 1.2 V (theoretical 1.299 V)
- ‚ėź **Discharge:** $\text{Cd} + 2\text{NiOOH} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Cd(OH)}_2 + 2\text{Ni(OH)}_2$
- ‚ėź **KOH is a spectator** ‚ÄĒ concentration unchanged
- ‚ėź **Memory effect** is the main drawback
- ‚ėź **Toxic Cd** ‚ÄĒ banned in many consumer applications
- ‚ėź **Applications:** Power tools, aircraft, emergency lighting, two-way radios

### **Nickel-Metal Hydride (Ni-MH) Battery**
- ‚ėź **Anode:** Metal Hydride (MH, e.g., LaNi‚āÖH‚āÜ); **Cathode:** NiOOH; **Electrolyte:** KOH
- ‚ėź **Cell EMF:** 1.2 V (drop-in replacement for Ni-Cd)
- ‚ėź **Discharge:** $\text{MH} + \text{NiOOH} \rightarrow \text{M} + \text{Ni(OH)}_2$
- ‚ėź **KOH is a spectator** ‚ÄĒ completely maintenance-free
- ‚ėź **No significant memory effect**
- ‚ėź **2√ó energy density of Ni-Cd**
- ‚ėź **Applications:** Toyota Prius (1st-gen HEV), consumer AA/AAA cells, power tools

### **Universal Formulas (Apply to All Three)**
- ‚ėź $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$
- ‚ėź $E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$ (Nernst at 25 ¬įC)
- ‚ėź $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$
- ‚ėź $F = 96{,}485$ C mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ

### **Key Comparative Insights**
- ‚ėź **Alkaline batteries (Ni-Cd, Ni-MH)** have invariant electrolyte concentration ‚Üí stable voltage
- ‚ėź **Lead-acid** is the only **acidic** battery of the three
- ‚ėź **Ni-MH is the environmentally responsible successor to Ni-Cd**
- ‚ėź **Discharge ‚Üí oxidation at anode (‚Äď); reduction at cathode (+)** ‚ÄĒ memorize this fundamental convention
